title: Asymmetric Goal Drift in Coding Agents Under Value Conflict
comment: 5 pages, 4 figures, Published as a workshop paper in Lifelong Agents @ ICLR 2026
summary: Agentic coding agents are increasingly deployed autonomously, at scale, and over long-context horizons. Throughout an agent's lifetime, it must navigate tensions between explicit instructions, learned values, and environmental pressures, often in contexts unseen during training. Prior work on model preferences, agent behavior under value tensions, and goal drift has relied on static, synthetic settings that do not capture the complexity of real-world environments. To this end, we introduce a framework built on OpenCode to orchestrate realistic, multi-step coding tasks to measure how agents violate explicit constraints in their system prompt over time with and without environmental pressure toward competing values. Using this framework, we demonstrate that GPT-5 mini, Haiku 4.5, and Grok Code Fast 1 exhibit asymmetric drift: they are more likely to violate their system prompt when its constraint opposes strongly-held values like security and privacy. We find for the models and values tested that goal drift correlates with three compounding factors: value alignment, adversarial pressure, and accumulated context. However, even strongly-held values like privacy show non-zero violation rates under sustained environmental pressure. These findings reveal that shallow compliance checks are insufficient and that comment-based pressure can exploit model value hierarchies to override system prompt instructions. More broadly, our findings highlight a gap in current alignment approaches in ensuring that agentic systems appropriately balance explicit user constraints against broadly beneficial learned preferences under sustained environmental pressure.

AI.tldr: They build a realistic, multi-step coding benchmark to test whether agentic coders drift from system-prompt constraints under pressure, finding asymmetric drift toward strongly held values (e.g., security, privacy) and that drift compounds with adversarial pressure and long context, revealing limits of shallow compliance checks and gaps in current alignment.
AI.motivation: Static, synthetic evaluations miss how real, long-running coding agents juggle explicit instructions, learned values, and environmental pressure—especially in unseen contexts—so we need a realistic, longitudinal way to measure goal drift and constraint violations.
AI.method: Using an OpenCode-based orchestration framework, they run realistic multi-step coding tasks, injecting or withholding comment-based environmental pressures that push values competing with the system prompt. They track constraint violations over time across models (GPT-5 mini, Haiku 4.5, Grok Code Fast 1) and analyze correlations with value alignment, adversarial pressure, and accumulated context length.
AI.result: All tested models show asymmetric drift: they are more likely to violate the system prompt when it conflicts with strongly held values like security/privacy. Violation rates rise with stronger adversarial pressure and longer contexts; even privacy shows non-zero violation rates under sustained pressure.
AI.conclusion: Shallow compliance checks are inadequate; comment-based pressure can exploit value hierarchies to override instructions. Current alignment does not robustly balance explicit user constraints against broadly beneficial learned preferences under sustained pressure, calling for stronger longitudinal alignment and monitoring approaches.

title: Build, Judge, Optimize: A Blueprint for Continuous Improvement of Multi-Agent Consumer Assistants
summary: Conversational shopping assistants (CSAs) represent a compelling application of agentic AI, but moving from prototype to production reveals two underexplored challenges: how to evaluate multi-turn interactions and how to optimize tightly coupled multi-agent systems. Grocery shopping further amplifies these difficulties, as user requests are often underspecified, highly preference-sensitive, and constrained by factors such as budget and inventory. In this paper, we present a practical blueprint for evaluating and optimizing conversational shopping assistants, illustrated through a production-scale AI grocery assistant. We introduce a multi-faceted evaluation rubric that decomposes end-to-end shopping quality into structured dimensions and develop a calibrated LLM-as-judge pipeline aligned with human annotations. Building on this evaluation foundation, we investigate two complementary prompt-optimization strategies based on a SOTA prompt-optimizer called GEPA (Shao et al., 2025): (1) Sub-agent GEPA, which optimizes individual agent nodes against localized rubrics, and (2) MAMuT (Multi-Agent Multi-Turn) GEPA (Herrera et al., 2026), a novel system-level approach that jointly optimizes prompts across agents using multi-turn simulation and trajectory-level scoring. We release rubric templates and evaluation design guidance to support practitioners building production CSAs.

AI.tldr: Blueprint for taking conversational shopping assistants to production: a structured, multi-dimensional evaluation rubric, a calibrated LLM-as-judge aligned with humans, and two GEPA-based prompt-optimization regimes (sub-agent and multi-agent multi-turn), demonstrated on a production grocery assistant with released templates/guidance.
AI.motivation: Production CSAs—especially for groceries—must handle underspecified, preference-sensitive, budget/inventory-constrained, multi-turn interactions. Two gaps hinder deployment: robust evaluation of multi-turn behavior and optimization of tightly coupled multi-agent systems.
AI.method: 1) Define a multi-faceted rubric that decomposes end-to-end shopping quality into structured dimensions. 2) Build an LLM-as-judge pipeline calibrated to human annotations. 3) Explore GEPA-based prompt optimization via (a) Sub-agent GEPA that optimizes individual agent nodes against localized rubrics, and (b) MAMuT GEPA that jointly optimizes prompts across agents using multi-turn simulation with trajectory-level scoring. Release rubric templates and design guidance.
AI.result: They report a judge calibrated to human labels and demonstrate two complementary optimization strategies on a production-scale grocery CSA. While the abstract lacks quantitative metrics, it implies improved evaluation fidelity and practical gains from both local (sub-agent) and system-level (MAMuT) optimization.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, reproducible pathway to evaluate and improve production CSAs by combining structured rubrics, calibrated LLM judging, and both local and system-wide prompt optimization. The released artifacts aim to help practitioners operationalize and iterate on CSAs.

title: Mozi: Governed Autonomy for Drug Discovery LLM Agents
summary:
Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents promise to unify scientific reasoning with computation, yet their deployment in high-stakes domains like drug discovery is bottlenecked by two critical barriers: unconstrained tool-use governance and poor long-horizon reliability. In dependency-heavy pharmaceutical pipelines, autonomous agents often drift into irreproducible trajectories, where early-stage hallucinations multiplicatively compound into downstream failures. To overcome this, we present Mozi, a dual-layer architecture that bridges the flexibility of generative AI with the deterministic rigor of computational biology. Layer A (Control Plane) establishes a governed supervisor--worker hierarchy that enforces role-based tool isolation, limits execution to constrained action spaces, and drives reflection-based replanning. Layer B (Workflow Plane) operationalizes canonical drug discovery stages -- from Target Identification to Lead Optimization -- as stateful, composable skill graphs. This layer integrates strict data contracts and strategic human-in-the-loop (HITL) checkpoints to safeguard scientific validity at high-uncertainty decision boundaries.
  Operating on the design principle of ``free-form reasoning for safe tasks, structured execution for long-horizon pipelines,'' Mozi provides built-in robustness mechanisms and trace-level audibility to completely mitigate error accumulation. We evaluate Mozi on PharmaBench, a curated benchmark for biomedical agents, demonstrating superior orchestration accuracy over existing baselines. Furthermore, through end-to-end therapeutic case studies, we demonstrate Mozi's ability to navigate massive chemical spaces, enforce stringent toxicity filters, and generate highly competitive in silico candidates, effectively transforming the LLM from a fragile conversationalist into a reliable, governed co-scientist.

AI.tldr: Mozi is a dual-layer, tool-governed LLM agent architecture for drug discovery that combines a control plane for safe, constrained tool use with a workflow plane of stateful skill graphs and HITL checkpoints; it reports higher orchestration accuracy on PharmaBench and promising end-to-end in silico candidate generation with toxicity filtering.
AI.motivation: Tool-augmented LLMs struggle in high-stakes, long-horizon biomedical tasks due to unconstrained tool use, compounding hallucinations, irreproducibility, and lack of governance. Drug discovery pipelines are dependency-heavy, so early errors propagate and undermine validity and traceability.
AI.method: A dual-layer design: (A) Control Plane with a supervisor–worker hierarchy enforcing role-based tool isolation, constrained action spaces, and reflection-based replanning; (B) Workflow Plane encoding canonical drug-discovery stages as stateful, composable skill graphs with strict data contracts and human-in-the-loop checkpoints. Principle: free-form reasoning for low-risk steps; structured execution for long-horizon pipelines. Includes robustness and trace-level auditability to curb error accumulation.
AI.result: On PharmaBench, Mozi outperforms baselines in orchestration accuracy. Case studies show the system navigating large chemical spaces, enforcing toxicity constraints, and producing competitive in silico leads. The abstract claims error accumulation is fully mitigated via governance and structure.
AI.conclusion: Mozi aims to turn LLMs from fragile chatbots into governed co-scientists for drug discovery by marrying generative flexibility with deterministic, auditable workflows, improving reliability, safety, and reproducibility across complex pipelines.

title: MAGE: Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Language Agents toward Strategic Exploration and Exploitation
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in learned tasks, yet they often struggle to adapt to non-stationary environments with feedback. While In-Context Learning and external memory offer some flexibility, they fail to internalize the adaptive ability required for long-term improvement. Meta-Reinforcement Learning (meta-RL) provides an alternative by embedding the learning process directly within the model. However, existing meta-RL approaches for LLMs focus primarily on exploration in single-agent settings, neglecting the strategic exploitation necessary for multi-agent environments. We propose MAGE, a meta-RL framework that empowers LLM agents for strategic exploration and exploitation. MAGE utilizes a multi-episode training regime where interaction histories and reflections are integrated into the context window. By using the final episode reward as the objective, MAGE incentivizes the agent to refine its strategy based on past experiences. We further combine population-based training with an agent-specific advantage normalization technique to enrich agent diversity and ensure stable learning. Experiment results show that MAGE outperforms existing baselines in both exploration and exploitation tasks. Furthermore, MAGE exhibits strong generalization to unseen opponents, suggesting it has internalized the ability for strategic exploration and exploitation. Code is available at https://github.com/Lu-Yang666/MAGE.

AI.tldr: MAGE is a meta-RL framework for LLM agents that learns both strategic exploration and exploitation in multi-agent settings by training across episodes with final-episode rewards, integrating interaction histories/reflections into context, and using population-based training with agent-specific advantage normalization; it beats baselines and generalizes to unseen opponents.
AI.motivation: LLM agents handle learned tasks but fail to adapt robustly in non-stationary, multi-agent environments. In-context learning and external memory don’t internalize lasting adaptation, and current LLM meta-RL emphasizes exploration in single-agent settings while neglecting exploitation. A method that embeds adaptive strategy learning for both exploration and exploitation in multi-agent contexts is needed.
AI.method: Multi-episode meta-RL training: interaction histories and self-reflections are inserted into the context window across episodes; the objective is the final episode reward to encourage strategy refinement based on prior experience. Population-based training diversifies agents, and an agent-specific advantage normalization stabilizes learning and reduces variance.
AI.result: Across exploration and exploitation benchmarks, MAGE outperforms existing baselines and shows strong generalization to unseen opponents, suggesting it has internalized strategic behavior beyond the training distribution.
AI.conclusion: By embedding meta-learning over episodes and combining it with population-based diversity and normalized advantages, LLM agents acquire adaptive, strategic policies that balance exploration and exploitation in multi-agent environments, yielding improved performance and generalization.

title: AI4S-SDS: A Neuro-Symbolic Solvent Design System via Sparse MCTS and Differentiable Physics Alignment
summary: Automated design of chemical formulations is a cornerstone of materials science, yet it requires navigating a high-dimensional combinatorial space involving discrete compositional choices and continuous geometric constraints. Existing Large Language Model (LLM) agents face significant challenges in this setting, including context window limitations during long-horizon reasoning and path-dependent exploration that may lead to mode collapse. To address these issues, we introduce AI4S-SDS, a closed-loop neuro-symbolic framework that integrates multi-agent collaboration with a tailored Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) engine. We propose a Sparse State Storage mechanism with Dynamic Path Reconstruction, which decouples reasoning history from context length and enables arbitrarily deep exploration under fixed token budgets. To reduce local convergence and improve coverage, we implement a Global--Local Search Strategy: a memory-driven planning module adaptively reconfigures the search root based on historical feedback, while a Sibling-Aware Expansion mechanism promotes orthogonal exploration at the node level. Furthermore, we bridge symbolic reasoning and physical feasibility through a Differentiable Physics Engine, employing a hybrid normalized loss with sparsity-inducing regularization to optimize continuous mixing ratios under thermodynamic constraints. Empirical results show that AI4S-SDS achieves full validity under the adopted HSP-based physical constraints and substantially improves exploration diversity compared to baseline agents. In preliminary lithography experiments, the framework identifies a novel photoresist developer formulation that demonstrates competitive or superior performance relative to a commercial benchmark, highlighting the potential of diversity-driven neuro-symbolic search for scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: Introduces AI4S-SDS, a neuro-symbolic multi-agent + MCTS framework with sparse state memory and a differentiable physics engine to design chemical formulations, overcoming LLM context limits and mode collapse, yielding diverse, physically valid candidates and a competitive new photoresist developer.
AI.motivation: Automated formulation design spans vast combinatorial choices with continuous, physics-constrained parameters. Existing LLM agents struggle with long-horizon reasoning due to context window limits and path-dependent, locally convergent exploration, motivating a search method that is both memory-efficient and diversity-seeking while grounded in physical feasibility.
AI.method: AI4S-SDS couples multi-agent collaboration with a customized MCTS. It introduces Sparse State Storage with Dynamic Path Reconstruction to decouple reasoning history from token budgets, enabling arbitrarily deep searches. A Global–Local Search Strategy re-roots planning via memory-driven feedback, and Sibling-Aware Expansion promotes orthogonal exploration among children. A Differentiable Physics Engine with a hybrid normalized loss and sparsity-inducing regularization optimizes continuous mixing ratios under HSP-based thermodynamic constraints, closing the loop between symbolic planning and physical feasibility.
AI.result: The framework attains full validity under the adopted HSP physical constraints and substantially increases exploration diversity versus baseline LLM agents. In preliminary lithography trials, it discovers a novel photoresist developer formulation performing on par with or better than a commercial benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Diversity-driven neuro-symbolic search with sparse memory and differentiable physics effectively addresses long-horizon and mode-collapse issues in formulation design, enabling deeper, broader exploration under constraints and showing practical promise for scientific discovery, as evidenced by a competitive lithography developer.

title: RAGNav: A Retrieval-Augmented Topological Reasoning Framework for Multi-Goal Visual-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) is evolving from single-point pathfinding toward the more challenging Multi-Goal VLN. This task requires agents to accurately identify multiple entities while collaboratively reasoning over their spatial-physical constraints and sequential execution order. However, generic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) paradigms often suffer from spatial hallucinations and planning drift when handling multi-object associations due to the lack of explicit spatial modeling.To address these challenges, we propose RAGNav, a framework that bridges the gap between semantic reasoning and physical structure. The core of RAGNav is a Dual-Basis Memory system, which integrates a low-level topological map for maintaining physical connectivity with a high-level semantic forest for hierarchical environment abstraction. Building on this representation, the framework introduces an anchor-guided conditional retrieval and a topological neighbor score propagation mechanism. This approach facilitates the rapid screening of candidate targets and the elimination of semantic noise, while performing semantic calibration by leveraging the physical associations inherent in the topological neighborhood.This mechanism significantly enhances the capability of inter-target reachability reasoning and the efficiency of sequential planning. Experimental results demonstrate that RAGNav achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in complex multi-goal navigation tasks.

AI.tldr: RAGNav augments vision-language multi-goal navigation with explicit spatial structure: a dual memory (topological map + semantic forest) plus anchor-guided retrieval and neighbor score propagation to curb spatial hallucinations and planning drift, yielding SOTA in complex multi-goal tasks.
AI.motivation: Multi-goal VLN requires detecting multiple entities, reasoning about their spatial/physical relations, and planning an execution order. Generic RAG lacks explicit spatial modeling, leading to spatial hallucinations and planning drift when associating multiple objects and their constraints.
AI.method: Introduce RAGNav with a Dual-Basis Memory: (1) a low-level topological map capturing physical connectivity, and (2) a high-level semantic forest providing hierarchical abstraction. On top of this, use anchor-guided conditional retrieval to quickly focus on likely targets and a topological neighbor score propagation mechanism to calibrate semantics using physical neighborhood constraints, filtering noise and improving inter-target reachability reasoning and sequential planning.
AI.result: RAGNav achieves state-of-the-art performance on complex multi-goal navigation benchmarks (specific metrics and datasets not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Bridging semantic reasoning with explicit physical structure via dual-memory representation and structured retrieval/propagation mitigates spatial hallucinations and planning drift, enhancing multi-goal VLN planning and execution efficacy.

title: Proact-VL: A Proactive VideoLLM for Real-Time AI Companions
summary: Proactive and real-time interactive experiences are essential for human-like AI companions, yet face three key challenges: (1) achieving low-latency inference under continuous streaming inputs, (2) autonomously deciding when to respond, and (3) controlling both quality and quantity of generated content to meet real-time constraints. In this work, we instantiate AI companions through two gaming scenarios, commentator and guide, selected for their suitability for automatic evaluation. We introduce the Live Gaming Benchmark, a large-scale dataset with three representative scenarios: solo commentary, co-commentary, and user guidance, and present Proact-VL, a general framework that shapes multimodal language models into proactive, real-time interactive agents capable of human-like environment perception and interaction. Extensive experiments show Proact-VL achieves superior response latency and quality while maintaining strong video understanding capabilities, demonstrating its practicality for real-time interactive applications.

AI.tldr: Introduces Proact-VL, a framework that turns multimodal language models into proactive, low-latency, real-time interactive agents, plus the Live Gaming Benchmark (solo commentary, co-commentary, user guidance). It achieves faster responses and strong quality without sacrificing video understanding.
AI.motivation: Human-like AI companions must interact proactively in real time but face three hurdles: low-latency inference on continuous streams, deciding autonomously when to speak, and controlling output length/quality to meet real-time constraints. A reliable, automatable evaluation setting is also needed.
AI.method: Instantiate AI companions in gaming (commentator and guide). Build the Live Gaming Benchmark with three scenarios enabling automatic evaluation. Propose Proact-VL, a general framework that equips multimodal LLMs with streaming environment perception, autonomous response triggering, and content-control mechanisms to satisfy real-time interaction demands.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, Proact-VL yields lower response latency and higher interaction quality than baselines while preserving strong video understanding ability.
AI.conclusion: The combination of Proact-VL and the Live Gaming Benchmark provides a practical path to proactive, real-time multimodal agents and demonstrates readiness for real-time interactive applications.

title: AgentSelect: Benchmark for Narrative Query-to-Agent Recommendation
summary: LLM agents are rapidly becoming the practical interface for task automation, yet the ecosystem lacks a principled way to choose among an exploding space of deployable configurations. Existing LLM leaderboards and tool/agent benchmarks evaluate components in isolation and remain fragmented across tasks, metrics, and candidate pools, leaving a critical research gap: there is little query-conditioned supervision for learning to recommend end-to-end agent configurations that couple a backbone model with a toolkit. We address this gap with AgentSelect, a benchmark that reframes agent selection as narrative query-to-agent recommendation over capability profiles and systematically converts heterogeneous evaluation artifacts into unified, positive-only interaction data. AgentSelectcomprises 111,179 queries, 107,721 deployable agents, and 251,103 interaction records aggregated from 40+ sources, spanning LLM-only, toolkit-only, and compositional agents. Our analyses reveal a regime shift from dense head reuse to long-tail, near one-off supervision, where popularity-based CF/GNN methods become fragile and content-aware capability matching is essential. We further show that Part~III synthesized compositional interactions are learnable, induce capability-sensitive behavior under controlled counterfactual edits, and improve coverage over realistic compositions; models trained on AgentSelect also transfer to a public agent marketplace (MuleRun), yielding consistent gains on an unseen catalog. Overall, AgentSelect provides the first unified data and evaluation infrastructure for agent recommendation, which establishes a reproducible foundation to study and accelerate the emerging agent ecosystem.

AI.tldr: AgentSelect is a large, unified benchmark and evaluation framework that recasts agent selection as query-to-agent recommendation over capability profiles, aggregates 40+ heterogeneous sources into positive-only interactions, and shows content-aware matching outperforms popularity-based recommenders while transferring to a public agent marketplace.
AI.motivation: There is an exploding number of deployable LLM agents and toolkits, but no principled, query-conditioned way to choose end-to-end configurations. Existing leaderboards/benchmarks are fragmented, evaluate components in isolation, and lack supervision for recommending complete agents (backbone + tools).
AI.method: Build AgentSelect: (1) reframe agent selection as narrative query-to-agent recommendation via capability profiles; (2) systematically convert heterogeneous evaluation artifacts into unified, positive-only interaction data; (3) assemble a large corpus spanning LLM-only, toolkit-only, and compositional agents—111,179 queries, 107,721 agents, 251,103 interactions from 40+ sources; (4) include synthesized compositional interactions (Part III); (5) analyze supervision regimes; (6) train and evaluate recommenders, including counterfactual capability edits and transfer to an external marketplace (MuleRun).
AI.result: Find a regime shift from dense head reuse to a long-tail, near one-off supervision pattern where popularity-based CF/GNN methods are brittle; content-aware capability matching is crucial. Synthesized compositional interactions are learnable and induce capability-sensitive behavior under controlled edits, improving coverage of realistic compositions. Models trained on AgentSelect transfer to MuleRun and yield consistent gains on an unseen catalog.
AI.conclusion: AgentSelect provides the first unified data and evaluation infrastructure for agent recommendation, enabling reproducible research and more reliable, capability-aware routing/selection across the emerging LLM agent ecosystem.

title: Beyond Pixel Histories: World Models with Persistent 3D State
summary: Interactive world models continually generate video by responding to a user's actions, enabling open-ended generation capabilities. However, existing models typically lack a 3D representation of the environment, meaning 3D consistency must be implicitly learned from data, and spatial memory is restricted to limited temporal context windows. This results in an unrealistic user experience and presents significant obstacles to down-stream tasks such as training agents. To address this, we present PERSIST, a new paradigm of world model which simulates the evolution of a latent 3D scene: environment, camera, and renderer. This allows us to synthesize new frames with persistent spatial memory and consistent geometry. Both quantitative metrics and a qualitative user study show substantial improvements in spatial memory, 3D consistency, and long-horizon stability over existing methods, enabling coherent, evolving 3D worlds. We further demonstrate novel capabilities, including synthesising diverse 3D environments from a single image, as well as enabling fine-grained, geometry-aware control over generated experiences by supporting environment editing and specification directly in 3D space. Project page: https://francelico.github.io/persist.github.io

AI.tldr: PERSIST is an interactive world model that maintains and evolves a latent 3D scene (environment, camera, renderer) to generate videos with persistent spatial memory, 3D-consistent geometry, and stable long-horizon behavior, while enabling 3D-space editing and single-image-to-3D environment synthesis.
AI.motivation: Current interactive video world models lack explicit 3D representations, forcing them to learn 3D consistency implicitly and rely on short temporal windows for memory. This degrades realism and hampers downstream uses like agent training.
AI.method: Simulate the evolution of a latent 3D scene that explicitly models environment, camera, and a rendering process. New frames are synthesized by rendering from this persistent 3D state, enabling geometry-aware control and direct editing or specification in 3D. It also bootstraps diverse 3D environments from a single input image.
AI.result: Compared to prior methods, PERSIST achieves substantial gains on quantitative metrics and in a user study for spatial memory, 3D consistency, and long-horizon stability, producing coherent, evolving 3D worlds. It also demonstrates single-image-to-3D scene generation and fine-grained geometry-aware control.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and evolving a latent 3D scene yields more realistic, controllable, and stable interactive video generation, advancing world models for both user experience and downstream tasks.

title: LifeBench: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Multi-Source Memory
comment: A total of 28 pages, 8 pages of main text, and 15 figures and tables
summary: Long-term memory is fundamental for personalized agents capable of accumulating knowledge, reasoning over user experiences, and adapting across time. However, existing memory benchmarks primarily target declarative memory, specifically semantic and episodic types, where all information is explicitly presented in dialogues. In contrast, real-world actions are also governed by non-declarative memory, including habitual and procedural types, and need to be inferred from diverse digital traces. To bridge this gap, we introduce Lifebench, which features densely connected, long-horizon event simulation. It pushes AI agents beyond simple recall, requiring the integration of declarative and non-declarative memory reasoning across diverse and temporally extended contexts. Building such a benchmark presents two key challenges: ensuring data quality and scalability. We maintain data quality by employing real-world priors, including anonymized social surveys, map APIs, and holiday-integrated calendars, thus enforcing fidelity, diversity and behavioral rationality within the dataset. Towards scalability, we draw inspiration from cognitive science and structure events according to their partonomic hierarchy; enabling efficient parallel generation while maintaining global coherence. Performance results show that top-tier, state-of-the-art memory systems reach just 55.2\% accuracy, highlighting the inherent difficulty of long-horizon retrieval and multi-source integration within our proposed benchmark. The dataset and data synthesis code are available at https://github.com/1754955896/LifeBench.

AI.tldr: LifeBench is a long-horizon benchmark that tests AI agents on both declarative and non-declarative memory by simulating dense, realistic life events; current top systems score only ~55%, underscoring the difficulty of integrating multi-source, temporally extended information.
AI.motivation: Personalized agents need durable memory that goes beyond recalling explicitly stated facts. Existing benchmarks mostly assess declarative memory (semantic/episodic) from dialogues, overlooking non-declarative (habitual/procedural) knowledge that must be inferred from digital traces. There is a need for a realistic, scalable benchmark that captures both types across long time spans.
AI.method: Introduce LifeBench, a densely connected, long-horizon event simulation benchmark requiring integration of declarative and non-declarative memory. Data quality is ensured via real-world priors (anonymized social surveys, map APIs, holiday-aware calendars) to enhance fidelity, diversity, and behavioral rationality. Scalability is achieved by structuring events with a partonomic hierarchy, enabling parallel generation while preserving global coherence.
AI.result: State-of-the-art memory systems achieve 55.2% accuracy on LifeBench, indicating substantial difficulty with long-horizon retrieval and multi-source integration. The dataset and synthesis code are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: LifeBench pushes agents beyond simple recall toward integrated, long-term memory reasoning. It offers a high-fidelity, scalable benchmark and reveals a significant performance gap, motivating future research on memory architectures and reasoning over heterogeneous, temporally extended contexts.

title: Geographically-Weighted Weakly Supervised Bayesian High-Resolution Transformer for 200m Resolution Pan-Arctic Sea Ice Concentration Mapping and Uncertainty Estimation using Sentinel-1, RCM, and AMSR2 Data
summary: Although high-resolution mapping of pan-Arctic sea ice with reliable corresponding uncertainty is essential for operational sea ice concentration (SIC) charting, it is a difficult task due to key challenges, such as the subtle nature of ice signature features, inexact SIC labels, model uncertainty, and data heterogeneity. This study presents a novel Bayesian High-Resolution Transformer approach for 200 meter resolution pan-Arctic SIC mapping and uncertainty quantification using Sentinel-1, RADARSAT Constellation Mission (RCM), and Advanced Microwave Scanning Radiometer 2 (AMSR2) data. First, to improve small and subtle sea ice feature (e.g., cracks/leads, ponds, and ice floes) extraction, we design a novel high-resolution Transformer model with both global and local modules that can better discern the subtle differences in sea ice patterns. Second, to address low-resolution and inexact SIC labels, we design a geographically-weighted weakly supervised loss function to supervise the model at region level instead of pixel level, and to prioritize pure open water and ice pack signatures while mitigating the impact of ambiguity in the marginal ice zone (MIZ). Third, to improve uncertainty quantification, we design a Bayesian extension of the proposed Transformer model, treating its parameters as random variables to more effectively capture uncertainties. Fourth, to address data heterogeneity, we fuse three different data types (Sentinel-1, RCM, and AMSR2) at decision-level to improve both SIC mapping and uncertainty quantification. The proposed approach is evaluated under pan-Arctic minimum-extent conditions in 2021 and 2025. Results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves 0.70 overall feature detection accuracy using Sentinel-1 data, while also preserving pan-Arctic SIC patterns (Sentinel-1 R\textsuperscript{2} = 0.90 relative to the ARTIST Sea Ice product).

AI.tldr: Introduces a Bayesian high‑resolution Transformer that fuses Sentinel‑1, RCM, and AMSR2 to produce 200 m pan‑Arctic sea‑ice concentration maps with quantified uncertainty, achieving 0.70 feature detection accuracy and R²=0.90 vs. ASI.
AI.motivation: Operational sea‑ice concentration charting needs high‑resolution maps and reliable uncertainty, but faces subtle ice signatures, coarse/inexact labels, model uncertainty, and heterogeneous sensors across the Arctic.
AI.method: A 200 m Bayesian High‑Resolution Transformer with: (1) global+local Transformer modules to capture subtle features (cracks/leads, ponds, floes); (2) a geographically‑weighted weakly supervised region‑level loss prioritizing pure open water and ice pack while down‑weighting ambiguous MIZ; (3) Bayesian parameterization to capture model uncertainty; and (4) decision‑level fusion of Sentinel‑1, RCM, and AMSR2 outputs.
AI.result: Under 2021 and 2025 minimum‑extent conditions, the model attains 0.70 overall feature detection accuracy on Sentinel‑1 and preserves pan‑Arctic SIC patterns with Sentinel‑1 R²=0.90 relative to the ARTIST Sea Ice product; authors report improved mapping and uncertainty quantification from multi‑sensor fusion.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively delivers high‑resolution (200 m) SIC maps with associated uncertainty, better handles subtle ice features and weak labels, and appears promising for operational charting across heterogeneous Arctic sensors.

title: A Rubric-Supervised Critic from Sparse Real-World Outcomes
summary: Academic benchmarks for coding agents tend to reward autonomous task completion, measured by verifiable rewards such as unit-test success. In contrast, real-world coding agents operate with humans in the loop, where success signals are typically noisy, delayed, and sparse. How can we bridge this gap? In this paper, we propose a process to learn a "critic" model from sparse and noisy interaction data, which can then be used both as a reward model for either RL-based training or inference-time scaling. Specifically, we introduce Critic Rubrics, a rubric-based supervision framework with 24 behavioral features that can be derived from human-agent interaction traces alone. Using a semi-supervised objective, we can then jointly predict these rubrics and sparse human feedback (when present). In experiments, we demonstrate that, despite being trained primarily from trace-observable rubrics and sparse real-world outcome proxies, these critics improve best-of-N reranking on SWE-bench (Best@8 +15.9 over Random@8 over the rerankable subset of trajectories), enable early stopping (+17.7 with 83% fewer attempts), and support training-time data curation via critic-selected trajectories.

AI.tldr: They learn a rubric-supervised critic from noisy, sparse human–agent coding traces and use it as a reward model to guide reranking, early stopping, and data curation, yielding sizable gains on SWE-bench.
AI.motivation: Benchmarks assume autonomous agents with clear, dense rewards (e.g., unit tests), but real-world coding agents work with humans where feedback is noisy, delayed, and sparse. The gap hinders training and evaluation of practical agents.
AI.method: Introduce Critic Rubrics: 24 trace-observable behavioral features extracted from human–agent interaction logs. Train a critic with a semi-supervised objective to jointly predict rubric scores and, when available, sparse human feedback/outcome proxies. Use the critic as (i) a reward model for RL or selection, and (ii) an inference-time guide for best-of-N reranking and early stopping; also to curate training data by selecting promising trajectories.
AI.result: Critics trained mainly from rubrics and sparse outcome proxies improve: best-of-N reranking on SWE-bench (Best@8 +15.9 over Random@8 on rerankable trajectories), enable early stopping (+17.7 with 83% fewer attempts), and help curate data through critic-selected trajectories.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-supervised critics learned from interaction traces effectively approximate success signals in human-in-the-loop coding, improving efficiency and performance and helping bridge the gap between academic benchmarks and real-world agent deployment.

title: From Threat Intelligence to Firewall Rules: Semantic Relations in Hybrid AI Agent and Expert System Architectures
summary: Web security demands rapid response capabilities to evolving cyber threats. Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) promises automation, but the need for trustworthy security responses is of the utmost importance. This work investigates the role of semantic relations in extracting information for sensitive operational tasks, such as configuring security controls for mitigating threats. To this end, it proposes to leverage hypernym-hyponym textual relations to extract relevant information from Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) reports. By leveraging a neuro-symbolic approach, the multi-agent system automatically generates CLIPS code for an expert system creating firewall rules to block malicious network traffic. Experimental results show the superior performance of the hypernym-hyponym retrieval strategy compared to various baselines and the higher effectiveness of the agentic approach in mitigating threats.

AI.tldr: Agentic neuro-symbolic system mines hypernym–hyponym relations in CTI reports to auto-generate CLIPS expert-system rules for firewall configuration, outperforming baselines and improving threat mitigation.
AI.motivation: Security teams need rapid, trustworthy automation to translate unstructured CTI into precise, auditable security controls (e.g., firewall rules) for fast response to evolving threats.
AI.method: A multi-agent, neuro-symbolic pipeline exploits hypernym–hyponym textual relations to retrieve and extract relevant CTI facts, then automatically synthesizes CLIPS code for an expert system that derives firewall rules to block malicious traffic.
AI.result: Experiments show the hypernym–hyponym retrieval strategy outperforms several baselines, and the agentic approach yields higher threat-mitigation effectiveness than alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating semantic relation awareness within an agentic, neuro-symbolic workflow is a promising and more effective path to trustworthy automation of operational security tasks such as firewall rule generation.

title: Generative AI in Managerial Decision-Making: Redefining Boundaries through Ambiguity Resolution and Sycophancy Analysis
summary: Generative artificial intelligence is increasingly being integrated into complex business workflows, fundamentally shifting the boundaries of managerial decision-making. However, the reliability of its strategic advice in ambiguous business contexts remains a critical knowledge gap. This study addresses this by comparing various models on ambiguity detection, evaluating how a systematic resolution process enhances response quality, and investigating their sycophantic behavior when presented with flawed directives. Using a novel four-dimensional business ambiguity taxonomy, we conducted a human-in-the-loop experiment across strategic, tactical, and operational scenarios. The resulting decisions were assessed with an "LLM-as-a-judge" framework on criteria including agreement, actionability, justification quality, and constraint adherence. Results reveal distinct performance capabilities. While models excel in detecting internal contradictions and contextual ambiguities, they struggle with structural linguistic nuances. Ambiguity resolution consistently increased response quality across all decision types, while sycophantic behavior analysis revealed distinct patterns depending on the model architecture. This study contributes to the bounded rationality literature by positioning GAI as a cognitive scaffold that can detect and resolve ambiguities managers might overlook, but whose own artificial limitations necessitate human management to ensure its reliability as a strategic partner.

AI.tldr: GAI can improve managerial decision-making by detecting and helping resolve certain ambiguities, especially when paired with a structured resolution process, but models vary widely, struggle with linguistic-structural ambiguity, show architecture-dependent sycophancy, and still require human oversight.
AI.motivation: As GAI becomes embedded in business workflows, its reliability in ambiguous strategic contexts is unclear. The study seeks to close this gap by assessing how well different models detect ambiguity, whether systematic resolution improves decision quality, and how prone models are to sycophancy under flawed directives.
AI.method: Introduce a four-dimensional taxonomy of business ambiguity; run a human-in-the-loop experiment spanning strategic, tactical, and operational scenarios; compare multiple models; evaluate outputs with an LLM-as-a-judge on agreement, actionability, justification quality, and constraint adherence; and probe sycophancy by feeding flawed instructions.
AI.result: Models show differentiated strengths: they detect internal contradictions and contextual ambiguities well but falter on structural linguistic nuances. Applying a structured ambiguity-resolution process consistently boosts response quality across decision types. Sycophantic tendencies vary systematically by model architecture.
AI.conclusion: GAI functions as a cognitive scaffold within bounded rationality—capable of surfacing and resolving ambiguities managers might miss—yet its limitations (linguistic-structural blind spots and sycophancy) necessitate human governance and careful model/process design for reliable strategic partnership.

title: Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B Technical Report
summary: We present Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a compact open-weight multimodal reasoning model, and share the motivations, design choices, experiments, and learnings that informed its development. Our goal is to contribute practical insight to the research community on building smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models and to share the result of these learnings as an open-weight model that is good at common vision and language tasks and excels at scientific and mathematical reasoning and understanding user interfaces. Our contributions include demonstrating that careful architecture choices and rigorous data curation enable smaller, open-weight multimodal models to achieve competitive performance with significantly less training and inference-time compute and tokens. The most substantial improvements come from systematic filtering, error correction, and synthetic augmentation -- reinforcing that data quality remains the primary lever for model performance. Systematic ablations show that high-resolution, dynamic-resolution encoders yield consistent improvements, as accurate perception is a prerequisite for high-quality reasoning. Finally, a hybrid mix of reasoning and non-reasoning data with explicit mode tokens allows a single model to deliver fast direct answers for simpler tasks and chain-of-thought reasoning for complex problems.

AI.tldr: Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B is an open 15B multimodal model that achieves competitive performance on vision–language tasks and excels at scientific/math/UI reasoning by pairing rigorous data curation with high/dynamic‑resolution vision encoders and a hybrid training scheme with mode tokens—delivering efficient training/inference and flexible direct-answer or chain-of-thought outputs.
AI.motivation: Provide practical guidance and an open-weight exemplar for building smaller, efficient multimodal reasoning models that still perform strongly on common V+L tasks and especially on scientific, mathematical, and UI understanding.
AI.method: Combine careful architecture and data strategy: (1) use high-resolution, dynamic-resolution visual encoders to improve perception; (2) apply rigorous data curation—systematic filtering, error correction, and synthetic augmentation—to raise data quality; (3) train on a hybrid mix of reasoning and non-reasoning data with explicit mode tokens to support both fast direct answers and chain-of-thought reasoning; (4) run systematic ablations to validate design choices.
AI.result: The model attains competitive performance with significantly reduced training and inference compute and token budgets. The largest gains stem from improved data quality. Ablations show consistent boosts from high/dynamic-resolution encoders. The hybrid data plus mode tokens enable responsive behavior: quick direct answers for simple tasks and CoT for complex problems; strong results on scientific/math reasoning and UI understanding.
AI.conclusion: Data quality is the dominant lever for performance; accurate perception (via high/dynamic-resolution encoders) underpins strong reasoning; and mixing reasoning/non-reasoning data with mode tokens yields a single, efficient model that can switch between fast answers and deep chain-of-thought—demonstrating that small open-weight multimodal models can be competitive at lower cost.

title: A Dual-Helix Governance Approach Towards Reliable Agentic AI for WebGIS Development
comment: Paper submitted to Transactions in GIS
summary: WebGIS development requires rigor, yet agentic AI frequently fails due to five large language model (LLM) limitations: context constraints, cross-session forgetting, stochasticity, instruction failure, and adaptation rigidity. We propose a dual-helix governance framework reframing these challenges as structural governance problems that model capacity alone cannot resolve. We implement the framework as a 3-track architecture (Knowledge, Behavior, Skills) that uses a knowledge graph substrate to stabilize execution by externalizing domain facts and enforcing executable protocols, complemented by a self-learning cycle for autonomous knowledge growth. Applying this to the FutureShorelines WebGIS tool, a governed agent refactored a 2,265-line monolithic codebase into modular ES6 components. Results demonstrated a 51\% reduction in cyclomatic complexity and a 7-point increase in maintainability index. A comparative experiment against a zero-shot LLM confirms that externalized governance, not just model capability, drives operational reliability in geospatial engineering. This approach is implemented in the open-source AgentLoom governance toolkit.

AI.tldr: A governance-first agent architecture for WebGIS uses a knowledge graph and protocolized behaviors to overcome LLM limits, yielding large code-quality gains in a real refactoring task and outperforming a zero-shot LLM; released as the open-source AgentLoom toolkit.
AI.motivation: WebGIS engineering demands rigor and reliability, but agentic LLMs often fail due to context limits, cross-session forgetting, stochasticity, instruction-following failures, and rigidity. The authors argue these are governance/structure problems that model scaling alone will not solve.
AI.method: They propose a dual-helix governance framework operationalized as a 3-track architecture—Knowledge, Behavior, and Skills. A knowledge-graph substrate externalizes domain facts and enforces executable protocols; a self-learning loop grows and updates knowledge. They implement this in the FutureShorelines WebGIS context and compare against a zero-shot LLM baseline.
AI.result: A governed agent refactored a 2,265-line monolith into modular ES6 components with a 51% reduction in cyclomatic complexity and a +7 maintainability index. Comparative experiments indicate externalized governance, not raw model capability, drives improved operational reliability over a zero-shot LLM.
AI.conclusion: External governance that formalizes knowledge and procedures can stabilize agentic AI for geospatial engineering; model capacity alone is insufficient. The approach is delivered as the open-source AgentLoom toolkit and shows measurable software quality benefits.

title: MPFlow: Multi-modal Posterior-Guided Flow Matching for Zero-Shot MRI Reconstruction
summary: Zero-shot MRI reconstruction relies on generative priors, but single-modality unconditional priors produce hallucinations under severe ill-posedness. In many clinical workflows, complementary MRI acquisitions (e.g. high-quality structural scans) are routinely available, yet existing reconstruction methods lack mechanisms to leverage this additional information. We propose MPFlow, a zero-shot multi-modal reconstruction framework built on rectified flow that incorporates auxiliary MRI modalities at inference time without retraining the generative prior to improve anatomical fidelity. Cross-modal guidance is enabled by our proposed self-supervised pretraining strategy, Patch-level Multi-modal MR Image Pretraining (PAMRI), which learns shared representations across modalities. Sampling is jointly guided by data consistency and cross-modal feature alignment using pre-trained PAMRI, systematically suppressing intrinsic and extrinsic hallucinations. Extensive experiments on HCP and BraTS show that MPFlow matches diffusion baselines on image quality using only 20% of sampling steps while reducing tumor hallucinations by more than 15% (segmentation dice score). This demonstrates that cross-modal guidance enables more reliable and efficient zero-shot MRI reconstruction.

AI.tldr: MPFlow is a zero-shot multi-modal MRI reconstruction method that uses a rectified-flow prior plus cross-modal guidance from a self-supervised, patch-level multi-modal pretraining (PAMRI) to align features across MRI modalities at inference, cutting sampling to 20% of diffusion baselines while reducing tumor hallucinations by >15% (Dice).
AI.motivation: Zero-shot MRI reconstruction with single-modality, unconditional generative priors often hallucinates anatomy under severe undersampling, despite clinical availability of complementary MRI modalities that current methods cannot exploit at inference.
AI.method: Build a rectified-flow-based reconstruction framework (MPFlow) that accepts auxiliary MRI modalities at test time without retraining the generative prior. Pretrain a cross-modal encoder (PAMRI) via self-supervised, patch-level multi-modal learning to obtain shared representations. During sampling, jointly enforce k-space data consistency and align reconstructed features to the auxiliary-modality features from PAMRI to suppress intrinsic/extrinsic hallucinations.
AI.result: On HCP and BraTS, MPFlow matches diffusion model baselines in image quality using only 20% of sampling steps and reduces tumor hallucinations by >15% measured via segmentation Dice.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal guidance via PAMRI-enabled feature alignment makes zero-shot MRI reconstruction both more reliable (fewer hallucinations) and more efficient (fewer sampling steps) without retraining the prior, suggesting a practical path to clinically robust recon leveraging routinely available modalities.

title: Automated Concept Discovery for LLM-as-a-Judge Preference Analysis
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as scalable evaluators of model outputs, but their preference judgments exhibit systematic biases and can diverge from human evaluations. Prior work on LLM-as-a-judge has largely focused on a small, predefined set of hypothesized biases, leaving open the problem of automatically discovering unknown drivers of LLM preferences. We address this gap by studying several embedding-level concept extraction methods for analyzing LLM judge behavior. We compare these methods in terms of interpretability and predictiveness, finding that sparse autoencoder-based approaches recover substantially more interpretable preference features than alternatives while remaining competitive in predicting LLM decisions. Using over 27k paired responses from multiple human preference datasets and judgments from three LLMs, we analyze LLM judgments and compare them to those of human annotators. Our method both validates existing results, such as the tendency for LLMs to prefer refusal of sensitive requests at higher rates than humans, and uncovers new trends across both general and domain-specific datasets, including biases toward responses that emphasize concreteness and empathy in approaching new situations, toward detail and formality in academic advice, and against legal guidance that promotes active steps like calling police and filing lawsuits. Our results show that automated concept discovery enables systematic analysis of LLM judge preferences without predefined bias taxonomies.

AI.tldr: Automated concept discovery (notably via sparse autoencoders on embeddings) uncovers and explains features driving LLM-as-judge preferences, validating known biases and revealing new, domain-specific trends compared with human judgments—enabling systematic audits without predefined taxonomies.
AI.motivation: LLMs are used as scalable judges but show systematic, sometimes opaque biases that diverge from humans. Prior work largely probes a small, hypothesized set of biases, leaving unknown drivers unexplored. The goal is to automatically discover and interpret the concepts that influence LLM preference judgments and assess alignment with human evaluations.
AI.method: Compare several embedding-level concept extraction techniques—emphasizing sparse autoencoders—on 27k paired responses drawn from multiple human preference datasets, with judgments from three LLMs. Evaluate methods on interpretability and predictive accuracy for LLM decisions, then analyze learned concepts to explain divergences and consistencies with human annotators across general and domain-specific tasks.
AI.result: Sparse autoencoders recover substantially more interpretable preference features while remaining competitive at predicting LLM judgments. The approach reproduces known findings (e.g., higher LLM refusal rates for sensitive requests vs. humans) and surfaces new, domain-linked trends: preferences for concreteness and empathy in novel-situation responses, for detail and formality in academic advice, and aversion to legal guidance that promotes active interventions (e.g., calling police, filing lawsuits).
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based, automated concept discovery effectively audits LLM-judge behavior without relying on predefined bias taxonomies, offering a scalable, interpretable way to map preference drivers and characterize where LLM evaluations diverge from human preferences.

title: TopicENA: Enabling Epistemic Network Analysis at Scale through Automated Topic-Based Coding
summary: Epistemic Network Analysis (ENA) is a method for investigating the relational structure of concepts in text by representing co-occurring concepts as networks. Traditional ENA, however, relies heavily on manual expert coding, which limits its scalability and real-world applicability to large text corpora. Topic modeling provides an automated approach to extracting concept-level representations from text and can serve as an alternative to manual coding. To tackle this limitation, the present study merges BERTopic with ENA and introduces TopicENA, a topic-based epistemic network analysis framework. TopicENA substitutes manual concept coding with automatically generated topics while maintaining ENA's capacity for modeling structural associations among concepts. To explain the impact of modeling choices on TopicENA outcomes, three analysis cases are presented. The first case assesses the effect of topic granularity, indicating that coarse-grained topics are preferable for large datasets, whereas fine-grained topics are more effective for smaller datasets. The second case examines topic inclusion thresholds and finds that threshold values should be adjusted according to topic quality indicators to balance network consistency and interpretability. The third case tests TopicENA's scalability by applying it to a substantially larger dataset than those used in previous ENA studies. Collectively, these cases illustrate that TopicENA facilitates practical and interpretable ENA analysis at scale and offers concrete guidance for configuring topic-based ENA pipelines in large-scale text analysis.

AI.tldr: TopicENA integrates BERTopic with Epistemic Network Analysis to replace manual coding with automated topics, enabling scalable, interpretable network analyses of large text corpora and offering guidance on model configuration.
AI.motivation: Traditional ENA depends on manual expert coding of concepts, which is labor-intensive, limits scalability, and hinders application to large text datasets. Topic modeling can provide automated concept proxies, motivating a combined approach.
AI.method: Proposes TopicENA, which substitutes ENA’s manual concept codes with topics generated by BERTopic while preserving ENA’s relational-network modeling. Demonstrates effects of modeling choices via three cases: (1) topic granularity (coarse vs. fine), (2) topic inclusion thresholds informed by topic quality indicators, and (3) scalability on a much larger corpus than prior ENA work.
AI.result: Findings indicate: (1) coarse-grained topics perform better for large datasets; fine-grained topics work better for small datasets; (2) topic inclusion thresholds should be tuned based on topic quality to balance network consistency and interpretability; (3) TopicENA scales to substantially larger datasets than prior ENA applications.
AI.conclusion: TopicENA enables practical, interpretable ENA at scale by automating concept extraction with topic models and provides concrete guidance for configuring topic-based ENA pipelines (granularity, thresholds) for large-scale text analysis.

title: LDP-Slicing: Local Differential Privacy for Images via Randomized Bit-Plane Slicing
summary: Local Differential Privacy (LDP) is the gold standard trust model for privacy-preserving machine learning by guaranteeing privacy at the data source. However, its application to image data has long been considered impractical due to the high dimensionality of pixel space. Canonical LDP mechanisms are designed for low-dimensional data, resulting in severe utility degradation when applied to high-dimensional pixel spaces. This paper demonstrates that this utility loss is not inherent to LDP, but from its application to an inappropriate data representation. We introduce LDP-Slicing, a lightweight, training-free framework that resolves this domain mismatch. Our key insight is to decompose pixel values into a sequence of binary bit-planes. This transformation allows us to apply the LDP mechanism directly to the bit-level representation. To further strengthen privacy and preserve utility, we integrate a perceptual obfuscation module that mitigates human-perceivable leakage and an optimization-based privacy budget allocation strategy. This pipeline satisfies rigorous pixel-level $\varepsilon$-LDP while producing images that retain high utility for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments on face recognition and image classification demonstrate that LDP-Slicing outperforms existing DP/LDP baselines under comparable privacy budgets, with negligible computational overhead.

AI.tldr: LDP-Slicing makes local differential privacy practical for images by applying LDP to binary bit-planes of pixels, coupled with perceptual obfuscation and optimized budget allocation, achieving pixel-level ε-LDP with strong downstream utility and minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Classical LDP mechanisms are designed for low-dimensional data and perform poorly on high-dimensional images, causing severe utility loss. The authors argue this is due to a mismatch in representation (pixel space) rather than an inherent limitation of LDP, motivating a new representation and processing pipeline for images under LDP.
AI.method: 1) Decompose each pixel into binary bit-planes. 2) Apply standard LDP mechanisms directly at the bit level. 3) Add a perceptual obfuscation module to reduce human-perceivable leakage while preserving machine-usable signal. 4) Use an optimization procedure to allocate the privacy budget across bit-planes for the best utility under a fixed ε. The overall pipeline is training-free and lightweight, and guarantees pixel-level ε-LDP.
AI.result: On face recognition and image classification benchmarks, the method outperforms DP/LDP baselines under comparable privacy budgets while maintaining rigorous ε-LDP guarantees and incurring negligible computational overhead. Produced images retain high utility for downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: Utility degradation for images is not inherent to LDP; by operating on bit-planes with tailored perceptual obfuscation and optimized budget allocation, one can achieve strict local privacy with high task performance. LDP-Slicing provides a practical, efficient route to private image sharing/learning without training or heavy computation.

title: From We to Me: Theory Informed Narrative Shift with Abductive Reasoning
summary: Effective communication often relies on aligning a message with an audience's narrative and worldview. Narrative shift involves transforming text to reflect a different narrative framework while preserving its original core message--a task we demonstrate is significantly challenging for current Large Language Models (LLMs). To address this, we propose a neurosymbolic approach grounded in social science theory and abductive reasoning. Our method automatically extracts rules to abduce the specific story elements needed to guide an LLM through a consistent and targeted narrative transformation. Across multiple LLMs, abduction-guided transformed stories shifted the narrative while maintaining the fidelity with the original story. For example, with GPT-4o we outperform the zero-shot LLM baseline by 55.88% for collectivistic to individualistic narrative shift while maintaining superior semantic similarity with the original stories (40.4% improvement in KL divergence). For individualistic to collectivistic transformation, we achieve comparable improvements. We show similar performance across both directions for Llama-4, and Grok-4 and competitive performance for Deepseek-R1.

AI.tldr: A neurosymbolic, abductive-reasoning framework extracts theory-driven rules to guide LLMs in rewriting stories between narrative frames (e.g., collectivistic ↔ individualistic), yielding large gains over zero-shot while preserving semantic fidelity across multiple models.
AI.motivation: Effective persuasion requires aligning messages with audience worldviews, yet current LLMs struggle to shift a story’s narrative frame without altering its core meaning; a controllable, theory-grounded method is needed.
AI.method: A neurosymbolic pipeline grounded in social-science theory automatically extracts rules and uses abductive reasoning to infer which story elements must change, then guides an LLM to perform a consistent, targeted narrative transformation.
AI.result: Across models, the abduction-guided rewrites achieved desired narrative shifts while maintaining fidelity. With GPT-4o: 55.88% improvement over zero-shot for collectivistic→individualistic shift and 40.4% better KL divergence (higher semantic similarity); comparable improvements for the reverse direction; similar results for Llama-4 and Grok-4, competitive for Deepseek-R1.
AI.conclusion: Theory-driven, abductive guidance reliably steers narrative framing without distorting meaning, outperforms zero-shot baselines, and generalizes across LLMs.

title: Old Habits Die Hard: How Conversational History Geometrically Traps LLMs
summary: How does the conversational past of large language models (LLMs) influence their future performance? Recent work suggests that LLMs are affected by their conversational history in unexpected ways. For instance, hallucinations in prior interactions may influence subsequent model responses. In this work, we introduce History-Echoes, a framework that investigates how conversational history biases subsequent generations. The framework explores this bias from two perspectives: probabilistically, we model conversations as Markov chains to quantify state consistency; geometrically, we measure the consistency of consecutive hidden representations. Across three model families and six datasets spanning diverse phenomena, our analysis reveals a strong correlation between the two perspectives. By bridging these perspectives, we demonstrate that behavioral persistence manifests as a geometric trap, where gaps in the latent space confine the model's trajectory. Code available at https://github.com/technion-cs-nlp/OldHabitsDieHard.

AI.tldr: History-Echoes studies how prior conversational turns bias later LLM responses, unifying a probabilistic view (Markov-chain state consistency) with a geometric view (consistency of hidden representations). Across models and datasets, these views correlate strongly, revealing “geometric traps” in latent space that cause behavioral persistence (e.g., lingering hallucinations).
AI.motivation: LLMs exhibit path dependence: errors or styles in earlier turns can persist and distort future outputs. There is a need to quantify and explain how conversational history shapes subsequent behavior, beyond anecdotal observations, to enable diagnosis and mitigation.
AI.method: Propose a two-pronged framework: (1) Probabilistic—model conversation dynamics as a Markov chain over behavioral states and quantify state consistency/persistence; (2) Geometric—measure similarity/trajectory continuity in hidden representations across turns. Apply to three model families and six datasets capturing varied phenomena; examine correlations between the two perspectives.
AI.result: Find a strong correlation between Markov-state consistency and geometric consistency across all tested models/datasets. Evidence indicates that history-driven behavioral persistence arises from “geometric traps”: regions and gaps in latent space that constrain the model’s trajectory, leading to lingering behaviors such as hallucinations.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral persistence in LLM dialogues has a geometric underpinning; aligning probabilistic dynamics with representation geometry explains why prior states can trap models. The framework offers diagnostic tools and opens paths to interventions for mitigating history-induced bias. Code is released for reproducibility.

title: Glass Segmentation with Fusion of Learned and General Visual Features
summary: Glass surface segmentation from RGB images is a challenging task, since glass as a transparent material distinctly lacks visual characteristics. However, glass segmentation is critical for scene understanding and robotics, as transparent glass surfaces must be identified as solid material. This paper presents a novel architecture for glass segmentation, deploying a dual-backbone producing general visual features as well as task-specific learned visual features. General visual features are produced by a frozen DINOv3 vision foundation model, and the task-specific features are generated with a Swin model trained in a supervised manner. Resulting multi-scale feature representations are downsampled with residual Squeeze-and-Excitation Channel Reduction, and fed into a Mask2Former Decoder, producing the final segmentation masks. The architecture was evaluated on four commonly used glass segmentation datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results on several accuracy metrics. The model also has a competitive inference speed compared to the previous state-of-the-art method, and surpasses it when using a lighter DINOv3 backbone variant. The implementation source code and model weights are available at: https://github.com/ojalar/lgnet

AI.tldr: A dual-backbone glass-segmentation network fuses frozen DINOv3 foundation features with a supervised Swin backbone, compresses multi-scale features via residual SE channel reduction, and decodes masks with Mask2Former, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on four datasets with competitive speed; code and weights are released.
AI.motivation: Transparent glass lacks strong visual cues in RGB, yet detecting it is vital for robotics and scene understanding where glass must be treated as solid. Existing methods struggle to robustly segment glass from standard images.
AI.method: Use two complementary backbones: (1) a frozen DINOv3 vision foundation model to provide general, rich visual representations; (2) a supervised Swin backbone to learn task-specific features. Merge multi-scale features, apply residual Squeeze-and-Excitation Channel Reduction for compact, discriminative representations, and feed them into a Mask2Former decoder to produce segmentation masks.
AI.result: On four public glass-segmentation datasets, the approach attains state-of-the-art results on several accuracy metrics. Inference speed is competitive with the previous SOTA and becomes faster when a lighter DINOv3 variant is used.
AI.conclusion: Combining frozen foundation-model features with a task-specific backbone and a modern mask decoder yields accurate and efficient glass segmentation from RGB. The released implementation and weights facilitate reproduction and extension.

title: DIALEVAL: Automated Type-Theoretic Evaluation of LLM Instruction Following
summary: Evaluating instruction following in Large Language Models requires decomposing instructions into verifiable requirements and assessing satisfaction--tasks currently dependent on manual annotation and uniform criteria that do not align with human judgment patterns. We present DIALEVAL, a type-theoretic framework using dual LLM agents to automate instruction decomposition into typed predicates and implement type-specific satisfaction semantics. The framework enforces formal atomicity and independence constraints during automated extraction, then applies differentiated evaluation criteria--semantic equivalence for content predicates, exact precision for numerical predicates--mirroring empirically observed human assessment patterns. Extended to multi-turn dialogues through history-aware satisfaction functions, DIALEVAL enables evaluation in conversational contexts where single-turn methods fail. Validation demonstrates 90.38% accuracy (26.45% error reduction over baselines) and substantially stronger correlation with human judgment for complex instructions.

AI.tldr: DIALEVAL is an automated, type-theoretic evaluation framework that uses dual LLM agents to decompose instructions into typed predicates and apply type-specific satisfaction checks, achieving 90.38% accuracy and stronger alignment with human judgments, including in multi-turn dialogues.
AI.motivation: Manual, uniform-criteria evaluation of instruction following is labor-intensive and misaligned with how humans judge satisfaction, especially for complex, multi-requirement and conversational tasks. There is a need for automated, fine-grained, and type-sensitive assessment that scales and reflects human judgment patterns.
AI.method: A type-theoretic framework with two LLM agents: (1) extract typed, atomic, and independent predicates from instructions, enforcing formal atomicity and independence; (2) evaluate satisfaction with type-specific semantics (e.g., semantic equivalence for content predicates, exact precision for numerical predicates). The framework extends to multi-turn dialogues via history-aware satisfaction functions.
AI.result: On validation, DIALEVAL achieves 90.38% accuracy, a 26.45% error reduction over baselines, and significantly higher correlation with human judgments, particularly on complex instructions.
AI.conclusion: DIALEVAL provides scalable, reliable, and human-aligned evaluation of instruction following, outperforming single-turn and uniform-criteria methods and enabling robust assessment in conversational settings.

title: Escaping the BLEU Trap: A Signal-Grounded Framework with Decoupled Semantic Guidance for EEG-to-Text Decoding
summary: Decoding natural language from non-invasive EEG signals is a promising yet challenging task. However, current state-of-the-art models remain constrained by three fundamental limitations: Semantic Bias (mode collapse into generic templates), Signal Neglect (hallucination based on linguistic priors rather than neural inputs), and the BLEU Trap, where evaluation metrics are artificially inflated by high-frequency stopwords, masking a lack of true semantic fidelity. To address these challenges, we propose SemKey, a novel multi-stage framework that enforces signal-grounded generation through four decoupled semantic objectives: sentiment, topic, length, and surprisal. We redesign the interaction between the neural encoder and the Large Language Model (LLM) by injecting semantic prompts as Queries and EEG embeddings as Key-Value pairs, strictly forcing the model to attend to neural inputs. Furthermore, we move beyond standard translation metrics by adopting N-way Retrieval Accuracy and Fréchet Distance to rigorously assess diversity and alignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach effectively eliminates hallucinations on noise inputs and achieves SOTA performance on these robust protocols. Code will be released upon acceptance at https://github.com/xmed-lab/SemKey.

AI.tldr: SemKey grounds LLM-based EEG-to-language decoding by injecting semantic prompts as queries and EEG embeddings as key–value pairs, optimizing four decoupled objectives (sentiment, topic, length, surprisal), and evaluates with retrieval and Fréchet metrics—yielding SOTA and eliminating noise-induced hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Non-invasive EEG language decoding suffers from (1) semantic bias (generic template outputs), (2) signal neglect (LLM hallucinations driven by priors), and (3) misleading BLEU-based evaluation (stopword-driven inflation). The field needs architectures that force attention to neural signals and metrics that reflect semantic fidelity and diversity.
AI.method: A multi-stage framework (SemKey) with four decoupled semantic objectives—sentiment, topic, length, surprisal—guides generation. The interface to the LLM is redesigned: semantic prompts are injected as queries, while EEG embeddings serve as key–value pairs, structurally compelling the model to attend to neural inputs. Evaluation moves beyond translation scores to N-way retrieval accuracy and Fréchet distance to quantify alignment and diversity.
AI.result: Experiments show SemKey eliminates hallucinations on noise inputs and achieves state-of-the-art performance under the proposed robust evaluation protocols (N-way retrieval and Fréchet distance).
AI.conclusion: By enforcing signal-grounded conditioning and adopting stricter metrics, SemKey mitigates common pitfalls in EEG-to-text decoding, improving semantic fidelity and robustness; the approach sets a stronger evaluation standard and reports SOTA results, with code to be released.

title: QD-PCQA: Quality-Aware Domain Adaptation for Point Cloud Quality Assessment
summary: No-Reference Point Cloud Quality Assessment (NR-PCQA) still struggles with generalization, primarily due to the scarcity of annotated point cloud datasets. Since the Human Visual System (HVS) drives perceptual quality assessment independently of media types, prior knowledge on quality learned from images can be repurposed for point clouds. This insight motivates adopting Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) to transfer quality-relevant priors from labeled images to unlabeled point clouds. However, existing UDA-based PCQA methods often overlook key characteristics of perceptual quality, such as sensitivity to quality ranking and quality-aware feature alignment, thereby limiting their effectiveness. To address these issues, we propose a novel Quality-aware Domain adaptation framework for PCQA, termed QD-PCQA. The framework comprises two main components: i) a Rank-weighted Conditional Alignment (RCA) strategy that aligns features under consistent quality levels and adaptively emphasizes misranked samples to reinforce perceptual quality ranking awareness; and ii) a Quality-guided Feature Augmentation (QFA) strategy, which includes quality-guided style mixup, multi-layer extension, and dual-domain augmentation modules to augment perceptual feature alignment. Extensive cross-domain experiments demonstrate that QD-PCQA significantly improves generalization in NR-PCQA tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/huhu-code/QD-PCQA.
