AI.tldr: QD-PCQA is a UDA-based no-reference point cloud quality assessment framework that transfers quality priors from labeled images to unlabeled point clouds using rank-aware conditional alignment and quality-guided feature augmentation, yielding notably improved cross-domain generalization.
AI.motivation: NR-PCQA models generalize poorly due to scarce annotated point cloud data. Since human perceptual quality cues are shared across media types, quality knowledge learned from images can be repurposed for point clouds. Existing UDA-PCQA methods ignore sensitivity to quality rankings and lack quality-aware feature alignment, limiting transfer effectiveness.
AI.method: Use unsupervised domain adaptation from labeled images (source) to unlabeled point clouds (target). Introduce: (i) Rank-weighted Conditional Alignment (RCA) to align features conditioned on consistent quality levels and upweight misranked samples to enforce ranking awareness; (ii) Quality-guided Feature Augmentation (QFA) with quality-guided style mixup, multi-layer extension, and dual-domain augmentation to improve perceptual feature alignment across domains.
AI.result: Extensive cross-domain experiments show QD-PCQA significantly improves generalization in NR-PCQA compared to prior methods (exact metrics not provided in the abstract). Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Quality-aware alignment and augmentation enable effective transfer of image-based quality priors to point clouds, substantially enhancing NR-PCQA generalization. Ranking sensitivity and quality-guided feature design are key to the gains, indicating a promising direction for cross-modal quality assessment.

title: Can Large Language Models Derive New Knowledge? A Dynamic Benchmark for Biological Knowledge Discovery
summary: Recent advancements in Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable potential in automatic knowledge discovery. However, rigorously evaluating an AI's capacity for knowledge discovery remains a critical challenge. Existing benchmarks predominantly rely on static datasets, leading to inevitable data contamination where models have likely seen the evaluation knowledge during training. Furthermore, the rapid release cycles of modern LLMs render static benchmarks quickly outdated, failing to assess the ability to discover truly new knowledge. To address these limitations, we propose DBench-Bio, a dynamic and fully automated benchmark designed to evaluate AI's biological knowledge discovery ability. DBench-Bio employs a three-stage pipeline: (1) data acquisition of rigorous, authoritative paper abstracts; (2) QA extraction utilizing LLMs to synthesize scientific hypothesis questions and corresponding discovery answers; and (3) QA filter to ensure quality based on relevance, clarity, and centrality. We instantiate this pipeline to construct a monthly-updated benchmark covering 12 biomedical sub-domains. Extensive evaluations of SOTA models reveal current limitations in discovering new knowledge. Our work provides the first dynamic, automatic framework for assessing the new knowledge discovery capabilities of AI systems, establishing a living, evolving resource for AI research community to catalyze the development of knowledge discovery.

AI.tldr: DBench-Bio is a dynamic, fully automated, monthly-updated benchmark that evaluates LLMs’ ability to discover new biological knowledge by generating and filtering hypothesis-style QA pairs from fresh, authoritative paper abstracts; SOTA models currently underperform on it.
AI.motivation: Static, fixed benchmarks are prone to training-data contamination and quickly become outdated, making them unreliable for assessing whether LLMs can discover genuinely new knowledge, especially in fast-moving biomedical domains.
AI.method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) continuously ingest recent, authoritative biomedical paper abstracts; (2) use LLMs to synthesize hypothesis-driven questions with corresponding discovery answers; (3) apply a QA quality filter ensuring relevance, clarity, and centrality. The benchmark is instantiated to update monthly and spans 12 biomedical subfields.
AI.result: The authors build the benchmark and run extensive evaluations of state-of-the-art LLMs, finding notable limitations in their ability to discover or reason about newly emerging biological knowledge.
AI.conclusion: DBench-Bio is presented as the first dynamic, automated benchmark for new-knowledge discovery in AI, offering a living resource that evolves with the literature and aims to catalyze progress in AI systems capable of true knowledge discovery.

title: How does fine-tuning improve sensorimotor representations in large language models?
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit a significant "embodiment gap", where their text-based representations fail to align with human sensorimotor experiences. This study systematically investigates whether and how task-specific fine-tuning can bridge this gap. Utilizing Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) and dimension-specific correlation metrics, we demonstrate that the internal representations of LLMs can be steered toward more embodied, grounded patterns through fine-tuning. Furthermore, the results show that while sensorimotor improvements generalize robustly across languages and related sensory-motor dimensions, they are highly sensitive to the learning objective, failing to transfer across two disparate task formats.

AI.tldr: Fine-tuning can steer LLM internal representations toward more embodied, sensorimotor-grounded structure (per RSA), with gains that generalize across languages and related sensorimotor dimensions but fail to transfer across disparate task formats, highlighting the critical role of the learning objective.
AI.motivation: LLMs operate over text and lack alignment with human sensorimotor experience (“embodiment gap”). The study asks whether targeted fine-tuning can make LLM representations more grounded and whether such gains generalize across languages, dimensions, and tasks.
AI.method: Apply task-specific fine-tuning to LLMs and evaluate representational changes using Representational Similarity Analysis and dimension-specific correlation metrics. Assess generalization across languages and related sensorimotor dimensions, and test transfer across two different task formats to probe objective sensitivity.
AI.result: Fine-tuning shifts internal representations toward more embodied patterns. Improvements are robust across languages and among related sensorimotor dimensions but are highly sensitive to the training objective, showing little to no transfer across two divergent task formats.
AI.conclusion: Task-specific fine-tuning can partially bridge the embodiment gap by reshaping LLM representations, with benefits that generalize in some axes (language, related dimensions) but not across task formats. Effective grounding requires careful choice of objectives; broad task-transfer remains limited.

title: PROSPECT: Unified Streaming Vision-Language Navigation via Semantic--Spatial Fusion and Latent Predictive Representation
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have advanced zero-shot end-to-end Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), yet robust navigation requires not only semantic understanding but also predictive modeling of environment dynamics and spatial structure. We propose PROSPECT, a unified streaming navigation agent that couples a streaming Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policy with latent predictive representation learning. PROSPECT uses CUT3R as a streaming 3D foundation spatial encoder to produce long-context, absolute-scale spatial features, and fuses them with SigLIP semantic features via cross-attention. During training, we introduce learnable stream query tokens that query the streaming context and predict next-step 2D and 3D latent features (rather than pixels or explicit modalities), supervised in the latent spaces of frozen SigLIP and CUT3R teachers. The predictive branch shapes internal representations without inference overhead. Experiments on VLN-CE benchmarks and real-robot deployment demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and improved long-horizon robustness under diverse lighting. We will release code for the community soon.

AI.tldr: PROSPECT is a streaming Vision-Language-Action navigation agent that fuses a 3D spatial encoder (CUT3R) with semantic features (SigLIP) and adds a training-only latent predictive branch, achieving state-of-the-art VLN-CE and robust real-robot performance without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: While MLLMs enable zero-shot VLN, robust navigation also needs predictive modeling of environment dynamics and explicit 3D spatial structure—long-context, absolute-scale mapping, and robustness to lighting—capabilities that typical semantic-only policies lack.
AI.method: PROSPECT couples a streaming VLA policy with latent predictive representation learning. It uses CUT3R as a streaming 3D foundation encoder to produce long-context, absolute-scale spatial features, and fuses them with SigLIP semantic features via cross-attention. During training, learnable stream query tokens predict next-step 2D and 3D latent features, supervised in the frozen SigLIP and CUT3R latent spaces. The predictive branch shapes internal representations but is dropped at inference, adding no runtime overhead.
AI.result: Experiments on VLN-CE benchmarks and real-robot deployments show state-of-the-art performance and improved long-horizon robustness under diverse lighting conditions.
AI.conclusion: Unifying strong 3D spatial encoding with semantic fusion and latent predictive supervision yields a robust, streaming VLN agent that improves long-horizon performance without increasing inference cost; the approach transfers to real robots. Code will be released.

title: Discern Truth from Falsehood: Reducing Over-Refusal via Contrastive Refinement
summary: Large language models (LLMs) aligned for safety often suffer from over-refusal, the tendency to reject seemingly toxic or benign prompts by misclassifying them as toxic. This behavior undermines models' helpfulness and restricts usability in sensitive or nuanced contexts. While prior work has proposed mitigation strategies such as data augmentation and activation steering, these approaches often face a trade-off: reducing over-refusal typically degrades the model's ability to reject genuinely harmful content. We argue that this issue arises from the ambiguous influence of toxic and seemingly toxic prompts on the model's learning dynamics. To address it, we introduce a preceding alignment stage, DCR: Discernment via Contrastive Refinement. Both theoretically and empirically, we demonstrate that contrastive refinement improves an LLM's capacity to distinguish truly toxic prompts from superficially toxic ones. Evaluation across diverse benchmarks shows that our method effectively reduces over-refusal while preserving the safety benefits of alignment. Importantly, it achieves this with minimal degradation of general capabilities, offering a more principled and robust direction for safety alignment.

AI.tldr: They propose DCR, a contrastive pre-alignment stage that trains LLMs to distinguish truly toxic from superficially toxic prompts, cutting over-refusals while preserving safety and general capability.
AI.motivation: Safety-aligned LLMs often over-refuse by misclassifying benign or nuanced prompts as toxic, reducing helpfulness; existing fixes (e.g., data augmentation, activation steering) alleviate this but typically weaken genuine harm rejection.
AI.method: Discernment via Contrastive Refinement (DCR): a preceding alignment stage using contrastive learning to separate representations of truly toxic vs seemingly toxic/benign prompts, with theoretical grounding and empirical training to sharpen the model’s decision boundary before standard safety alignment.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, DCR reduces over-refusal rates without increasing harmful compliance, and shows minimal degradation of general capabilities compared to baselines and prior mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive refinement offers a principled, robust path for safety alignment—improving discernment between harmful and superficially harmful content, maintaining safety benefits, and avoiding the usual helpfulness–safety trade-off.

title: Towards Self-Robust LLMs: Intrinsic Prompt Noise Resistance via CoIPO
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable and steadily improving performance across a wide range of tasks. However, LLM performance may be highly sensitive to prompt variations especially in scenarios with limited openness or strict output formatting requirements, indicating insufficient robustness. In real-world applications, user prompts provided to LLMs often contain imperfections, which may undermine the quality of the model's responses. To address this issue, previous work has primarily focused on preprocessing prompts, employing external tools or even LLMs to refine prompt formulations in advance. However, these approaches overlook the intrinsic robustness of LLMs, and their reliance on external components introduces additional computational overhead and uncertainty. In this work, we propose a Contrastive Learning-based Inverse Direct Preference Optimization (CoIPO) method that minimizes the discrepancy between the label-aligned logits produced by the model under a clean prompt and its noisy counterpart, and conduct a detailed analysis using mutual information theory. We augment the FLAN dataset by constructing paired prompts, each consisting of a clean prompt and its corresponding noisy version for training. Additionally, to evaluate the effectiveness, we develop NoisyPromptBench, a benchmark enhanced and derived from the existing PromptBench. Experimental results conducted on NoisyPromptBench demonstrate that our proposed method achieves a significant improvement in average accuracy over the current state-of-the-art approaches. The source code of CoIPO, pair-wise FLAN datasets, and NoisyPromptBench have already been released on https://github.com/vegetable-yx/CoIPO.

AI.tldr: CoIPO trains LLMs to be intrinsically robust to noisy prompts by aligning their label-relevant logits between clean and noisy versions of the same prompt, yielding state-of-the-art accuracy on a new NoisyPromptBench.
AI.motivation: LLMs are brittle to prompt variations and strict formatting; real-world prompts are often imperfect. Prior fixes rely on external prompt-cleaning tools/LLMs, adding overhead and uncertainty rather than improving a model’s innate robustness.
AI.method: Contrastive Learning-based Inverse DPO: construct paired clean–noisy prompts (from FLAN), and minimize the discrepancy between their label-aligned logits so the model produces consistent, correct outputs under noise. Provide mutual-information analysis to justify the objective. Build NoisyPromptBench from PromptBench to evaluate robustness.
AI.result: Across NoisyPromptBench tasks, CoIPO delivers significant average accuracy gains over existing state-of-the-art robustness approaches. Code, paired FLAN data, and the benchmark are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Training with CoIPO yields intrinsic robustness to prompt noise without external preprocessing, improving reliability in constrained or noisy real-world settings and offering reusable data and evaluation resources.

title: DAGE: Dual-Stream Architecture for Efficient and Fine-Grained Geometry Estimation
summary: Estimating accurate, view-consistent geometry and camera poses from uncalibrated multi-view/video inputs remains challenging - especially at high spatial resolutions and over long sequences. We present DAGE, a dual-stream transformer whose main novelty is to disentangle global coherence from fine detail. A low-resolution stream operates on aggressively downsampled frames with alternating frame/global attention to build a view-consistent representation and estimate cameras efficiently, while a high-resolution stream processes the original images per-frame to preserve sharp boundaries and small structures. A lightweight adapter fuses these streams via cross-attention, injecting global context without disturbing the pretrained single-frame pathway. This design scales resolution and clip length independently, supports inputs up to 2K, and maintains practical inference cost. DAGE delivers sharp depth/pointmaps, strong cross-view consistency, and accurate poses, establishing new state-of-the-art results for video geometry estimation and multi-view reconstruction.

AI.tldr: DAGE is a dual‑stream transformer that separates global coherence (low‑res stream) from fine detail (high‑res stream) and fuses them via a lightweight cross‑attention adapter, yielding scalable, efficient, and state‑of‑the‑art geometry and camera pose estimation from uncalibrated multi‑view/video inputs.
AI.motivation: Accurately recovering view‑consistent geometry and camera poses from uncalibrated, high‑resolution, long video sequences is hard: methods often trade off global consistency against fine detail and become computationally prohibitive as resolution or clip length grows.
AI.method: Two coordinated streams: (1) a low‑resolution pathway operating on aggressively downsampled frames with alternating frame/global attention to build a view‑consistent representation and estimate camera poses efficiently; (2) a high‑resolution, per‑frame pathway that preserves sharp boundaries and small structures. A lightweight cross‑attention adapter injects global context from the low‑res stream into the high‑res pathway without disrupting a pretrained single‑frame model. This decouples scaling in resolution and clip length, supports up to 2K inputs, and keeps inference practical.
AI.result: Produces sharp depth/pointmaps, strong cross‑view consistency, and accurate poses, achieving new state‑of‑the‑art performance for video geometry estimation and multi‑view reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling global coherence from fine detail and fusing them with a minimal adapter enables scalable and efficient geometry/pose estimation on high‑resolution and long sequences, advancing the state of the art in multi‑view/video reconstruction.

title: Controlling Chat Style in Language Models via Single-Direction Editing
summary: Controlling stylistic attributes in large language models (LLMs) remains challenging, with existing approaches relying on either prompt engineering or post-training alignment. This paper investigates this challenge through the lens of representation engineering, testing the hypothesis that distinct stylistic attributes - from emotional tone to linguistic structure - are encoded as linear directions in the model's activation space. We provide strong empirical evidence for this hypothesis across a wide range of styles and, based on this finding, present a lightweight, training-free method for precise style control. Our approach supports linear style composition, enhances safety by ablating undesirable behaviors, and, as confirmed by experiments on over a dozen models, achieves high style adherence while preserving core capabilities at minimal computational cost.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that stylistic attributes (e.g., tone, structure) in LLMs correspond to approximately linear directions in activation space, enabling a lightweight, training-free method to steer style, compose styles, and ablate unsafe behaviors while preserving model capabilities across many models.
AI.motivation: Controlling style in LLM outputs is difficult with prompt engineering or post-training alignment, which can be brittle, costly, or imprecise. The authors seek a more principled, low-cost, and precise way to steer style and improve safety.
AI.method: Representation engineering: identify linear directions in the model’s activation space that correspond to stylistic attributes and manipulate activations at inference time. Leverage linear composition for combining styles and projection/ablation to suppress undesirable behaviors—without additional training.
AI.result: Empirical evidence across diverse styles and over a dozen models supports the linear-direction hypothesis. The proposed steering achieves high style adherence, allows linear style composition, enhances safety via ablation of unwanted traits, and maintains core capabilities with minimal compute overhead.
AI.conclusion: Stylistic features are linearly encoded in LLM activations and can be effectively controlled through training-free activation steering. This offers a practical, general, and efficient alternative to prompting or fine-tuning, enabling precise style control, composition, and safety improvements with little performance cost.

title: M-QUEST -- Meme Question-Understanding Evaluation on Semantics and Toxicity
summary: Internet memes are a powerful form of online communication, yet their nature and reliance on commonsense knowledge make toxicity detection challenging. Identifying key features for meme interpretation and understanding, is a crucial task. Previous work has been focused on some elements contributing to the meaning, such as the Textual dimension via OCR, the Visual dimension via object recognition, upper layers of meaning like the Emotional dimension, Toxicity detection via proxy variables, such as hate speech detection, and sentiment analysis. Nevertheless, there is still a lack of an overall architecture able to formally identify elements contributing to the meaning of a meme, and be used in the sense-making process. In this work, we present a semantic framework and a corresponding benchmark for automatic knowledge extraction from memes. First, we identify the necessary dimensions to understand and interpret a meme: Textual material, Visual material, Scene, Background Knowledge, Emotion, Semiotic Projection, Analogical Mapping, Overall Intent, Target Community, and Toxicity Assessment. Second, the framework guides a semi-automatic process of generating a benchmark with commonsense question-answer pairs about meme toxicity assessment and its underlying reason. The resulting benchmark M-QUEST consists of 609 question-answer pairs for 307 memes. Thirdly, we evaluate eight open-source large language models on their ability to correctly solve M-QUEST. Our results show that current models' commonsense reasoning capabilities for toxic meme interpretation vary depending on the dimension and architecture. Models with instruction tuning and reasoning capabilities significantly outperform the others, though pragmatic inference questions remain challenging. We release code, benchmark, and prompts to support future research intersecting multimodal content safety and commonsense reasoning.

AI.tldr: They introduce a semantic framework with 10 meaning-bearing dimensions for interpreting memes and build M-QUEST, a QA benchmark (609 pairs over 307 memes) targeting toxicity reasoning. Evaluating 8 open-source LLMs shows instruction-tuned/reasoning models perform better, but pragmatic inference remains difficult. Resources are released to foster research on multimodal safety and commonsense reasoning.
AI.motivation: Toxic memes are hard to detect because meaning depends on multimodal cues and commonsense/pragmatic knowledge. Prior work targets isolated elements (OCR text, objects, sentiment/hate proxies) without a unified architecture for sense-making. A structured framework and evaluative benchmark are needed to systematically capture the elements that drive meme meaning and toxicity.
AI.method: 1) Define a semantic framework comprising 10 dimensions: Textual material, Visual material, Scene, Background Knowledge, Emotion, Semiotic Projection, Analogical Mapping, Overall Intent, Target Community, and Toxicity Assessment. 2) Use the framework to semi-automatically generate commonsense QA pairs that probe toxicity and the underlying reasons. 3) Compile the M-QUEST benchmark with 609 QA pairs for 307 memes. 4) Evaluate eight open-source LLMs on these questions, analyzing performance by dimension and model architecture (e.g., instruction tuning, reasoning capabilities).
AI.result: Performance differs across both dimensions and model types. Models with instruction tuning and explicit reasoning capabilities substantially outperform others. However, questions requiring pragmatic inference remain challenging for all models.
AI.conclusion: A unified semantic framework and the M-QUEST benchmark expose key gaps in LLMs’ commonsense/pragmatic reasoning for toxic meme interpretation. Public release of code, data, and prompts aims to catalyze progress in multimodal content safety; advancing pragmatic inference is a priority for future model development.

title: WSI-INR: Implicit Neural Representations for Lesion Segmentation in Whole-Slide Images
summary: Whole-slide images (WSIs) are fundamental for computational pathology, where accurate lesion segmentation is critical for clinical decision making. Existing methods partition WSIs into discrete patches, disrupting spatial continuity and treating multi-resolution views as independent samples, which leads to spatially fragmented segmentation and reduced robustness to resolution variations. To address the issues, we propose WSI-INR, a novel patch-free framework based on Implicit Neural Representations (INRs). WSI-INR models the WSI as a continuous implicit function mapping spatial coordinates directly to tissue semantics features, outputting segmentation results while preserving intrinsic spatial information across the entire slide. In the WSI-INR, we incorporate multi-resolution hash grid encoding to regard different resolution levels as varying sampling densities of the same continuous tissue, achieving a consistent feature representation across resolutions. In addition, by jointly training a shared INR decoder, WSI-INR can capture general priors across different cases. Experimental results showed that WSI-INR maintains robust segmentation performance across resolutions; at Base/4, our resolution-specific optimization improves Dice score by +26.11%, while U-Net and TransUNet decrease by 54.28% and 36.18%, respectively. Crucially, this work enables INRs to segment highly heterogeneous pathological lesions beyond structurally consistent anatomical tissues, offering a fresh perspective for pathological analysis.

AI.tldr: WSI-INR is a patch-free, coordinate-based segmentation framework for whole-slide images that uses implicit neural representations with multi-resolution hash grids and a shared decoder to preserve spatial continuity and remain robust across resolutions, substantially outperforming U-Net/TransUNet at lower resolutions.
AI.motivation: Patch-based WSI segmentation disrupts spatial continuity and treats multiple resolutions as independent, causing fragmented masks and poor robustness to resolution changes. The goal is to build a resolution-consistent, spatially continuous model for WSIs.
AI.method: Model a WSI as a continuous implicit function that maps spatial coordinates to tissue-semantic features and segmentation. Use multi-resolution hash grid encoding so different pyramid levels are viewed as different sampling densities of the same tissue, enabling consistent features across scales. Jointly train a shared INR decoder across cases to capture general priors, while enabling slide-specific optimization.
AI.result: Robust cross-resolution performance; at Base/4 resolution, a resolution-specific optimization of WSI-INR improves Dice by +26.11%, whereas U-Net and TransUNet drop by 54.28% and 36.18%, respectively. Capable of segmenting heterogeneous pathological lesions beyond anatomically consistent tissues.
AI.conclusion: Implicit, coordinate-based modeling can deliver continuous, resolution-consistent WSI segmentation with strong robustness to downscaling, offering a new direction for computational pathology beyond patch-based approaches.

title: IntPro: A Proxy Agent for Context-Aware Intent Understanding via Retrieval-conditioned Inference
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have become integral to modern Human-AI collaboration workflows, where accurately understanding user intent serves as a crucial step for generating satisfactory responses. Context-aware intent understanding, which involves inferring user intentions from situational environments, is inherently challenging because it requires reasoning over both the immediate context and the user's underlying motivations that drive their behavior. Moreover, existing approaches often treat intent understanding as a static recognition task, overlooking users' accumulated intent patterns that could provide valuable references for more accurate and generalizable understanding. To address this gap, we propose IntPro, a proxy agent that learns to adapt to individual users via retrieval-conditioned intent inference. We design intent explanations that abstract how contextual signals connect to expressed intents, and store them in an individual intent history library for retrieval. We train IntPro through supervised fine-tuning on retrieval-conditioned trajectories and multi-turn Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with tool-aware reward functions, enabling the agent to learn when to leverage historical intent patterns and when to infer directly. Experiments across three diverse scenarios (Highlight-Intent, MIntRec2.0, and Weibo Post-Sync) demonstrate that IntPro achieves strong intent understanding performance with effective context-aware reasoning capabilities across different scenarios and model types.

AI.tldr: IntPro is a retrieval-conditioned proxy agent that adapts to individual users by leveraging per-user intent histories and policy optimization, enabling stronger context-aware intent understanding across tasks.
AI.motivation: Context-aware intent understanding with LLMs is hard because it requires reasoning over both immediate context and latent user motivations. Existing methods treat intent recognition as static and ignore users’ accumulated intent patterns, limiting accuracy and generalization.
AI.method: Design per-user “intent explanations” that link contextual cues to expressed intents and store them in an intent history library for retrieval. Train a proxy agent (IntPro) with supervised fine-tuning on retrieval-conditioned trajectories and multi-turn Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using tool-aware rewards so the agent learns when to exploit historical patterns versus infer from current context.
AI.result: Across three scenarios—Highlight-Intent, MIntRec2.0, and Weibo Post-Sync—IntPro shows strong performance and effective context-aware reasoning, robust across different scenarios and base model types.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging retrieval of per-user intent explanations plus GRPO training enables personalized, context-aware intent inference that generalizes across tasks and models, improving intent understanding over static approaches.

title: The Influence of Iconicity in Transfer Learning for Sign Language Recognition
summary: Most sign language recognition research relies on Transfer Learning (TL) from vision-based datasets such as ImageNet. Some extend this to alternatively available language datasets, often focusing on signs with cross-linguistic similarities. This body of work examines the necessity of these likenesses on effective knowledge transfer by comparing TL performance between iconic signs of two different sign language pairs: Chinese to Arabic and Greek to Flemish. Google Mediapipe was utilised as an input feature extractor, enabling spatial information of these signs to be processed with a Multilayer Perceptron architecture and the temporal information with a Gated Recurrent Unit. Experimental results showed a 7.02% improvement for Arabic and 1.07% for Flemish when conducting iconic TL from Chinese and Greek respectively.

AI.tldr: Tests whether cross-linguistic iconic similarity aids transfer learning for sign language recognition using Mediapipe features with an MLP (spatial) + GRU (temporal); finds modest gains transferring Chinese→Arabic (+7.02%) and Greek→Flemish (+1.07%).
AI.motivation: Conventional SLR transfer learning from vision datasets (e.g., ImageNet) may not capture linguistic/gestural properties of signs. If signs share iconic form across languages, leveraging source-language sign data might transfer better to a target language. The paper asks whether such cross-linguistic likeness is necessary or beneficial for effective knowledge transfer.
AI.method: Use Google Mediapipe to extract pose/hand/face landmarks, process spatial features with a Multilayer Perceptron and temporal dynamics with a Gated Recurrent Unit. Compare performance on target languages when applying transfer learning from source languages containing iconic counterparts (Chinese→Arabic; Greek→Flemish). Baseline presumably trained without such transfer; metrics not specified in abstract.
AI.result: Iconic transfer yields +7.02% improvement for Arabic when transferred from Chinese and +1.07% for Flemish when transferred from Greek, relative to the baseline without such transfer.
AI.conclusion: Cross-linguistic iconicity can help transfer learning in SLR, but the effect size varies by language pair and is sometimes modest. This suggests potential benefits of linguistically informed transfer, while highlighting the need for broader evaluation, clearer baselines/metrics, and analysis of when and why iconicity yields larger gains.

title: Seeing as Experts Do: A Knowledge-Augmented Agent for Open-Set Fine-Grained Visual Understanding
summary: Fine-grained visual understanding is shifting from static classification to knowledge-augmented reasoning, where models must justify as well as recognise. Existing approaches remain limited by closed-set taxonomies and single-label prediction, leading to significant degradation under open-set or context-dependent conditions. We present the Knowledge-Augmented Fine-Grained Reasoning Agent (KFRA), a unified framework that transforms fine-grained perception into evidence-driven reasoning. KFRA operates through a three-stage closed reasoning loop that emulates expert analysis. It first performs open-vocabulary detection and web-scale retrieval to generate category hypotheses. It then conducts discriminative regions localisation by aligning textual knowledge with visual evidence through a global-to-local focusing mechanism. Finally, it integrates all multimodal evidence within a large multimodal model to perform interpretable reasoning. Unlike existing agents that treat retrieval and reasoning as independent processes, KFRA establishes a retrieval-grounding coupling that converts retrieved knowledge into spatially grounded evidence for verification. This design enables factual, interpretable, and task-agnostic reasoning across diverse fine-grained scenarios. To evaluate this capability, we construct FGExpertBench, a benchmark designed to assess reasoning depth and cross-task generalisation across six knowledge dimensions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that KFRA consistently surpasses both standalone large multimodal models and current agent frameworks, achieving up to 19 percent improvement in reasoning accuracy and delivering evidence-grounded interpretability in open-set fine-grained visual understanding.

AI.tldr: KFRA is an agentic, knowledge-augmented framework that couples web-scale retrieval with spatial grounding and LMM reasoning to deliver interpretable, open-set fine-grained recognition. It introduces FGExpertBench and reports up to 19% higher reasoning accuracy over strong LMM and agent baselines.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained visual understanding increasingly needs reasoning and justification, not just closed-set classification. Existing methods rely on fixed taxonomies and single-label outputs, failing under open-set and context-dependent scenarios and lacking grounded interpretability.
AI.method: A three-stage closed reasoning loop: (1) open-vocabulary detection plus web-scale retrieval to form category hypotheses; (2) discriminative region localization by aligning retrieved textual knowledge with visual evidence via a global-to-local focusing mechanism; (3) integration of all grounded multimodal evidence in a large multimodal model for interpretable reasoning. Core design: tightly coupling retrieval and grounding so retrieved knowledge is verified against spatial evidence.
AI.result: New benchmark FGExpertBench evaluates reasoning depth and cross-task generalization across six knowledge dimensions. Across diverse fine-grained tasks, KFRA outperforms standalone LMMs and prior agent frameworks, achieving up to 19% improvement in reasoning accuracy while producing evidence-grounded explanations.
AI.conclusion: Coupling retrieval with spatial grounding and LMM reasoning yields factual, interpretable, and task-agnostic fine-grained understanding under open-set conditions. KFRA advances evidence-driven reasoning and FGExpertBench provides a comprehensive evaluation suite.

title: Controllable and explainable personality sliders for LLMs at inference time
summary: Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with specific personas typically relies on expensive and monolithic Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) or RLHF. While effective, these methods require training distinct models for every target personality profile. Inference-time activation steering offers a parameter-efficient alternative, yet naive approaches fail to control multiple traits simultaneously due to destructive vector interference. In this work, we propose a modular framework for continuous, multi-dimensional personality control. Our key innovation is Sequential Adaptive Steering (SAS): a method that orthogonalizes steering vectors by training subsequent probes on the residual stream shifted by prior interventions. This approach transforms steering vectors into reusable primitives, allowing users to instantly synthesize complex, high-fidelity personality profiles by simply adjusting coefficients alpha. We validate our framework on the Big Five personality traits, demonstrating that it outperforms naive baselines in both goal adherence and coherence, enabling precise, holistic personality modulation without updating model parameters.

AI.tldr: SAS orthogonalizes activation-steering vectors to enable modular, continuous, multi-trait persona control in LLMs at inference time, outperforming naive steering on Big Five traits without fine-tuning or RLHF.
AI.motivation: SFT/RLHF for persona alignment is costly and inflexible, requiring separate models per profile. Inference-time steering is parameter-efficient but naive multi-trait control suffers from destructive interference between steering vectors, limiting reliability and fidelity.
AI.method: Introduce Sequential Adaptive Steering (SAS): learn steering probes sequentially on the residual stream after applying earlier interventions, effectively orthogonalizing vectors. This yields reusable, modular primitives that can be linearly combined via user-set coefficients (alpha) to synthesize complex, continuous personality profiles—without updating base model weights.
AI.result: On Big Five personality control tasks, SAS improves goal adherence and response coherence versus naive activation-steering baselines, enabling precise, holistic modulation across multiple traits at once. All results are achieved with zero model parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: SAS provides a scalable, parameter-efficient framework for high-fidelity, multi-dimensional persona alignment, mitigating vector interference and allowing instant composition of desired traits. It makes personality control practical without training distinct models.

title: Quantum-Inspired Self-Attention in a Large Language Model
summary: Recent advances in Natural Language Processing have been predominantly driven by transformer-based architectures, which rely heavily on self-attention mechanisms to model relationships between tokens in a sequence. Similarly, the field of Quantum Natural Language Processing, which seeks to leverage quantum principles to address challenges in language understanding and generation tasks, has seen the recent development of quantum self-attention mechanisms. We propose a classical quantum-inspired self-attention (QISA) mechanism and integrate it into the full autoregressive language modeling pipeline of GPT-1. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first integration of this kind, as previous quantum self-attention mechanisms have been primarily tested on text classification. In our experiments, QISA achieves better performance when compared to standard self-attention on the metrics character error rate ($15.5\times$ better), word error rate ($4.7 \times $) and cross-entropy loss ($13 \times$). This is achieved while only requiring a $ 2.6\times$ longer inference time.

AI.tldr: They replace standard self-attention in a GPT‑1–style autoregressive LM with a classical, quantum‑inspired self‑attention (QISA) and report large accuracy/loss gains at the cost of ~2.6× slower inference.
AI.motivation: Transformer performance hinges on self‑attention; quantum NLP proposes alternative mechanisms but prior quantum self‑attention was evaluated mostly on classification. The authors aim to bring quantum‑inspired attention into full language modeling to potentially gain better inductive biases and performance.
AI.method: Design a classical quantum‑inspired self‑attention module (QISA), integrate it as a drop‑in replacement for standard attention within the GPT‑1 autoregressive pipeline, and evaluate against a standard baseline on language modeling metrics (character error rate, word error rate, cross‑entropy) while tracking inference latency.
AI.result: QISA outperforms standard self‑attention with reported multiplicative improvements: character error rate 15.5× better, word error rate 4.7× better, and cross‑entropy loss 13× better, incurring a 2.6× increase in inference time.
AI.conclusion: Quantum‑inspired self‑attention can be effectively integrated into autoregressive LMs and may yield substantial quality gains with a moderate runtime penalty, marking the first such demonstration beyond classification and motivating further exploration and optimization.

title: LiDAR Prompted Spatio-Temporal Multi-View Stereo for Autonomous Driving
summary:
Accurate metric depth is critical for autonomous driving perception and simulation, yet current approaches struggle to achieve high metric accuracy, multi-view and temporal consistency, and cross-domain generalization. To address these challenges, we present DriveMVS, a novel multi-view stereo framework that reconciles these competing objectives through two key insights:
  (1) Sparse but metrically accurate LiDAR observations can serve as geometric prompts to anchor depth estimation in absolute scale, and (2) deep fusion of diverse cues is essential for resolving ambiguities and enhancing robustness, while a spatio-temporal decoder ensures consistency across frames. Built upon these principles, DriveMVS embeds the LiDAR prompt in two ways: as a hard geometric prior that anchors the cost volume, and as soft feature-wise guidance fused by a triple-cue combiner. Regarding temporal consistency, DriveMVS employs a spatio-temporal decoder that jointly leverages geometric cues from the MVS cost volume and temporal context from neighboring frames. Experiments show that DriveMVS achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks, excelling in metric accuracy, temporal stability, and zero-shot cross-domain transfer, demonstrating its practical value for scalable, reliable autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: DriveMVS anchors multi-view stereo depth to absolute scale using sparse LiDAR prompts and fuses diverse visual–geometric cues with a spatio-temporal decoder, yielding state-of-the-art metric accuracy, temporal consistency, and cross-domain robustness for autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Depth estimation for autonomous driving needs absolute metric scale, frame-to-frame and multi-view consistency, and strong cross-domain generalization—requirements that existing MVS/monocular systems struggle to satisfy simultaneously.
AI.method: Use sparse but accurate LiDAR as geometric prompts in two ways: (1) a hard prior to anchor the MVS cost volume to true scale; (2) soft, feature-level guidance via a triple-cue combiner for deep fusion of diverse cues. A spatio-temporal decoder then aggregates geometric cues from the MVS cost volume with temporal context from neighboring frames to enforce consistency over time.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks, DriveMVS achieves state-of-the-art performance, with improved metric accuracy, temporal stability, and zero-shot cross-domain transfer compared to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Prompting MVS with sparse LiDAR and deeply fusing multi-cue, spatio-temporal information reconciles accuracy, consistency, and generalization, making DriveMVS practical for scalable, reliable autonomous driving perception and simulation.

title: A benchmark for joint dialogue satisfaction, emotion recognition, and emotion state transition prediction
summary: User satisfaction is closely related to enterprises, as it not only directly reflects users' subjective evaluation of service quality or products, but also affects customer loyalty and long-term business revenue. Monitoring and understanding user emotions during interactions helps predict and improve satisfaction. However, relevant Chinese datasets are limited, and user emotions are dynamic; relying on single-turn dialogue cannot fully track emotional changes across multiple turns, which may affect satisfaction prediction. To address this, we constructed a multi-task, multi-label Chinese dialogue dataset that supports satisfaction recognition, as well as emotion recognition and emotional state transition prediction, providing new resources for studying emotion and satisfaction in dialogue systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Chinese multi-turn dialogue dataset annotated for satisfaction recognition, emotion recognition, and emotion state transition prediction to better capture dynamic user emotions for satisfaction modeling.
AI.motivation: User satisfaction drives loyalty and revenue. Emotion monitoring during interactions aids satisfaction prediction, but Chinese resources are scarce and single-turn analyses miss cross-turn emotion dynamics.
AI.method: Construct a multi-task, multi-label Chinese dialogue dataset with annotations for user satisfaction, per-turn emotions, and emotion state transitions across multiple turns, enabling multi-task learning and temporal emotion modeling.
AI.result: A new dataset resource supporting three tasks is provided; the abstract does not report quantitative benchmarks or model performance.
AI.conclusion: The dataset enables research on jointly modeling emotion dynamics and satisfaction in Chinese dialogue systems, potentially improving satisfaction prediction and monitoring over multi-turn interactions.

title: DMD-augmented Unpaired Neural Schrödinger Bridge for Ultra-Low Field MRI Enhancement
summary: Ultra Low Field (64 mT) brain MRI improves accessibility but suffers from reduced image quality compared to 3 T. As paired 64 mT - 3 T scans are scarce, we propose an unpaired 64 mT $\rightarrow$ 3 T translation framework that enhances realism while preserving anatomy. Our method builds upon the Unpaired Neural Schrödinge Bridge (UNSB) with multi-step refinement. To strengthen target distribution alignment, we augment the adversarial objective with DMD2-style diffusion-guided distribution matching using a frozen 3T diffusion teacher. To explicitly constrain global structure beyond patch-level correspondence, we combine PatchNCE with an Anatomical Structure Preservation (ASP) regularizer that enforces soft foreground background consistency and boundary aware constraints. Evaluated on two disjoint cohorts, the proposed framework achieves an improved realism structure trade-off, enhancing distribution level realism on unpaired benchmarks while increasing structural fidelity on the paired cohort compared to unpaired baselines.

AI.tldr: Unpaired 64 mT→3 T brain MRI translation that fuses UNSB with diffusion-teacher–guided distribution matching and anatomy-preserving regularization, yielding more 3T-like realism without sacrificing structure across two cohorts.
AI.motivation: Ultra–low-field (64 mT) MRI is accessible but has inferior image quality; paired 64 mT/3 T datasets are scarce. There is a need for unpaired translation that increases 3 T–like realism while strictly preserving anatomy.
AI.method: Builds on Unpaired Neural Schrödinger Bridge (UNSB) with multi-step refinement. Strengthens target distribution alignment by augmenting the adversarial objective with DMD2-style diffusion-guided distribution matching using a frozen 3 T diffusion teacher. Preserves global anatomy by combining PatchNCE with an Anatomical Structure Preservation (ASP) regularizer enforcing soft foreground–background consistency and boundary-aware constraints.
AI.result: Across two disjoint cohorts, the framework improves the realism–structure trade-off: better distribution-level realism on unpaired benchmarks and increased structural fidelity on a paired cohort compared with unpaired baselines.
AI.conclusion: Teacher-guided diffusion matching plus explicit anatomy-preserving constraints enables effective unpaired 64 mT→3 T translation, improving perceived 3 T realism while maintaining anatomical integrity, and shows promise for enhancing low-field MRI utility.

title: StructLens: A Structural Lens for Language Models via Maximum Spanning Trees
summary: Language exhibits inherent structures, a property that explains both language acquisition and language change. Given this characteristic, we expect language models to manifest internal structures as well. While interpretability research has investigated the components of language models, existing approaches focus on local inter-token relationships within layers or modules (e.g., Multi-Head Attention), leaving global inter-layer relationships largely overlooked. To address this gap, we introduce StructLens, an analytical framework designed to reveal how internal structures relate holistically through their inter-token connection within a layer. StructLens constructs maximum spanning trees based on the semantic representations in residual streams, analogous to dependency parsing, and leverages the tree properties to quantify inter-layer distance (or similarity) from a structural perspective. Our findings demonstrate that StructLens yields an inter-layer similarity pattern that is distinctively different from conventional cosine similarity. Moreover, this structure-aware similarity proves to be beneficial for practical tasks, such as layer pruning, highlighting the effectiveness of structural analysis for understanding and optimizing language models. Our code is available at https://github.com/naist-nlp/structlens.

AI.tldr: StructLens builds maximum spanning trees from residual-stream token representations to define a structure-aware inter-layer similarity, revealing patterns unlike cosine similarity and enabling practical gains such as better layer pruning.
AI.motivation: Language and language models possess inherent structure, yet most interpretability work examines local token interactions (e.g., within attention) and neglects global inter-layer relationships. A holistic, structure-centric view may better explain model organization and aid optimization.
AI.method: For each layer, compute token-level similarities from residual stream representations and construct a maximum spanning tree (dependency-parse–like). Derive distances/similarities between layers from properties of these trees to quantify how internal structures relate across layers; compare against conventional cosine-based measures and apply to pruning decisions.
AI.result: The structure-aware inter-layer similarity exhibits patterns that differ markedly from cosine similarity and yields practical benefits, notably improving layer pruning performance. Code is released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Structural analysis via tree-based views of residual streams provides complementary insight into how layers relate and can guide optimization, suggesting that holistic structural signals matter beyond standard vector-similarity metrics.

title: Small Object Detection in Complex Backgrounds with Multi-Scale Attention and Global Relation Modeling
summary: Small object detection under complex backgrounds remains a challenging task due to severe feature degradation, weak semantic representation, and inaccurate localization caused by downsampling operations and background interference. Existing detection frameworks are mainly designed for general objects and often fail to explicitly address the unique characteristics of small objects, such as limited structural cues and strong sensitivity to localization errors. In this paper, we propose a multi-level feature enhancement and global relation modeling framework tailored for small object detection. Specifically, a Residual Haar Wavelet Downsampling module is introduced to preserve fine-grained structural details by jointly exploiting spatial-domain convolutional features and frequency-domain representations. To enhance global semantic awareness and suppress background noise, a Global Relation Modeling module is employed to capture long-range dependencies at high-level feature stages. Furthermore, a Cross-Scale Hybrid Attention module is designed to establish sparse and aligned interactions across multi-scale features, enabling effective fusion of high-resolution details and high-level semantic information with reduced computational overhead. Finally, a Center-Assisted Loss is incorporated to stabilize training and improve localization accuracy for small objects. Extensive experiments conducted on the large-scale RGBT-Tiny benchmark demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art detectors under both IoU-based and scale-adaptive evaluation metrics. These results validate the effectiveness and robustness of the proposed framework for small object detection in complex environments.

AI.tldr: A small-object–focused detection framework combining wavelet-preserving downsampling, global relation modeling, cross-scale hybrid attention, and a center-assisted loss achieves state-of-the-art results on RGBT-Tiny under IoU and scale-adaptive metrics.
AI.motivation: Small objects suffer from feature loss during downsampling, weak semantics, and high localization sensitivity, especially amid complex backgrounds; general detectors don’t explicitly address these small-object characteristics.
AI.method: Introduce (1) Residual Haar Wavelet Downsampling to retain fine-grained structural cues by fusing spatial convolutional and frequency (Haar) features; (2) Global Relation Modeling to capture long-range dependencies at high-level stages, boosting semantics and suppressing background noise; (3) Cross-Scale Hybrid Attention to align and sparsify interactions across feature scales for efficient detail–semantics fusion; (4) Center-Assisted Loss to stabilize training and improve localization of small objects.
AI.result: On the large-scale RGBT-Tiny benchmark, the method consistently surpasses state-of-the-art detectors across IoU-based and scale-adaptive evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Multi-level feature enhancement plus global context modeling specifically tailored to small objects delivers effective and robust detection in complex environments.

title: AutoHarness: improving LLM agents by automatically synthesizing a code harness
comment: agent harness, code synthesis, self-improvement, code-as-policy, text games
summary: Despite significant strides in language models in the last few years, when used as agents, such models often try to perform actions that are not just suboptimal for a given state, but are strictly prohibited by the external environment. For example, in the recent Kaggle GameArena chess competition, 78% of Gemini-2.5-Flash losses were attributed to illegal moves. Often people manually write "harnesses" around LLMs to prevent such failures. In this paper, we demonstrate that Gemini-2.5-Flash can automatically synthesize such a code harness, using a small number of rounds of iterative code refinement given feedback from the (game) environment. The resulting harness prevents all illegal moves in 145 different TextArena games (both 1-player and 2-player), enabling the smaller Gemini-2.5-Flash model to outperform larger models, such as Gemini-2.5-Pro. Pushing our technique to the limit, we can get Gemini-2.5-Flash to generate the entire policy in code, thus eliminating the need to use the LLM at decision making time. The resulting code-policy receives a higher average reward than Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5.2-High on 16 TextArena 1-player games. Our results show that using a smaller model to synthesize a custom code harness (or entire policy) can outperform a much larger model, while also being more cost effective.

AI.tldr: Small LLMs can synthesize code harnesses (and even full policies) via environment-in-the-loop refinement to prevent illegal actions, yielding higher rewards than larger models on many TextArena games at lower cost.
AI.motivation: LLM agents frequently attempt invalid or prohibited actions (e.g., illegal chess moves), leading to failures; hand-written safety harnesses are labor-intensive and brittle. The authors aim to automate harness creation—and potentially the entire decision policy—to improve safety, performance, and cost-efficiency.
AI.method: Use Gemini-2.5-Flash in few iterative rounds to generate and refine code with feedback from the game environment. First synthesize a legality-enforcing harness for diverse TextArena games (1- and 2-player). Then push further to synthesize a complete policy in code, removing the LLM from inference-time decision making.
AI.result: The synthesized harness blocks all illegal moves across 145 TextArena games, letting Gemini-2.5-Flash outperform larger models like Gemini-2.5-Pro. Fully synthesized code-policies achieve higher average reward than Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5.2-High on 16 single-player TextArena games.
AI.conclusion: Environment-guided code synthesis by a smaller LLM can tailor constraints and policies to specific tasks, eliminating illegal actions and surpassing larger models while reducing cost; custom harnesses or full code policies can be a practical path to safer, stronger LLM agents.

title: TAP: A Token-Adaptive Predictor Framework for Training-Free Diffusion Acceleration
summary: Diffusion models achieve strong generative performance but remain slow at inference due to the need for repeated full-model denoising passes. We present Token-Adaptive Predictor (TAP), a training-free, probe-driven framework that adaptively selects a predictor for each token at every sampling step. TAP uses a single full evaluation of the model's first layer as a low-cost probe to compute proxy losses for a compact family of candidate predictors (instantiated primarily with Taylor expansions of varying order and horizon), then assigns each token the predictor with the smallest proxy error. This per-token "probe-then-select" strategy exploits heterogeneous temporal dynamics, requires no additional training, and is compatible with various predictor designs. TAP incurs negligible overhead while enabling large speedups with little or no perceptual quality loss. Extensive experiments across multiple diffusion architectures and generation tasks show that TAP substantially improves the accuracy-efficiency frontier compared to fixed global predictors and caching-only baselines.

AI.tldr: TAP is a training‑free, per‑token adaptive predictor selection scheme for diffusion sampling that uses a cheap first‑layer probe to choose among lightweight predictors (e.g., Taylor expansions), yielding large speedups with little or no quality loss across architectures and tasks.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models are slow at inference because they require many full denoising passes. Existing accelerations often use fixed, global predictors that ignore token‑wise temporal heterogeneity, leaving efficiency on the table without retraining.
AI.method: At each sampling step, run a single full evaluation of the model’s first layer as a low‑cost probe. Use it to compute proxy losses for a compact family of candidate predictors (primarily Taylor expansions with varying order/horizon). For each token, select the predictor with the smallest proxy error (“probe‑then‑select”). This is training‑free, per‑token, and compatible with diverse predictor designs, adding negligible overhead.
AI.result: Across multiple diffusion architectures and generation tasks, TAP achieves substantial speedups with minimal or no perceptual degradation and improves the accuracy‑efficiency frontier compared to fixed global predictors and caching‑only baselines.
AI.conclusion: A probe‑driven, per‑token adaptive selection mechanism can effectively exploit heterogeneous temporal dynamics in diffusion models, delivering significant sampling acceleration without extra training and with limited overhead, generalizing across models and tasks.

title: Certainty robustness: Evaluating LLM stability under self-challenging prompts
comment: 20 pages, 7 tables Benchmark and evaluation study of large language models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often present answers with high apparent confidence despite lacking an explicit mechanism for reasoning about certainty or truth. While existing benchmarks primarily evaluate single-turn accuracy, truthfulness or confidence calibration, they do not capture how models behave when their responses are challenged in interactive settings. We introduce the Certainty Robustness Benchmark, a two-turn evaluation framework that measures how LLMs balance stability and adaptability under self-challenging prompts such as uncertainty ("Are you sure?") and explicit contradiction ("You are wrong!"), alongside numeric confidence elicitation. Using 200 reasoning and mathematics questions from LiveBench, we evaluate four state-of-the-art LLMs and distinguish between justified self-corrections and unjustified answer changes. Our results reveal substantial differences in interactive reliability that are not explained by baseline accuracy alone: some models abandon correct answers under conversational pressure, while others demonstrate strong resistance to challenge and better alignment between confidence and correctness. These findings identify certainty robustness as a distinct and critical dimension of LLM evaluation, with important implications for alignment, trustworthiness and real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Introduces a two-turn “Certainty Robustness Benchmark” that tests how LLMs handle being challenged (“Are you sure?” / “You are wrong!”) and report confidence, revealing interactive reliability differences not captured by single-turn accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs often sound confident without explicit uncertainty reasoning, and current benchmarks focus on single-turn accuracy/calibration, missing how models react under conversational pressure. Real deployments require balancing stability (holding to correct answers) and adaptability (correcting mistakes) when challenged.
AI.method: Create a two-turn evaluation with self-challenging prompts (uncertainty and explicit contradiction) plus numeric confidence elicitation. Use 200 reasoning/math questions from LiveBench, evaluate four SOTA LLMs, and label second-turn changes as justified (correct corrections) or unjustified (abandoning correct answers). Analyze resistance to challenge and confidence–correctness alignment.
AI.result: Models differ substantially in certainty robustness beyond what baseline accuracy predicts: some unjustifiably abandon correct answers when challenged, while others maintain correct answers and show better confidence–correctness alignment.
AI.conclusion: Certainty robustness is a distinct, critical evaluation axis. Interactive challenge testing should complement standard accuracy/calibration to improve alignment, trustworthiness, and deployment decisions.

title: Separators in Enhancing Autoregressive Pretraining for Vision Mamba
summary: The state space model Mamba has recently emerged as a promising paradigm in computer vision, attracting significant attention due to its efficient processing of long sequence tasks. Mamba's inherent causal mechanism renders it particularly suitable for autoregressive pretraining. However, current autoregressive pretraining methods are constrained to short sequence tasks, failing to fully exploit Mamba's prowess in handling extended sequences. To address this limitation, we introduce an innovative autoregressive pretraining method for Vision Mamba that substantially extends the input sequence length. We introduce new \textbf{S}epara\textbf{T}ors for \textbf{A}uto\textbf{R}egressive pretraining to demarcate and differentiate between different images, known as \textbf{STAR}. Specifically, we insert identical separators before each image to demarcate its inception. This strategy enables us to quadruple the input sequence length of Vision Mamba while preserving the original dimensions of the dataset images. Employing this long sequence pretraining technique, our STAR-B model achieved an impressive accuracy of 83.5\% on ImageNet-1k, which is highly competitive in Vision Mamba. These results underscore the potential of our method in enhancing the performance of vision models through improved leveraging of long-range dependencies.

AI.tldr: Proposes STAR, a simple separator-token scheme that enables much longer autoregressive pretraining for Vision Mamba by concatenating multiple images into one sequence, quadrupling sequence length without resizing images and achieving 83.5% ImageNet-1k accuracy.
AI.motivation: Although Mamba excels at long-sequence modeling and is naturally causal, existing autoregressive pretraining in vision remains confined to short sequences, underutilizing Mamba’s strength in capturing long-range dependencies.
AI.method: Insert identical separator tokens before each image to mark its start when concatenating multiple images into a single long sequence for autoregressive pretraining (Vision Mamba). This preserves original image dimensions while extending input length by 4×. The approach is instantiated in a STAR-B model.
AI.result: With long-sequence pretraining using STAR, the STAR-B model attains 83.5% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet-1k, reported as competitive for Vision Mamba models.
AI.conclusion: Simple structural delimiters (STAR) let Mamba leverage long-range dependencies during autoregressive pretraining, improving image classification performance without changing image size, suggesting a scalable path for vision state-space models.

title: PulseLM: A Foundation Dataset and Benchmark for PPG-Text Learning
summary: Photoplethysmography (PPG) is a widely used non-invasive sensing modality for continuous cardiovascular and physiological monitoring across clinical, laboratory, and wearable settings. While existing PPG datasets support a broad range of downstream tasks, they typically provide supervision in the form of numerical measurements or task-specific labels, limiting their suitability for language-based physiological reasoning and multimodal foundation models. In this work, we introduce PulseLM, a large-scale PPG-text dataset designed to bridge raw PPG waveforms and natural language through a unified, closed-ended question answering (QA) formulation. PulseLM aggregates PPG recordings from fifteen publicly available sources and harmonizes heterogeneous annotations into twelve common physiologically QA tasks. The dataset comprises 1.31 million standardized 10-second PPG segments, associated with 3.15 million question-answer pairs. We further define reproducible preprocessing, supervision, and evaluation protocols and establish baseline benchmarks using multimodal PPG-aware large language models. PulseLM provides a standardized foundation for studying multimodal physiological reasoning, cross-dataset generalization, and scalable benchmarking of PPG-based language models. The data and code can be found publicly available at: https://github.com/manhph2211/PulseLM.

AI.tldr: PulseLM is a large-scale PPG-to-text dataset framing physiological understanding as closed-ended QA, unifying 15 sources into 12 tasks with 1.31M 10-second segments and 3.15M QA pairs, plus protocols and baselines for multimodal PPG-aware LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing PPG datasets mainly provide numeric measurements or task-specific labels, which are ill-suited for language-based reasoning and training/evaluating multimodal foundation models that require aligned natural language supervision.
AI.method: Aggregate PPG signals from 15 public datasets; standardize to 10-second segments; harmonize heterogeneous annotations into 12 physiologically meaningful QA tasks; generate 3.15M question–answer pairs; define preprocessing, supervision, and evaluation protocols; train and benchmark multimodal LLM baselines conditioned on PPG.
AI.result: A public dataset (1.31M segments, 3.15M QA pairs) with unified task taxonomy and standardized pipelines; released code and evaluation protocols; initial baseline results from PPG-aware LLMs demonstrating feasibility of language-grounded physiological reasoning.
AI.conclusion: PulseLM offers a scalable, standardized foundation for multimodal physiological reasoning with PPG, facilitating cross-dataset generalization studies and reproducible benchmarking of PPG-conditioned language models.

title: Adaptive Enhancement and Dual-Pooling Sequential Attention for Lightweight Underwater Object Detection with YOLOv10
comment: Accepted in 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence, and Networking (QPAIN)
summary: Underwater object detection constitutes a pivotal endeavor within the realms of marine surveillance and autonomous underwater systems; however, it presents significant challenges due to pronounced visual impairments arising from phenomena such as light absorption, scattering, and diminished contrast. In response to these formidable challenges, this manuscript introduces a streamlined yet robust framework for underwater object detection, grounded in the YOLOv10 architecture. The proposed method integrates a Multi-Stage Adaptive Enhancement module to improve image quality, a Dual-Pooling Sequential Attention (DPSA) mechanism embedded into the backbone to strengthen multi-scale feature representation, and a Focal Generalized IoU Objectness (FGIoU) loss to jointly improve localization accuracy and objectness prediction under class imbalance. Comprehensive experimental evaluations conducted on the RUOD and DUO benchmark datasets substantiate that the proposed DPSA_FGIoU_YOLOv10n attains exceptional performance, achieving mean Average Precision (mAP) scores of 88.9% and 88.0% at IoU threshold 0.5, respectively. In comparison to the baseline YOLOv10n, this represents enhancements of 6.7% for RUOD and 6.2% for DUO, all while preserving a compact model architecture comprising merely 2.8M parameters. These findings validate that the proposed framework establishes an efficacious equilibrium among accuracy, robustness, and real-time operational efficiency, making it suitable for deployment in resource-constrained underwater settings.

AI.tldr: A compact YOLOv10-based detector augments images, improves multi-scale attention, and uses a new FGIoU loss to boost underwater object detection, yielding ~6–7% mAP@0.5 gains on RUOD and DUO with only 2.8M parameters.
AI.motivation: Underwater imagery suffers from light absorption, scattering, and low contrast, degrading detection reliability for surveillance and autonomous systems. The work aims to balance accuracy, robustness, and real-time efficiency under these challenging visual conditions and limited compute.
AI.method: Build on YOLOv10 with three key components: (1) Multi-Stage Adaptive Enhancement to restore/improve image quality; (2) Dual-Pooling Sequential Attention (DPSA) in the backbone to strengthen multi-scale feature representation; (3) Focal Generalized IoU Objectness (FGIoU) loss to jointly improve localization and objectness under class imbalance. Overall architecture remains lightweight (2.8M params).
AI.result: On RUOD and DUO benchmarks, the proposed DPSA_FGIoU_YOLOv10n achieves mAP@0.5 of 88.9% and 88.0%, improving over baseline YOLOv10n by 6.7% and 6.2%, respectively, while keeping a 2.8M-parameter model size.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers a strong accuracy–efficiency trade-off for underwater detection, demonstrating robustness and real-time suitability for resource-constrained deployments.

title: Fragile Thoughts: How Large Language Models Handle Chain-of-Thought Perturbations
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has emerged as a foundational technique for eliciting reasoning from Large Language Models (LLMs), yet the robustness of this approach to corruptions in intermediate reasoning steps remains poorly understood. This paper presents a comprehensive empirical evaluation of LLM robustness to a structured taxonomy of 5 CoT perturbation types: \textit{MathError, UnitConversion, Sycophancy, SkippedSteps,} and \textit{ExtraSteps}. We evaluate 13 models spanning three orders of magnitude in parameter count (3B to 1.5T\footnote{Assumed parameter count of closed models}), testing their ability to complete mathematical reasoning tasks despite perturbations injected at different points in the reasoning chain. Our key findings reveal heterogeneous vulnerability patterns: MathError perturbations produce the most severe degradation in small models (50-60\% accuracy loss) but show strong scaling benefits; UnitConversion remains challenging across all scales (20-30\% loss even for largest models); ExtraSteps incur minimal accuracy degradation (0-6\%) regardless of scale; Sycophancy produces modest effects (7\% loss for small models); and SkippedSteps cause intermediate damage (15\% loss). Scaling relationships follow power-law patterns, with model size serving as a protective factor against some perturbations but offering limited defense against dimensional reasoning tasks. These findings have direct implications for deploying LLMs in multi-stage reasoning pipelines and underscore the necessity of task-specific robustness assessments and mitigation strategies. The code and results are available \href{https://github.com/Mystic-Slice/CoTPerturbation}{here}.

AI.tldr: They systematically test how robust Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning is to five targeted perturbations across 13 LLMs (3B–1.5T). Math errors devastate small models but improve with scale; unit conversions remain hard even for the largest; extra steps are mostly harmless; skipped steps cause moderate harm; sycophancy modestly affects small models. Scaling shows power-law gains but doesn’t fix dimensional reasoning. Implications: assess robustness per task and design mitigations for multi-stage pipelines.
AI.motivation: CoT is widely used to elicit reasoning in LLMs, but it’s unclear how fragile it is when intermediate steps are corrupted. Understanding which perturbations derail reasoning, and how model size mitigates them, is crucial for safe deployment in multi-step pipelines.
AI.method: Define a structured taxonomy of five CoT perturbations—MathError, UnitConversion, Sycophancy, SkippedSteps, ExtraSteps—and inject them at different points of the chain during mathematical reasoning tasks. Evaluate 13 models spanning ~3B to ~1.5T parameters, measuring accuracy degradation and analyzing scaling trends (power-law fits).
AI.result: Heterogeneous vulnerabilities: MathError causes the largest accuracy loss for small models (≈50–60%) but shows strong improvements with scale; UnitConversion remains difficult at all scales (≈20–30% loss even for the largest); ExtraSteps minimally affects accuracy (≈0–6%); Sycophancy yields modest drops (≈7% in small models); SkippedSteps causes intermediate harm (≈15%). Scaling generally helps and follows power-law relationships, but offers limited defense against dimensional reasoning (unit conversion).
AI.conclusion: CoT robustness is non-uniform across perturbation types. Model scaling protects against some errors but not dimensional reasoning. Reliable multi-stage deployments require task-specific robustness evaluations and targeted mitigations; simply scaling models is insufficient. Code/results: https://github.com/Mystic-Slice/CoTPerturbation

title: Vector-Quantized Soft Label Compression for Dataset Distillation
summary: Dataset distillation is an emerging technique for reducing the computational and storage costs of training machine learning models by synthesizing a small, informative subset of data that captures the essential characteristics of a much larger dataset. Recent methods pair synthetic samples and their augmentations with soft labels from a teacher model, enabling student models to generalize effectively despite the small size of the distilled dataset. While soft labels are critical for effective distillation, the storage and communication overhead they incur, especially when accounting for augmentations, is often overlooked. In practice, each distilled sample is associated with multiple soft labels, making them the dominant contributor to storage costs, particularly in large-class settings such as ImageNet-1K. In this paper, we present a rigorous analysis of bit requirements across dataset distillation frameworks, quantifying the storage demands of both distilled samples and their soft labels. To address the overhead, we introduce a vector-quantized autoencoder (VQAE) for compressing soft labels, achieving substantial compression while preserving the effectiveness of the distilled data. We validate our method on both vision and language distillation benchmarks. On ImageNet-1K, our proposed VQAE achieves 30--40x additional compression over RDED, LPLD, SRE2L, and CDA baselines while retaining over $90\%$ of their original performance.

AI.tldr: They show that in dataset distillation, soft labels (especially across augmentations) dominate storage, then compress those labels with a vector‑quantized autoencoder to cut bits by 30–40× while keeping >90% of baseline accuracy on ImageNet‑1K and other tasks.
AI.motivation: Dataset distillation promises tiny training sets that mimic large datasets, but practical deployment is hampered by the storage/communication cost of per-sample soft labels—multiplied further by augmentations and large class counts (e.g., ImageNet‑1K).
AI.method: (1) A rigorous bit‑budget analysis covering distilled samples and their augmentation‑conditioned soft labels across frameworks. (2) A vector‑quantized autoencoder (VQAE) tailored to compress soft labels, enabling lossy yet faithful reconstruction at training time. Evaluated by attaching to existing distillation methods (RDED, LPLD, SRE2L, CDA) on vision and language benchmarks.
AI.result: Soft labels are the primary storage bottleneck in modern distillation. The proposed VQAE yields 30–40× extra compression of soft labels over strong baselines while preserving over 90% of their original performance on ImageNet‑1K; similar trends on language tasks.
AI.conclusion: Compressing soft labels is key to scalable dataset distillation. Vector‑quantized label encodings substantially reduce storage/communication without severely harming downstream accuracy, making distillation more practical in large‑class and augmentation‑heavy regimes.
