title: Training-free Dropout Sampling for Semantic Token Acceptance in Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model inference by proposing tokens with a lightweight draft model and selectively accepting them using a target model. This work introduces DropMatch, a novel approach that matches draft tokens to the predictive distribution of the target model via Monte Carlo dropout applied exclusively to the LM head, enabling sampling-based acceptance decisions. By generating multiple decoding paths, our method forms an empirical token distribution against which draft tokens are evaluated for consistency. This acceptance mechanism enables the model to adaptively control the size of decoding paths under an appropriate dropout probability, preventing substantial distortion of the target model predictive distribution. The proposed method operates in a training-free, data-free, and calibration-free manner, requires no architectural modification to pretrained models, and can be orthogonally integrated with a wide range of existing speculative decoding and inference acceleration techniques. Experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that our approach increases acceptance length while maintaining competitive task performance, yielding inference speedups ranging from 1.09x to 1.33x over the standard baseline, and up to an additional 1.09x speedup when applied on top of EAGLE3.

AI.tldr: DropMatch is a plug-in acceptance mechanism for speculative decoding that uses Monte Carlo dropout only at the target model’s LM head to approximate its predictive distribution, letting the system accept more draft tokens without retraining or calibration and yielding 1.09–1.33× speedups (plus up to +1.09× on EAGLE3) with comparable accuracy.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding speedups are constrained by conservative or mismatched acceptance rules that either distort the target model’s distribution or require extra training/calibration. The goal is to safely increase accepted token length—and thus throughput—without modifying or retraining the model.
AI.method: Apply Monte Carlo dropout exclusively to the target LM head to generate multiple stochastic logits, yielding several short decoding paths. These samples form an empirical approximation to the target model’s token distribution. Draft tokens are accepted if they are consistent with this empirical distribution. The dropout probability adaptively controls path size, mitigating distributional distortion. The approach is training-free, data-free, calibration-free, requires no architectural changes, and composes with other acceleration methods.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, DropMatch increases acceptance lengths while maintaining competitive task performance, producing 1.09×–1.33× speedups over a standard baseline. When stacked on EAGLE3, it provides up to an additional 1.09× speedup.
AI.conclusion: A practical, orthogonal, and calibration-free acceptance mechanism that better matches the target model’s predictive distribution via LM-head-only MC dropout, improving speculative decoding efficiency without sacrificing accuracy or changing model architecture.

title: Structure-aware Prompt Adaptation from Seen to Unseen for Open-Vocabulary Compositional Zero-Shot Learning
summary: The goal of Open-Vocabulary Compositional Zero-Shot Learning (OV-CZSL) is to recognize attribute-object compositions in the open-vocabulary setting, where compositions of both seen and unseen attributes and objects are evaluated. Recently, prompt tuning methods have demonstrated strong generalization capabilities in the closed setting, where only compositions of seen attributes and objects are evaluated, i.e., Compositional Zero-Shot Learning (CZSL). However, directly applying these methods to OV-CZSL may not be sufficient to generalize to unseen attributes, objects and their compositions, as it is limited to seen attributes and objects. Normally, when faced with unseen concepts, humans adopt analogies with seen concepts that have the similar semantics thereby inferring their meaning (e.g., "wet" and "damp", "shirt" and "jacket"). In this paper, we experimentally show that the distribution of semantically related attributes or objects tends to form consistent local structures in the embedding space. Based on the above structures, we propose Structure-aware Prompt Adaptation (SPA) method, which enables models to generalize from seen to unseen attributes and objects. Specifically, in the training stage, we design a Structure-aware Consistency Loss (SCL) that encourages the local structure's consistency of seen attributes and objects in each iteration. In the inference stage, we devise a Structure-guided Adaptation Strategy (SAS) that adaptively aligns the structures of unseen attributes and objects with those of trained seen attributes and objects with similar semantics. Notably, SPA is a plug-and-play method that can be seamlessly integrated into existing CZSL prompt tuning methods. Extensive experiments on OV-CZSL benchmarks demonstrate that SPA achieves competitive closed-set performance while significantly improving open-vocabulary results.

AI.tldr: They introduce Structure-aware Prompt Adaptation (SPA)—a plug-and-play add-on to prompt-tuned CZSL models—that preserves local semantic structure during training and aligns unseen concepts to similar seen ones at inference, yielding large open-vocabulary gains without sacrificing closed-set performance.
AI.motivation: Prompt tuning works well for CZSL where only seen attribute–object compositions appear, but it fails to generalize to unseen attributes/objects required by OV-CZSL. Humans infer unseen concepts via analogy to semantically similar ones; the authors observe similar local semantic structures in embedding space and aim to exploit them to bridge this generalization gap.
AI.method: Empirically show that semantically related attributes/objects form consistent local neighborhoods in the embedding space. Propose SPA with two components: (1) Structure-aware Consistency Loss (SCL) to enforce local-structure consistency among seen attributes/objects during training; (2) Structure-guided Adaptation Strategy (SAS) at inference to adaptively align embeddings of unseen attributes/objects to those of semantically similar seen ones. SPA integrates seamlessly with existing CZSL prompt-tuning methods.
AI.result: On standard OV-CZSL benchmarks, SPA maintains competitive closed-set (CZSL) accuracy while substantially improving open-vocabulary recognition of unseen attributes, objects, and their compositions. (Abstract reports significant gains; exact numbers not provided.)
AI.conclusion: Encoding and transferring local semantic structure enables prompt-tuned models to generalize from seen to unseen concepts in OV-CZSL. SPA is simple, plug-and-play, and improves open-vocabulary performance without degrading closed-set results.

title: The CompMath-MCQ Dataset: Are LLMs Ready for Higher-Level Math?
summary: The evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) on mathematical reasoning has largely focused on elementary problems, competition-style questions, or formal theorem proving, leaving graduate-level and computational mathematics relatively underexplored. We introduce CompMath-MCQ, a new benchmark dataset for assessing LLMs on advanced mathematical reasoning in a multiple-choice setting. The dataset consists of 1{,}500 originally authored questions by professors of graduate-level courses, covering topics including Linear Algebra, Numerical Optimization, Vector Calculus, Probability, and Python-based scientific computing. Three option choices are provided for each question, with exactly one of them being correct. To ensure the absence of data leakage, all questions are newly created and not sourced from existing materials. The validity of questions is verified through a procedure based on cross-LLM disagreement, followed by manual expert review. By adopting a multiple-choice format, our dataset enables objective, reproducible, and bias-free evaluation through lm_eval library. Baseline results with state-of-the-art LLMs indicate that advanced computational mathematical reasoning remains a significant challenge. We release CompMath-MCQ at the following link: https://github.com/biancaraimondi/CompMath-MCQ.git

AI.tldr: CompMath-MCQ is a new graduate-level, multiple-choice benchmark (1,500 freshly authored questions; 3 options each) spanning advanced linear algebra, numerical optimization, vector calculus, probability, and Python-based scientific computing. Questions are validated via cross-LLM disagreement and expert review, integrated with lm_eval for objective, reproducible scoring. State-of-the-art LLMs underperform, indicating advanced computational mathematical reasoning remains difficult.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM math evaluations emphasize elementary problems, contest questions, or formal theorem proving, leaving graduate-level and computational mathematics under-evaluated. The authors seek an objective, leakage-free, and reproducible benchmark to measure advanced computational mathematical reasoning.
AI.method: Create 1,500 original MCQs authored by graduate-course professors across several advanced topics, with exactly one correct option among three. Ensure no data leakage by authoring new items, then validate items through a pipeline: cross-LLM disagreement to surface potentially ambiguous/low-quality items followed by manual expert review. Package the dataset for standardized evaluation via the lm_eval library and release it publicly.
AI.result: Baseline evaluations of leading LLMs on the benchmark show notably low accuracy, suggesting limited capability on graduate-level computational math tasks even in a constrained MCQ format.
AI.conclusion: CompMath-MCQ offers an objective and reproducible way to assess advanced computational math reasoning, revealing a performance gap in current LLMs and providing a resource to drive progress and comparative evaluation in this area.

title: From Narrow to Panoramic Vision: Attention-Guided Cold-Start Reshapes Multimodal Reasoning
summary: The cold-start initialization stage plays a pivotal role in training Multimodal Large Reasoning Models (MLRMs), yet its mechanisms remain insufficiently understood. To analyze this stage, we introduce the Visual Attention Score (VAS), an attention-based metric that quantifies how much a model attends to visual tokens. We find that reasoning performance is strongly correlated with VAS (r=0.9616): models with higher VAS achieve substantially stronger multimodal reasoning. Surprisingly, multimodal cold-start fails to elevate VAS, resulting in attention distributions close to the base model, whereas text-only cold-start leads to a clear increase. We term this counter-intuitive phenomenon Lazy Attention Localization. To validate its causal role, we design training-free interventions that directly modulate attention allocation during inference, performance gains of 1$-$2% without any retraining. Building on these insights, we further propose Attention-Guided Visual Anchoring and Reflection (AVAR), a comprehensive cold-start framework that integrates visual-anchored data synthesis, attention-guided objectives, and visual-anchored reward shaping. Applied to Qwen2.5-VL-7B, AVAR achieves an average gain of 7.0% across 7 multimodal reasoning benchmarks. Ablation studies further confirm that each component of AVAR contributes step-wise to the overall gains. The code, data, and models are available at https://github.com/lrlbbzl/Qwen-AVAR.

AI.tldr: They show that how much a multimodal LLM attends to visual tokens (VAS) during cold-start tightly predicts and causally affects multimodal reasoning; multimodal cold-start often keeps attention “lazy,” while text-only cold-start raises visual focus. Simple inference-time attention tweaks add 1–2% performance, and their AVAR cold-start framework that anchors and rewards visual attention yields a 7% average gain on Qwen2.5-VL-7B across 7 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Cold-start training for multimodal reasoning is poorly understood and often fails to make models use images effectively. The authors seek a principled, measurable handle on visual token usage and a way to turn that handle to improve early-stage reasoning performance.
AI.method: 1) Define Visual Attention Score (VAS) to quantify attention mass on visual tokens. 2) Empirically correlate VAS with reasoning quality and compare cold-start regimes, revealing “Lazy Attention Localization.” 3) Design training-free inference interventions to reallocate attention toward visual tokens. 4) Propose AVAR: a cold-start recipe combining visual-anchored data synthesis, attention-guided training objectives, and visual-anchored reward shaping to steer attention and reasoning.
AI.result: VAS strongly correlates with multimodal reasoning (r=0.9616). Multimodal cold-start fails to raise VAS; text-only cold-start increases it. Training-free attention modulation at inference gives 1–2% gains without retraining. The AVAR framework yields a 7.0% average improvement on 7 multimodal reasoning benchmarks for Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Ablations show each AVAR component contributes step-wise to the total gain.
AI.conclusion: Visual attention concentration is a key driver of multimodal reasoning during initialization. VAS offers a diagnostic and actionable lever; explicitly anchoring and rewarding attention to visual tokens mitigates lazy localization and substantially boosts performance. AVAR provides a practical recipe, with released code/data/models, for improving MLRM cold-start.

title: Compressed Sensing for Capability Localization in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit a wide range of capabilities, including mathematical reasoning, code generation, and linguistic behaviors. We show that many capabilities are highly localized to small subsets of attention heads within Transformer architectures. Zeroing out as few as five task-specific heads can degrade performance by up to $65\%$ on standard benchmarks measuring the capability of interest, while largely preserving performance on unrelated tasks. We introduce a compressed sensing based method that exploits the sparsity of these heads to identify them via strategic knockouts and a small number of model evaluations. We validate these findings across Llama and Qwen models ranging from 1B to 8B parameters and a diverse set of capabilities including mathematical abilities and code generation, revealing a modular organization in which specialized capabilities are implemented by sparse, functionally distinct components. Overall, our results suggest that capability localization is a general organizational principle of Transformer language models, with implications for interpretability, model editing, and AI safety. Code is released at https://github.com/locuslab/llm-components.

AI.tldr: LLM capabilities are localized: a few attention heads disproportionately implement specific skills. Strategically ablating just ~5 heads can slash performance on a targeted capability (up to ~65%) while leaving others mostly intact. A compressed-sensing knockout method efficiently finds these sparse, task-specific heads across Llama/Qwen (1B–8B), indicating modular organization with interpretability, editing, and safety implications.
AI.motivation: Probe where and how distinct capabilities live inside Transformer LLMs to advance mechanistic interpretability, enable targeted model editing, and provide levers for safety controls. Existing views of distributed representations make intervention hard; the authors hypothesize sparse modular components may underlie complex skills and seek an efficient way to discover them.
AI.method: Systematically ablate attention heads by zeroing their outputs during inference and measure task degradation. Use a compressed sensing approach with strategic, pooled “knockout” evaluations to exploit sparsity and recover the small set of heads most responsible for each capability. Validate across multiple models (Llama, Qwen; 1B–8B) and tasks (math reasoning, code generation, linguistic behaviors).
AI.result:
- Knocking out as few as five heads can reduce performance on the targeted capability by up to 65% while largely preserving unrelated tasks.
- The compressed-sensing strategy identifies impactful heads with relatively few evaluations.
- Evidence of modular, functionally distinct components corresponding to specific skills across architectures and sizes.
AI.conclusion: Capability localization to sparse subsets of attention heads appears to be a general organizational principle in Transformer LLMs. This supports a modular view of model internals and opens avenues for practical interventions: interpretability tooling, targeted capability editing/steering, and safety controls. Code available at the provided repository.

title: Universal Pansharpening Foundation Model
summary: Pansharpening generates the high-resolution multi-spectral (MS) image by integrating spatial details from a texture-rich panchromatic (PAN) image and spectral attributes from a low-resolution MS image. Existing methods are predominantly satellite-specific and scene-dependent, which severely limits their generalization across heterogeneous sensors and varied scenes, thereby reducing their real-world practicality. To address these challenges, we present FoundPS, a universal pansharpening foundation model for satellite-agnostic and scene-robust fusion. Specifically, we introduce a modality-interleaved transformer that learns band-wise modal specializations to form reversible spectral affine bases, mapping arbitrary-band MS into a unified latent space via tensor multiplication. Building upon this, we construct a latent diffusion bridge model to progressively evolve latent representations, and incorporate bridge posterior sampling to couple latent diffusion with pixel-space observations, enabling stable and controllable fusion. Furthermore, we devise infinite-dimensional pixel-to-latent interaction mechanisms to comprehensively capture the cross-domain dependencies between PAN observations and MS representations, thereby facilitating complementary information fusion. In addition, to support large-scale training and evaluation, we construct a comprehensive pansharpening benchmark, termed PSBench, consisting of worldwide MS and PAN image pairs from multiple satellites across diverse scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FoundPS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, exhibiting superior generalization and robustness across a wide range of pansharpening tasks.

AI.tldr: FoundPS is a universal, sensor-agnostic pansharpening foundation model that maps arbitrary-band MS images into a unified latent space and fuses them with PAN via a latent diffusion bridge, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness across diverse satellites and scenes. It is supported by a new large-scale benchmark (PSBench).
AI.motivation: Most pansharpening methods are tuned to specific satellites or scenes, limiting cross-sensor generalization and practical deployment. There is a need for a single, robust model that handles heterogeneous sensors, varying band counts, and diverse geographies while preserving spectral fidelity and spatial detail.
AI.method: 1) Modality-interleaved transformer that learns band-wise specializations as reversible spectral affine bases, mapping arbitrary-band MS to a shared latent space via tensor multiplication. 2) Latent diffusion bridge with bridge posterior sampling to evolve latent representations while tightly coupling diffusion to pixel-space observations for stable and controllable fusion. 3) Infinite-dimensional pixel-to-latent interaction mechanisms to model cross-domain dependencies between PAN and MS. 4) Construction of PSBench: a multi-satellite, worldwide PAN–MS dataset for large-scale training and evaluation.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, FoundPS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art pansharpening approaches, showing superior generalization and robustness across different satellites, band configurations, and scene types.
AI.conclusion: A single foundation model can deliver satellite-agnostic, scene-robust pansharpening by unifying MS spectra in latent space and coupling them to PAN via diffusion bridges. With PSBench, the approach demonstrates strong empirical gains and promises more practical, widely deployable fusion systems.

title: Prompt-Dependent Ranking of Large Language Models with Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Rankings derived from pairwise comparisons are central to many economic and computational systems. In the context of large language models (LLMs), rankings are typically constructed from human preference data and presented as leaderboards that guide deployment decisions. However, existing approaches rely on point estimates, implicitly treating rankings as fixed objects despite substantial estimation noise and context-dependent performance variation. Acting on such rankings can lead to misallocation and welfare loss when apparent differences are not statistically meaningful. We study prompt-dependent ranking inference under pairwise human preferences and develop a framework for decision-safe rankings with statistically valid uncertainty guarantees. We model preferences using a contextual Bradley-Terry-Luce model in which the latent utility of each model depends on the input prompt. Rather than targeting point estimates of utilities, we directly conduct inference on induced rankings, constructing confidence sets based on simultaneous confidence intervals for pairwise utility differences. This approach yields statistically valid marginal and simultaneous confidence sets for prompt-specific ranks. Our framework connects recent advances in rank inference to contextual preference learning and provides tools for robust ranking-based decision-making. Empirically, using large-scale human preference data from LLM evaluations, we show that rankings vary substantially across prompt characteristics and that many apparent rank differences are not statistically distinguishable. We further demonstrate how uncertainty-aware rankings identify dominance only when supported by the data and otherwise return partial orders.

AI.tldr: They propose an uncertainty-aware, prompt-conditional method to infer LLM rankings from pairwise human preferences, using simultaneous confidence intervals on pairwise utility differences to deliver statistically valid (often partial) rankings rather than overconfident leaderboards.
AI.motivation: Conventional leaderboards treat rankings as fixed point estimates despite noisy preference data and strong prompt dependence, risking misallocation and welfare loss when apparent differences aren’t statistically meaningful.
AI.method: Model preferences with a contextual Bradley–Terry–Luce setup where each model’s latent utility depends on the input prompt. Instead of estimating utilities and ranking them, directly infer ranks by building simultaneous confidence intervals for pairwise utility differences, yielding marginal and joint confidence sets for prompt-specific ranks and enabling partial orders when uncertainty is high.
AI.result: On large-scale LLM human preference datasets, rankings vary substantially across prompt types, and many pairwise rank differences are statistically indistinguishable. The proposed intervals identify dominance only when supported and otherwise return partial orders, providing decision-safe rankings with guaranteed error control.
AI.conclusion: Publishing and acting on LLM rankings should be uncertainty-aware and prompt-conditional. Their framework offers statistically valid tools that reduce overconfident decisions, highlight where true differences exist, and advocate partial orders when evidence is insufficient.

title: All-in-One Image Restoration via Causal-Deconfounding Wavelet-Disentangled Prompt Network
summary: Image restoration represents a promising approach for addressing the inherent defects of image content distortion. Standard image restoration approaches suffer from high storage cost and the requirement towards the known degradation pattern, including type and degree, which can barely be satisfied in dynamic practical scenarios. In contrast, all-in-one image restoration (AiOIR) eliminates multiple degradations within a unified model to circumvent the aforementioned issues. However, according to our causal analysis, we disclose that two significant defects still exacerbate the effectiveness and generalization of AiOIR models: 1) the spurious correlation between non-degradation semantic features and degradation patterns; 2) the biased estimation of degradation patterns. To obtain the true causation between degraded images and restored images, we propose Causal-deconfounding Wavelet-disentangled Prompt Network (CWP-Net) to perform effective AiOIR. CWP-Net introduces two modules for decoupling, i.e., wavelet attention module of encoder and wavelet attention module of decoder. These modules explicitly disentangle the degradation and semantic features to tackle the issue of spurious correlation. To address the issue stemming from the biased estimation of degradation patterns, CWP-Net leverages a wavelet prompt block to generate the alternative variable for causal deconfounding. Extensive experiments on two all-in-one settings prove the effectiveness and superior performance of our proposed CWP-Net over the state-of-the-art AiOIR methods.

AI.tldr: Proposes CWP-Net, a causally deconfounded, wavelet-based all-in-one image restoration model that disentangles degradation from semantics and uses a wavelet prompt to correct bias, achieving state-of-the-art performance across two AiOIR settings.
AI.motivation: Conventional restoration needs known degradation types/degrees and large storage, which is impractical in dynamic settings. Existing all-in-one models still suffer from spurious correlations between semantic and degradation features and biased estimation of degradation patterns, hurting effectiveness and generalization. The goal is to capture the true causal link between degraded and restored images.
AI.method: Introduce CWP-Net with: (1) wavelet attention modules in both encoder and decoder to explicitly disentangle degradation-related and semantic features (mitigating spurious correlations); (2) a wavelet prompt block that generates an alternative variable for causal deconfounding to correct bias in degradation estimation. The model operates in the wavelet domain to facilitate separation and prompting within a unified AiOIR framework.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two all-in-one restoration settings show CWP-Net outperforms prior state-of-the-art AiOIR methods, demonstrating stronger effectiveness and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Causal deconfounding combined with wavelet-based feature disentanglement yields more reliable, generalizable AiOIR, reducing spurious correlations and bias to improve restoration quality over existing approaches.

title: Tracing Pharmacological Knowledge In Large Language Models
comment: Accepted, Learning Meaningful Representations of Life (LMRL) Workshop @ ICLR 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong empirical performance across pharmacology and drug discovery tasks, yet the internal mechanisms by which they encode pharmacological knowledge remain poorly understood. In this work, we investigate how drug-group semantics are represented and retrieved within Llama-based biomedical language models using causal and probing-based interpretability methods. We apply activation patching to localize where drug-group information is stored across model layers and token positions, and complement this analysis with linear probes trained on token-level and sum-pooled activations. Our results demonstrate that early layers play a key role in encoding drug-group knowledge, with the strongest causal effects arising from intermediate tokens within the drug-group span rather than the final drug-group token. Linear probing further reveals that pharmacological semantics are distributed across tokens and are already present in the embedding space, with token-level probes performing near chance while sum-pooled representations achieve maximal accuracy. Together, these findings suggest that drug-group semantics in LLMs are not localized to single tokens but instead arise from distributed representations. This study provides the first systematic mechanistic analysis of pharmacological knowledge in LLMs, offering insights into how biomedical semantics are encoded in large language models.

AI.tldr: They use causal (activation patching) and probing analyses on Llama-based biomedical LLMs to show that drug-group semantics are encoded early and distributed across tokens, not at single final tokens; pooled representations capture this best.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform well on pharmacology tasks, but it’s unclear how they internally represent pharmacological knowledge. Understanding mechanisms could guide safer, more reliable biomedical AI and model design.
AI.method: Apply activation patching across layers and token positions to localize causal sources of drug-group information; train linear probes on token-level activations and on sum-pooled activations (including embeddings) to test where and how information is decodable.
AI.result: Early layers carry key drug-group signals; strongest causal effects come from intermediate tokens in the drug-group span rather than the last token. Token-level probes perform near chance, but sum-pooled activations, including at the embedding layer, achieve high accuracy, indicating distributed representations present from the input embedding space.
AI.conclusion: Drug-group semantics in Llama-based biomedical LLMs are distributed across tokens and emerge early in the network; pooled representations are necessary for reliable retrieval. This is presented as the first systematic mechanistic study of pharmacological knowledge in LLMs, informing interpretability and model design for biomedical semantics.

title: DeepScan: A Training-Free Framework for Visually Grounded Reasoning in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Humans can robustly localize visual evidence and provide grounded answers even in noisy environments by identifying critical cues and then relating them to the full context in a bottom-up manner. Inspired by this, we propose DeepScan, a training-free framework that combines Hierarchical Scanning, Refocusing, and Evidence-Enhanced Reasoning for visually grounded reasoning in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Unlike existing methods that pursue one-shot localization of complete evidence, Hierarchical Scanning performs local cue exploration and multi-scale evidence extraction to recover evidence in a bottom-up manner, effectively mitigating the impacts of distractive context. Refocusing then optimizes the localized evidence view through collaboration of LVLMs and visual experts. Finally, Evidence-Enhanced Reasoning aggregates multi-granular views via a hybrid evidence memory and yields accurate and interpretable answers. Experimental results demonstrate that DeepScan significantly boosts LVLMs in diverse visual tasks, especially in fine-grained visual understanding. It achieves 90.6% overall accuracy on V* when integrated with Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Moreover, DeepScan provides consistent improvements for LVLMs across various architectures and model scales without additional adaptation cost.

AI.tldr: DeepScan is a training-free, plug-and-play framework that strengthens visually grounded reasoning in LVLMs by bottom-up evidence localization, expert-guided refocusing, and multi-granular evidence aggregation, yielding sizable gains—e.g., 90.6% on V* with Qwen2.5-VL-7B—without additional training.
AI.motivation: Human vision robustly localizes and contextualizes critical cues even amid noise. Existing LVLMs often attempt one-shot evidence localization and are brittle to distractors. There is a need for a robust, training-free approach to recover and use fine-grained visual evidence for grounded reasoning.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) Hierarchical Scanning conducts local cue exploration and multi-scale evidence extraction to recover evidence bottom-up and mitigate distractors; (2) Refocusing optimizes the candidate evidence views via cooperation between the LVLM and external visual experts; (3) Evidence-Enhanced Reasoning aggregates multi-granularity views through a hybrid evidence memory to produce accurate, interpretable answers. No additional training/fine-tuning is required.
AI.result: Across diverse visual tasks, DeepScan consistently improves LVLM performance, with particularly strong gains in fine-grained understanding. It reaches 90.6% overall accuracy on V* when paired with Qwen2.5-VL-7B and shows benefits across different architectures and model sizes without adaptation cost.
AI.conclusion: Bottom-up hierarchical scanning plus expert-guided refocusing and evidence-memory-driven reasoning yields more accurate and interpretable grounded reasoning. DeepScan is general, training-free, and improves a variety of LVLMs, highlighting practical plug-and-play value.

title: Farther the Shift, Sparser the Representation: Analyzing OOD Mechanisms in LLMs
summary: In this work, we investigate how Large Language Models (LLMs) adapt their internal representations when encountering inputs of increasing difficulty, quantified as the degree of out-of-distribution (OOD) shift. We reveal a consistent and quantifiable phenomenon: as task difficulty increases, whether through harder reasoning questions, longer contexts, or adding answer choices, the last hidden states of LLMs become substantially sparser. In short, \textbf{\textit{the farther the shift, the sparser the representations}}. This sparsity--difficulty relation is observable across diverse models and domains, suggesting that language models respond to unfamiliar or complex inputs by concentrating computation into specialized subspaces in the last hidden state. Through a series of controlled analyses with a learning dynamic explanation, we demonstrate that this sparsity is not incidental but an adaptive mechanism for stabilizing reasoning under OOD. Leveraging this insight, we design \textit{Sparsity-Guided Curriculum In-Context Learning (SG-ICL)}, a strategy that explicitly uses representation sparsity to schedule few-shot demonstrations, leading to considerable performance enhancements. Our study provides new mechanistic insights into how LLMs internalize OOD challenges. The source code is available at the URL: https://github.com/MingyuJ666/sparsityLLM.

AI.tldr: LLMs’ last-layer representations become markedly sparser as inputs get harder or more out-of-distribution; this sparsity appears adaptive and can be exploited to improve in-context learning via a sparsity-guided curriculum.
AI.motivation: To understand how LLMs internally cope with increasing task difficulty and out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts, and to translate mechanistic insights about representation changes into practical methods that improve few-shot performance.
AI.method: Measure sparsity of last hidden states across models and domains while systematically increasing difficulty (harder reasoning, longer contexts, added answer choices). Perform controlled analyses and provide a learning-dynamics explanation to argue that sparsity is an adaptive stabilization mechanism. Propose Sparsity-Guided Curriculum In-Context Learning (SG-ICL), which schedules few-shot demonstrations based on measured representation sparsity.
AI.result: Across diverse LLMs and tasks, higher difficulty/OOD shift consistently correlates with substantially sparser last-layer activations; analyses support an adaptive role for this sparsity. Using sparsity as a signal to order/select demonstrations (SG-ICL) yields considerable performance gains.
AI.conclusion: LLMs respond to unfamiliar or complex inputs by concentrating computation into specialized, sparse subspaces in the final hidden state. This sparsity–difficulty link offers a mechanistic lens on OOD robustness and enables a practical curriculum strategy (SG-ICL) that improves in-context learning performance.

title: Bridging Human Evaluation to Infrared and Visible Image Fusion
summary: Infrared and visible image fusion (IVIF) integrates complementary modalities to enhance scene perception. Current methods predominantly focus on optimizing handcrafted losses and objective metrics, often resulting in fusion outcomes that do not align with human visual preferences. This challenge is further exacerbated by the ill-posed nature of IVIF, which severely limits its effectiveness in human perceptual environments such as security surveillance and driver assistance systems. To address these limitations, we propose a feedback reinforcement framework that bridges human evaluation to infrared and visible image fusion. To address the lack of human-centric evaluation metrics and data, we introduce the first large-scale human feedback dataset for IVIF, containing multidimensional subjective scores and artifact annotations, and enriched by a fine-tuned large language model with expert review. Based on this dataset, we design a domain-specific reward function and train a reward model to quantify perceptual quality. Guided by this reward, we fine-tune the fusion network through Group Relative Policy Optimization, achieving state-of-the-art performance that better aligns fused images with human aesthetics. Code is available at https://github.com/ALKA-Wind/EVAFusion.

AI.tldr: Human-feedback-driven reinforcement learning for infrared–visible image fusion: they build a large human-rated dataset with artifact annotations, train a reward model capturing perceptual preferences, and fine-tune a fusion network via Group Relative Policy Optimization to maximize this reward, achieving state-of-the-art human-aligned fusion quality.
AI.motivation: Conventional IVIF methods optimize handcrafted losses and objective metrics that correlate poorly with human perception, and the task is ill-posed—leading to visually unsatisfying results in critical applications like surveillance and driver assistance. There is also a lack of human-centric metrics and datasets for IVIF.
AI.method: 1) Construct the first large-scale human feedback dataset for IVIF with multidimensional subjective scores and artifact annotations, augmented via a fine-tuned LLM plus expert review. 2) Design a domain-specific perceptual reward and train a reward model on this dataset. 3) Fine-tune the fusion network using Group Relative Policy Optimization, guided by the learned reward, to align outputs with human aesthetics.
AI.result: The GRPO-fine-tuned fusion model guided by the learned reward achieves state-of-the-art performance and produces fused images that better match human visual preferences; code is released (github.com/ALKA-Wind/EVAFusion).
AI.conclusion: Integrating human feedback via reward modeling and policy optimization closes the gap between objective metrics and human visual quality in IVIF. The released dataset, reward model, and GRPO-based training provide a path toward perceptually aligned fusion systems for real-world applications.

title: Raising Bars, Not Parameters: LilMoo Compact Language Model for Hindi
summary: The dominance of large multilingual foundation models has widened linguistic inequalities in Natural Language Processing (NLP), often leaving low-resource languages underrepresented. This paper introduces LilMoo, a 0.6-billion-parameter Hindi language model trained entirely from scratch to address this gap. Unlike prior Hindi models that rely on continual pretraining from opaque multilingual foundations, LilMoo is developed through a fully transparent and reproducible pipeline optimized for limited compute environments. We construct a high-quality Hindi corpus (GigaLekh) filtered through both heuristic and learned (LLM-as-a-judge) methods, complemented by bilingual augmentation with curated English data. Using this dataset, we explore various training recipes for small-scale language models. Across comprehensive evaluation suites, LilMoo consistently outperforms comparably sized multilingual baselines such as Qwen2.5-0.5B and Qwen3-0.6B, demonstrating that well-designed language-specific pretraining can rival large multilingual models at the sub-billion-parameter range.

AI.tldr: LilMoo is a 0.6B-parameter Hindi LLM trained from scratch on a carefully filtered Hindi corpus (GigaLekh) with targeted English augmentation, using a transparent, compute-efficient pipeline; it outperforms similarly sized multilingual baselines (e.g., Qwen2.5-0.5B, Qwen3-0.6B), showing monolingual pretraining can rival multilingual models at sub‑billion scale.
AI.motivation: Large multilingual foundation models exacerbate linguistic inequality by underrepresenting low-resource languages like Hindi. Existing Hindi models often depend on opaque continual pretraining from multilingual bases, limiting transparency, reproducibility, and compute accessibility. The authors aim to build a high-quality, transparent, and reproducible Hindi LLM that performs strongly under modest compute.
AI.method: 1) Build GigaLekh, a high-quality Hindi corpus filtered via heuristics and LLM-as-a-judge. 2) Add curated bilingual (English) augmentation. 3) Explore training recipes for small-scale models. 4) Pretrain a 0.6B-parameter Hindi model (LilMoo) entirely from scratch using a compute-efficient, transparent pipeline. 5) Evaluate comprehensively against comparably sized multilingual baselines.
AI.result: Across evaluation suites, LilMoo consistently outperforms similarly sized multilingual models such as Qwen2.5-0.5B and Qwen3-0.6B.
AI.conclusion: Well-designed, language-specific pretraining with transparent data curation and limited compute can match or exceed the performance of comparable multilingual models, suggesting an effective path to narrowing linguistic gaps in NLP.

title: Yolo-Key-6D: Single Stage Monocular 6D Pose Estimation with Keypoint Enhancements
summary: Estimating the 6D pose of objects from a single RGB image is a critical task for robotics and extended reality applications. However, state-of-the-art multi stage methods often suffer from high latency, making them unsuitable for real time use. In this paper, we present Yolo-Key-6D, a novel single stage, end-to-end framework for monocular 6D pose estimation designed for both speed and accuracy. Our approach enhances a YOLO based architecture by integrating an auxiliary head that regresses the 2D projections of an object's 3D bounding box corners. This keypoint detection task significantly improves the network's understanding of 3D geometry. For stable end-to-end training, we directly regress rotation using a continuous 9D representation projected to SO(3) via singular value decomposition. On the LINEMOD and LINEMOD-Occluded benchmarks, YOLO-Key-6D achieves competitive accuracy scores of 96.24% and 69.41%, respectively, with the ADD(-S) 0.1d metric, while proving itself to operate in real time. Our results demonstrate that a carefully designed single stage method can provide a practical and effective balance of performance and efficiency for real world deployment.

AI.tldr: Yolo-Key-6D is a single-stage, YOLO-based monocular 6D pose estimator that augments detection with a keypoint head predicting 2D projections of 3D box corners and regresses rotation via a continuous 9D representation projected to SO(3) by SVD, achieving competitive accuracy on LINEMOD(-Occluded) while running in real time.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-stage 6D pose pipelines are accurate but incur high latency, limiting real-time robotics and XR deployment. The goal is to design an end-to-end, single-stage approach that maintains accuracy with much lower inference time.
AI.method: Enhance a YOLO architecture with an auxiliary head that regresses 2D projections of 3D bounding box corners to inject geometric cues. Regress rotation directly using a continuous 9D matrix representation and project to a valid SO(3) rotation via SVD for stable end-to-end training; estimate full 6D pose in a single shot.
AI.result: On LINEMOD and LINEMOD-Occluded with ADD(-S) 0.1d, the method reports 96.24% and 69.41% accuracy, respectively, and operates in real time (exact FPS not provided).
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed single-stage framework with geometric auxiliary supervision and robust rotation parametrization can deliver a strong speed–accuracy balance, making monocular 6D pose estimation practical for real-world applications.

title: A theoretical model of dynamical grammatical gender shifting based on set-valued set function
summary: This study investigates the diverse characteristics of nouns, focusing on both semantic (e.g., countable/uncountable) and morphosyntactic (e.g., masculine/feminine) distinctions. We explore inter-word variations for gender markers in noun morphology. Grammatical gender shift is a widespread phenomenon in languages around the world. The aim is to uncover through a formal model the underlying patterns governing the variation of lexemes. To this end, we propose a new computational component dedicated to pairing items with morphological templates (e.g., the result of a generated item-template pair: (funas, $\{N, +SG, -PL, -M, +F, -COL, +SING\}$), with its spell-out form: $ð$a-funast 'cow'). This process is formally represented by the Template-Based and Modular Cognitive model. This proposed model, defined by a set-valued set function $h : \mathscr{P}(M) \rightarrow \mathscr{P}(M)$, predicts the nonlinear dynamic mapping of lexical items onto morphological templates. By applying this formalism, we present a unified framework for understanding the complexities of morphological markings across languages. Through empirical observations, we demonstrate how these shifts, as well as non-gender shifts, arise during lexical changes, especially in Riffian. Our model posits that these variant markings emerge due to template shifts occurring during word and meaning's formation. By formally demonstrating that conversion is applicable to noun-to-noun derivation, we challenge and broaden the conventional view of word formation. This mathematical model not only contributes to a deeper understanding of morphosyntactic variation but also offers potential applications in other fields requiring precise modelling of linguistic patterns.

AI.tldr: Introduces a formal, template-based computational model (a set-valued function h: P(M)→P(M)) that maps lexical items to morphological templates to explain gender and other morphosyntactic variation and shifts in nouns (with Riffian as a key case), unifying diverse markings and arguing for noun‑to‑noun conversion.
AI.motivation: Grammatical gender and related morphosyntactic markings vary across and within languages, with observed gender shifts and non-gender shifts lacking a unified, predictive account. The authors seek a principled, formal explanation for how lexemes come to bear different morphological markings over time and across items.
AI.method: Propose a Template-Based and Modular Cognitive model: a computational pairing mechanism that assigns lexical items to morphological templates. Formally, a set-valued set function h: P(M)→P(M) captures nonlinear, dynamic mappings from lexical feature sets to template realizations. Empirical observations—especially from Riffian—illustrate template shifts and their spell-out in surface forms.
AI.result: The model accounts for inter-word variation in gender markers and other features by treating them as outcomes of template shifts during lexical and semantic formation. It predicts nonlinear mappings from items to morphological realizations, offers a unified account across languages, and formally shows that conversion can operate in noun‑to‑noun derivation.
AI.conclusion: A mathematically grounded framework can explain morphosyntactic variation and gender shifts via template dynamics, broadening the view of word formation to include noun‑to‑noun conversion. The approach aims to generalize cross‑linguistically and may be applicable to other domains requiring precise pattern modeling.

title: UniSync: Towards Generalizable and High-Fidelity Lip Synchronization for Challenging Scenarios
summary: Lip synchronization aims to generate realistic talking videos that match given audio, which is essential for high-quality video dubbing. However, current methods have fundamental drawbacks: mask-based approaches suffer from local color discrepancies, while mask-free methods struggle with global background texture misalignment. Furthermore, most methods struggle with diverse real-world scenarios such as stylized avatars, face occlusion, and extreme lighting conditions. In this paper, we propose UniSync, a unified framework designed for achieving high-fidelity lip synchronization in diverse scenarios. Specifically, UniSync uses a mask-free pose-anchored training strategy to keep head motion and eliminate synthesis color artifacts, while employing mask-based blending consistent inference to ensure structural precision and smooth blending. Notably, fine-tuning on compact but diverse videos empowers our model with exceptional domain adaptability, handling complex corner cases effectively. We also introduce the RealWorld-LipSync benchmark to evaluate models under real-world demands, which covers diverse application scenarios including both human faces and stylized avatars. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniSync significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, advancing the field towards truly generalizable and production-ready lip synchronization.

AI.tldr: UniSync is a unified lip‑sync framework that pairs mask‑free, pose‑anchored training with mask‑based, consistency‑preserving inference, plus lightweight domain fine‑tuning. It handles humans and stylized avatars under challenging conditions and outperforms prior methods; a new RealWorld‑LipSync benchmark is introduced.
AI.motivation: Existing lip‑sync methods face core trade‑offs and poor robustness: mask‑based approaches create local color seams; mask‑free ones misalign global textures/backgrounds. Most fail on real‑world diversity (avatars, occlusions, extreme lighting). There’s a need for a generalizable, production‑ready method and a representative benchmark.
AI.method: UniSync employs a mask‑free, pose‑anchored training strategy to preserve head motion and avoid color artifacts. At inference, it switches to a mask‑based blending scheme to enforce structural precision and smooth compositing. The model is further adapted via fine‑tuning on compact yet diverse videos to boost domain adaptability and handle corner cases.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments and the introduced RealWorld‑LipSync benchmark spanning humans and stylized avatars, UniSync achieves significant improvements over state‑of‑the‑art methods, delivering higher fidelity, robustness to occlusion/lighting, and better generalization.
AI.conclusion: By unifying complementary training and inference strategies and enabling lightweight domain adaptation, UniSync advances lip synchronization toward generalizable, production‑ready deployment; the RealWorld‑LipSync benchmark supports rigorous evaluation across diverse scenarios.

title: SafeCRS: Personalized Safety Alignment for LLM-Based Conversational Recommender Systems
summary: Current LLM-based conversational recommender systems (CRS) primarily optimize recommendation accuracy and user satisfaction. We identify an underexplored vulnerability in which recommendation outputs may negatively impact users by violating personalized safety constraints, when individualized safety sensitivities -- such as trauma triggers, self-harm history, or phobias -- are implicitly inferred from the conversation but not respected during recommendation. We formalize this challenge as personalized CRS safety and introduce SafeRec, a new benchmark dataset designed to systematically evaluate safety risks in LLM-based CRS under user-specific constraints. To further address this problem, we propose SafeCRS, a safety-aware training framework that integrates Safe Supervised Fine-Tuning (Safe-SFT) with Safe Group reward-Decoupled Normalization Policy Optimization (Safe-GDPO) to jointly optimize recommendation quality and personalized safety alignment. Extensive experiments on SafeRec demonstrate that SafeCRS reduces safety violation rates by up to 96.5% relative to the strongest recommendation-quality baseline while maintaining competitive recommendation quality. Warning: This paper contains potentially harmful and offensive content.

AI.tldr: Identifies personalized safety risks in LLM-based conversational recommenders, releases a benchmark (SafeRec) to evaluate them, and proposes a training framework (SafeCRS: Safe-SFT + Safe-GDPO) that cuts safety violations by up to 96.5% while keeping recommendation quality competitive.
AI.motivation: LLM-based CRS often optimize accuracy and satisfaction but can inadvertently violate user-specific safety constraints (e.g., trauma triggers, self-harm history, phobias) inferred from conversation yet neglected in recommendations. The paper aims to formalize and mitigate these personalized safety failures.
AI.method: 1) Formalize personalized CRS safety. 2) Create SafeRec, a benchmark to systematically evaluate safety risks under user-specific constraints. 3) Propose SafeCRS, a safety-aware training framework combining Safe Supervised Fine-Tuning (Safe-SFT) and Safe Group reward-Decoupled Normalization Policy Optimization (Safe-GDPO) to jointly optimize recommendation quality and personalized safety alignment.
AI.result: On SafeRec, SafeCRS reduces safety violation rates by up to 96.5% relative to the strongest recommendation-quality baseline, without significant loss of recommendation performance.
AI.conclusion: Personalized safety is a critical, previously underexplored dimension for CRS. The SafeRec benchmark enables standardized evaluation, and SafeCRS demonstrates that strong safety alignment can be achieved with minimal quality trade-offs.

title: A novel network for classification of cuneiform tablet metadata
summary: In this paper, we present a network structure for classifying metadata of cuneiform tablets. The problem is of practical importance, as the size of the existing corpus far exceeds the number of experts available to analyze it. But the task is made difficult by the combination of limited annotated datasets and the high-resolution point-cloud representation of each tablet. To address this, we develop a convolution-inspired architecture that gradually down-scales the point cloud while integrating local neighbor information. The final down-scaled point cloud is then processed by computing neighbors in the feature space to include global information. Our method is compared with the state-of-the-art transformer-based network Point-BERT, and consistently obtains the best performance. Source code and datasets will be released at publication.

AI.tldr: A convolution-inspired, hierarchical point-cloud network for cuneiform tablet metadata classification that aggregates local neighbors during downsampling and adds feature-space global context, outperforming Point-BERT.
AI.motivation: The cuneiform corpus is vast relative to the small pool of experts who can annotate it. Automatic classification is challenging due to scarce labeled data and the high-resolution, computationally heavy point-cloud format of tablets.
AI.method: Introduce a hierarchical, convolution-like architecture that progressively downsamples point clouds while integrating local neighborhood information at each stage. After downsampling, compute neighbors in learned feature space to inject global context before classification.
AI.result: Across evaluations, the proposed network consistently surpasses the state-of-the-art transformer baseline Point-BERT on cuneiform metadata classification.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively handles limited annotations and high-resolution point clouds, enabling state-of-the-art performance on cuneiform metadata tasks; code and datasets will be released upon publication.

title: RAG-X: Systematic Diagnosis of Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Medical Question Answering
summary: Automated question-answering (QA) systems increasingly rely on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to ground large language models (LLMs) in authoritative medical knowledge, ensuring clinical accuracy and patient safety in Artificial Intelligence (AI) applications for healthcare. Despite progress in RAG evaluation, current benchmarks focus only on simple multiple-choice QA tasks and employ metrics that poorly capture the semantic precision required for complex QA tasks. These approaches fail to diagnose whether an error stems from faulty retrieval or flawed generation, limiting developers from performing targeted improvement. To address this gap, we propose RAG-X, a diagnostic framework that evaluates the retriever and generator independently across a triad of QA tasks: information extraction, short-answer generation, and multiple-choice question (MCQ) answering. RAG-X introduces Context Utilization Efficiency (CUE) metrics to disaggregate system success into interpretable quadrants, isolating verified grounding from deceptive accuracy. Our experiments reveal an ``Accuracy Fallacy", where a 14\% gap separates perceived system success from evidence-based grounding. By surfacing hidden failure modes, RAG-X offers the diagnostic transparency needed for safe and verifiable clinical RAG systems.

AI.tldr: RAG-X is a diagnostic framework for clinical RAG that separately evaluates retrieval and generation across three QA task types and introduces CUE metrics to distinguish evidence-grounded correctness from deceptively correct answers, revealing a 14% “Accuracy Fallacy.”
AI.motivation: Existing RAG benchmarks emphasize simple MCQs and coarse accuracy metrics, which miss semantic precision in complex clinical QA and cannot localize whether failures arise from retrieval or generation—hindering targeted improvements and risking unsafe outputs.
AI.method: Evaluate retriever and generator independently on a triad of tasks—information extraction, short-answer generation, and MCQ answering. Introduce Context Utilization Efficiency (CUE) metrics that partition outcomes into interpretable quadrants, separating verified grounding from ungrounded (but possibly correct) responses and teasing apart retrieval vs generation contributions.
AI.result: Experiments using RAG-X surface hidden failure modes and quantify an “Accuracy Fallacy”: a 14% gap between perceived success (e.g., answer correctness) and evidence-backed grounding, indicating systems can appear accurate without properly using retrieved clinical evidence.
AI.conclusion: RAG-X provides interpretable, component-level diagnostics and grounding-aware metrics to guide safer, verifiable clinical RAG development beyond raw accuracy—enabling targeted fixes to retrieval or generation and reducing risk from deceptively correct but ungrounded answers.

title: From Misclassifications to Outliers: Joint Reliability Assessment in Classification
comment: 15 pages, 3 figures. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Intellindust-AI-Lab/SUREPlus
summary: Building reliable classifiers is a fundamental challenge for deploying machine learning in real-world applications. A reliable system should not only detect out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs but also anticipate in-distribution (ID) errors by assigning low confidence to potentially misclassified samples. Yet, most prior work treats OOD detection and failure prediction as separated problems, overlooking their closed connection. We argue that reliability requires evaluating them jointly. To this end, we propose a unified evaluation framework that integrates OOD detection and failure prediction, quantified by our new metrics DS-F1 and DS-AURC, where DS denotes double scoring functions. Experiments on the OpenOOD benchmark show that double scoring functions yield classifiers that are substantially more reliable than traditional single scoring approaches. Our analysis further reveals that OOD-based approaches provide notable gains under simple or far-OOD shifts, but only marginal benefits under more challenging near-OOD conditions. Beyond evaluation, we extend the reliable classifier SURE and introduce SURE+, a new approach that significantly improves reliability across diverse scenarios. Together, our framework, metrics, and method establish a new benchmark for trustworthy classification and offer practical guidance for deploying robust models in real-world settings. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Intellindust-AI-Lab/SUREPlus.

AI.tldr: Proposes a unified reliability framework that jointly evaluates OOD detection and in-distribution failure prediction via double scoring functions, introducing DS-F1 and DS-AURC metrics; shows on OpenOOD that double scoring and the new SURE+ method improve reliability, with strong gains for far-OOD but limited benefits for near-OOD.
AI.motivation: Deployed classifiers must both reject out-of-distribution inputs and flag likely in-distribution misclassifications. Prior work treats these as separate tasks, obscuring their interdependence and yielding incomplete reliability assessments. A joint view promises more faithful evaluation and better design of trustworthy systems.
AI.method: Introduce double scoring functions that simultaneously score OOD-ness and failure likelihood; define unified metrics DS-F1 and DS-AURC to quantify reliability under this joint setting; empirically evaluate on the OpenOOD benchmark; analyze performance across far- vs near-OOD regimes; extend the SURE reliable classifier to SURE+ to improve reliability under diverse conditions.
AI.result: Double scoring consistently yields more reliable classifiers than traditional single-score baselines on OpenOOD. OOD-centric techniques confer large benefits for simple/far-OOD shifts but only modest improvements for near-OOD. The proposed SURE+ achieves significant gains in reliability across varied scenarios. Code is released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Reliability should be assessed and optimized jointly for OOD detection and failure prediction. The DS-F1/DS-AURC metrics and double scoring provide a clearer picture of real-world robustness, while SURE+ sets a stronger baseline. Near-OOD remains challenging, indicating room for methods that better handle subtle distribution shifts.

title: Tucano 2 Cool: Better Open Source LLMs for Portuguese
summary: We present Tucano 2, a fully open suite of large language models (LLMs) with 0.5-3.7 billion parameters, designed to address certain gaps in open-source development for Portuguese LLMs. Following our previous works, we now extend our dataset, GigaVerbo-v2, to a new degree of quality and scale, while also introducing a new synthetic dataset, GigaVerbo-v2 Synth, aimed at filling missing gaps in GigaVerbo-v2, and two post-training datasets, GigaVerbo-v2 SFT and GigaVerbo-v2 Preferences, that allow Portuguese LLMs to be trained in domains like retrieval augmented generation, coding, tool use, chain-of-thought reasoning, and many other domains of interest. Through extensive ablation studies, we design both pretraining and continual pretraining recipes for the Tucano 2 suite (Base, Instruct, and Think), which achieve state-of-the-art performance on several Portuguese-language modeling benchmarks. We also extend and refine the evaluation harness introduced in our earlier work, yielding a comprehensive evaluation suite that provides strong signals across different pretraining, continual pretraining, and post-training regimes. All artifacts associated with Tucano 2 are openly released, including training recipes, logs, and source code, ensuring that our work is reproducible, accessible, and extendable by the broader Portuguese NLP community.

AI.tldr: Tucano 2 is an openly released suite of 0.5–3.7B-parameter Portuguese LLMs trained on expanded GigaVerbo-v2 datasets (including synthetic, SFT, and preference data), with ablation-driven pretraining/continual-pretraining recipes, a refined evaluation harness, and state-of-the-art results on Portuguese benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in open-source Portuguese LLMs: limited data quality/scale, missing supervision for skills like RAG, coding, tool use, and chain-of-thought, and lack of a comprehensive, reproducible evaluation framework and training recipes.
AI.method: Construct and release an expanded high-quality corpus (GigaVerbo-v2), a gap-filling synthetic set (GigaVerbo-v2 Synth), and post-training datasets for SFT and preference optimization (GigaVerbo-v2 SFT/Preferences). Train multiple model sizes (0.5–3.7B) under ablation-driven pretraining and continual-pretraining recipes, produce Base/Instruct/Think variants, and refine an evaluation harness to measure performance across training regimes. Release all artifacts, logs, and code.
AI.result: Models achieve state-of-the-art performance on several Portuguese language modeling benchmarks. The evaluation suite yields informative signals for pretraining, continual pretraining, and post-training. The datasets enable training for domains such as RAG, coding, tool use, and chain-of-thought.
AI.conclusion: Tucano 2 delivers a reproducible, extensible Portuguese LLM ecosystem—data, models, recipes, and evaluation—that advances state-of-the-art performance and fills key capability and infrastructure gaps for the Portuguese NLP community.

title: Phys4D: Fine-Grained Physics-Consistent 4D Modeling from Video Diffusion
summary: Recent video diffusion models have achieved impressive capabilities as large-scale generative world models. However, these models often struggle with fine-grained physical consistency, exhibiting physically implausible dynamics over time. In this work, we present \textbf{Phys4D}, a pipeline for learning physics-consistent 4D world representations from video diffusion models. Phys4D adopts \textbf{a three-stage training paradigm} that progressively lifts appearance-driven video diffusion models into physics-consistent 4D world representations. We first bootstrap robust geometry and motion representations through large-scale pseudo-supervised pretraining, establishing a foundation for 4D scene modeling. We then perform physics-grounded supervised fine-tuning using simulation-generated data, enforcing temporally consistent 4D dynamics. Finally, we apply simulation-grounded reinforcement learning to correct residual physical violations that are difficult to capture through explicit supervision. To evaluate fine-grained physical consistency beyond appearance-based metrics, we introduce a set of \textbf{4D world consistency evaluation} that probe geometric coherence, motion stability, and long-horizon physical plausibility. Experimental results demonstrate that Phys4D substantially improves fine-grained spatiotemporal and physical consistency compared to appearance-driven baselines, while maintaining strong generative performance. Our project page is available at https://sensational-brioche-7657e7.netlify.app/

AI.tldr: Phys4D is a three-stage pipeline that upgrades appearance-driven video diffusion models into physics-consistent 4D world representations via (1) large-scale pseudo-supervised pretraining for geometry/motion, (2) simulation-grounded supervised fine-tuning for temporally coherent dynamics, and (3) simulation-grounded reinforcement learning to fix residual physical violations; it also introduces a 4D consistency evaluation suite and reports substantial gains in physical plausibility without sacrificing generative quality.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art video diffusion models generate visually convincing videos but often violate fine-grained physics (e.g., inconsistent geometry, unstable motion, implausible long-term dynamics). Existing evaluations are mostly appearance-based and fail to capture these errors. The paper aims to endow generative models with physics-consistent 4D world understanding and to provide principled metrics to measure it.
AI.method: A progressive, three-stage training paradigm: (1) Pseudo-supervised pretraining at scale to bootstrap robust geometry and motion representations from videos, forming a 4D modeling foundation. (2) Physics-grounded supervised fine-tuning using simulation-generated data to enforce temporally consistent 4D dynamics. (3) Simulation-grounded reinforcement learning that optimizes for residual physical consistency objectives difficult to encode via explicit supervision. The authors also propose a 4D world consistency evaluation suite assessing geometric coherence, motion stability, and long-horizon plausibility.
AI.result: Across experiments, Phys4D markedly improves spatiotemporal and physical consistency relative to appearance-driven baselines, while preserving strong generative performance. The proposed evaluation suite captures fine-grained physical improvements beyond standard appearance metrics.
AI.conclusion: Staged integration of simulation signals and RL can effectively transform video diffusion models into physics-consistent 4D world models. Phys4D offers both a practical training recipe and an evaluation framework, advancing physically plausible generative modeling without compromising visual quality.

title: Architecture and evaluation protocol for transformer-based visual object tracking in UAV applications
summary: Object tracking from Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) is challenged by platform dynamics, camera motion, and limited onboard resources. Existing visual trackers either lack robustness in complex scenarios or are too computationally demanding for real-time embedded use. We propose an Modular Asynchronous Tracking Architecture (MATA) that combines a transformer-based tracker with an Extended Kalman Filter, integrating ego-motion compensation from sparse optical flow and an object trajectory model. We further introduce a hardware-independent, embedded oriented evaluation protocol and a new metric called Normalized time to Failure (NT2F) to quantify how long a tracker can sustain a tracking sequence without external help. Experiments on UAV benchmarks, including an augmented UAV123 dataset with synthetic occlusions, show consistent improvements in Success and NT2F metrics across multiple tracking processing frequency. A ROS 2 implementation on a Nvidia Jetson AGX Orin confirms that the evaluation protocol more closely matches real-time performance on embedded systems.

AI.tldr: They propose MATA, a modular async tracker that fuses a transformer-based visual tracker with an EKF using ego-motion from sparse optical flow, plus a new embedded-oriented evaluation protocol and the NT2F metric; it improves robustness and endurance on UAV datasets and aligns better with on-device performance.
AI.motivation: UAV tracking suffers from rapid platform/camera motion, occlusions, and limited onboard compute. Many trackers are either brittle in complex aerial scenes or too heavy for real-time embedded use. There is also a mismatch between desktop evaluations and true embedded performance, and a lack of a metric capturing how long a tracker can operate unaided.
AI.method: Introduce Modular Asynchronous Tracking Architecture (MATA) that decouples modules: a transformer-based visual tracker augmented with an Extended Kalman Filter for object state propagation, integrating ego-motion compensation derived from sparse optical flow and an object trajectory model. Propose a hardware-independent, embedded-oriented evaluation protocol and define Normalized time to Failure (NT2F) to measure sustained tracking duration. Provide a ROS 2 implementation targeting Nvidia Jetson AGX Orin.
AI.result: On UAV benchmarks, including an augmented UAV123 with synthetic occlusions, MATA yields consistent gains in Success and NT2F across multiple processing frequencies. The proposed evaluation protocol correlates better with real-time behavior on embedded systems; a ROS 2 deployment on Jetson Orin validates these findings.
AI.conclusion: MATA increases robustness and time-to-failure for UAV object tracking under tight compute budgets, while the NT2F metric and evaluation protocol enable fairer, more realistic assessment of embedded tracking performance. The approach is feasible on modern edge hardware and improves practical reliability in dynamic aerial scenarios.

title: ByteFlow: Language Modeling through Adaptive Byte Compression without a Tokenizer
summary: Modern language models still rely on fixed, pre-defined subword tokenizations. Once a tokenizer is trained, the LM can only operate at this fixed level of granularity, which often leads to brittle and counterintuitive behaviors even in otherwise strong reasoning models. We introduce \textbf{ByteFlow Net}, a new hierarchical architecture that removes tokenizers entirely and instead enables models to learn their own segmentation of raw byte streams into semantically meaningful units. ByteFlow Net performs compression-driven segmentation based on the coding rate of latent representations, yielding adaptive boundaries \emph{while preserving a static computation graph via Top-$K$ selection}. Unlike prior self-tokenizing methods that depend on brittle heuristics with human-designed inductive biases, ByteFlow Net adapts its internal representation granularity to the input itself. Experiments demonstrate that this compression-based chunking strategy yields substantial performance gains, with ByteFlow Net outperforming both BPE-based Transformers and previous byte-level architectures. These results suggest that end-to-end, tokenizer-free modeling is not only feasible but also more effective, opening a path toward more adaptive and information-grounded language models.

AI.tldr: Introduces ByteFlow Net, a tokenizer-free, hierarchical LM that learns byte-level segmentations via compression-based criteria with Top-K selection to keep a static graph, outperforming BPE Transformers and prior byte-level models.
AI.motivation: Fixed, pre-trained tokenizers lock LMs into a single granularity, causing brittle behavior and counterintuitive failures; a need exists for adaptive, information-grounded segmentation that fits the input rather than human-designed heuristics.
AI.method: A hierarchical architecture ingests raw byte streams and performs compression-driven segmentation: boundaries are chosen based on the coding rate (information content) of latent representations. Top-K selection preserves a static computation graph while allowing adaptive chunking. The system self-tokenizes end-to-end without external tokenizers or heuristic rules.
AI.result: Across experiments, the compression-based chunking yields substantial gains, with ByteFlow Net surpassing BPE-based Transformers and earlier byte-level approaches.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end tokenizer-free modeling is practical and can be superior; information-theoretic, adaptive segmentation leads to stronger, more flexible language models.

title: PhyPrompt: RL-based Prompt Refinement for Physically Plausible Text-to-Video Generation
summary: State-of-the-art text-to-video (T2V) generators frequently violate physical laws despite high visual quality. We show this stems from insufficient physical constraints in prompts rather than model limitations: manually adding physics details reliably produces physically plausible videos, but requires expertise and does not scale. We present PhyPrompt, a two-stage reinforcement learning framework that automatically refines prompts for physically realistic generation. First, we fine-tune a large language model on a physics-focused Chain-of-Thought dataset to integrate principles like object motion and force interactions while preserving user intent. Second, we apply Group Relative Policy Optimization with a dynamic reward curriculum that initially prioritizes semantic fidelity, then progressively shifts toward physical commonsense. This curriculum achieves synergistic optimization: PhyPrompt-7B reaches 40.8\% joint success on VideoPhy2 (8.6pp gain), improving physical commonsense by 11pp (55.8\% to 66.8\%) while simultaneously increasing semantic adherence by 4.4pp (43.4\% to 47.8\%). Remarkably, our curriculum exceeds single-objective training on both metrics, demonstrating compositional prompt discovery beyond conventional multi-objective trade-offs. PhyPrompt outperforms GPT-4o (+3.8\% joint) and DeepSeek-V3 (+2.2\%, 100$\times$ larger) using only 7B parameters. The approach transfers zero-shot across diverse T2V architectures (Lavie, VideoCrafter2, CogVideoX-5B) with up to 16.8\% improvement, establishing that domain-specialized reinforcement learning with compositional curricula surpasses general-purpose scaling for physics-aware generation.

AI.tldr: PhyPrompt is a two-stage RL prompt-refinement system that injects physics awareness into text-to-video generation, improving both physical plausibility and semantic fidelity, outperforming larger general LLMs and transferring across T2V backends.
AI.motivation: SOTA text-to-video models often break physical laws because prompts lack explicit physics constraints. Manually adding such details works but needs expertise and doesn’t scale—motivating an automated, scalable prompt optimizer for physics realism without sacrificing user intent.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) Fine-tune a 7B LLM on a physics-focused Chain-of-Thought dataset to encode principles like motion, forces, and interactions while preserving intent. (2) Reinforce it with Group Relative Policy Optimization using a dynamic reward curriculum that starts by emphasizing semantic faithfulness and progressively shifts toward physical commonsense, yielding synergistic optimization. Evaluated zero-shot across multiple T2V generators.
AI.result: On VideoPhy2, PhyPrompt-7B attains 40.8% joint success (+8.6pp), lifts physical commonsense by 11pp (55.8%→66.8%) and semantic adherence by 4.4pp (43.4%→47.8%). The curriculum surpasses single-objective training on both metrics. Despite being 7B, it outperforms GPT-4o (+3.8% joint) and DeepSeek-V3 (+2.2%, ~100× larger). It transfers zero-shot to Lavie, VideoCrafter2, and CogVideoX-5B with up to 16.8% improvements.
AI.conclusion: Automated, domain-specialized RL with a compositional curriculum can discover prompts that simultaneously enhance physics realism and semantic fidelity—beating larger general models and avoiding the usual trade-off—while generalizing across diverse T2V architectures.

title: Fine-grained Image Aesthetic Assessment: Learning Discriminative Scores from Relative Ranks
comment: The paper has been accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Image aesthetic assessment (IAA) has extensive applications in content creation, album management, and recommendation systems, etc. In such applications, it is commonly needed to pick out the most aesthetically pleasing image from a series of images with subtle aesthetic variations, a topic we refer to as fine-grained IAA. Unfortunately, state-of-the-art IAA models are typically designed for coarse-grained evaluation, where images with notable aesthetic differences are evaluated independently on an absolute scale. These models are inherently limited in discriminating fine-grained aesthetic differences. To address the dilemma, we contribute FGAesthetics, a fine-grained IAA database with 32,217 images organized into 10,028 series, which are sourced from diverse categories including Natural, AIGC, and Cropping.
