Annotations are collected via pairwise comparisons within each series. We also devise Series Refinement and Rank Calibration to ensure the reliability of data and labels. Based on FGAesthetics, we further propose FGAesQ, a novel IAA framework that learns discriminative aesthetic scores from relative ranks through Difference-preserved Tokenization (DiffToken), Comparative Text-assisted Alignment (CTAlign), and Rank-aware Regression (RankReg). FGAesQ enables accurate aesthetic assessment in fine-grained scenarios while still maintains competitive performance in coarse-grained evaluation. Extensive experiments and comparisons demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: They introduce FGAesthetics, a new fine-grained image aesthetics dataset with pairwise rankings across 10k image series, and FGAesQ, a model that converts relative ranks into discriminative aesthetic scores using DiffToken, CTAlign, and RankReg, achieving superior fine-grained and competitive coarse-grained performance.
AI.motivation: Real applications (content creation, album curation, recommendation) require choosing the best image among very similar variants. Existing IAA models are trained and evaluated on coarse, absolute scores and struggle to distinguish subtle aesthetic differences within near-duplicate series.
AI.method: 1) Build FGAesthetics: 32,217 images grouped into 10,028 series from Natural, AI-generated (AIGC), and Cropping categories. Collect annotations via within-series pairwise comparisons. Apply Series Refinement and Rank Calibration to ensure consistent, reliable ranks. 2) Propose FGAesQ: learn aesthetic scores from relative ranks via Difference-preserved Tokenization (DiffToken) to encode subtle visual differences, Comparative Text-assisted Alignment (CTAlign) to align visual features with comparative textual cues, and Rank-aware Regression (RankReg) to map ranks to calibrated scores.
AI.result: On fine-grained IAA benchmarks derived from FGAesthetics, FGAesQ outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods; it also remains competitive on standard coarse-grained IAA tasks. Extensive experiments and comparisons substantiate these gains.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained IAA is better addressed by learning from relative, within-series comparisons. The FGAesthetics dataset with refined, calibrated ranks and the FGAesQ framework provide reliable supervision and modeling to capture subtle aesthetic differences, improving fine-grained assessment without sacrificing coarse-grained performance.

title: Belief-Sim: Towards Belief-Driven Simulation of Demographic Misinformation Susceptibility
summary: Misinformation is a growing societal threat, and susceptibility to misinformative claims varies across demographic groups due to differences in underlying beliefs. As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to simulate human behaviors, we investigate whether they can simulate demographic misinformation susceptibility, treating beliefs as a primary driving factor. We introduce BeliefSim, a simulation framework that constructs demographic belief profiles using psychology-informed taxonomies and survey priors. We study prompt-based conditioning and post-training adaptation, and conduct a multi-fold evaluation using: (i) susceptibility accuracy and (ii) counterfactual demographic sensitivity. Across both datasets and modeling strategies, we show that beliefs provide a strong prior for simulating misinformation susceptibility, with accuracy up to 92%.

AI.tldr: BeliefSim conditions LLMs with psychology-informed demographic belief profiles (via survey priors) to simulate demographic susceptibility to misinformation, achieving up to 92% accuracy and showing beliefs are strong priors.
AI.motivation: Misinformation harms society, and susceptibility varies by demographic group largely due to differing underlying beliefs. As LLMs are used to model human behavior, the authors ask whether LLMs can realistically simulate these group-level susceptibilities by explicitly modeling beliefs.
AI.method: They propose BeliefSim: construct demographic belief profiles using psychology-informed taxonomies and survey-based priors; apply these profiles via (a) prompt-based conditioning and (b) post-training adaptation; evaluate with two lenses—(i) susceptibility accuracy and (ii) counterfactual demographic sensitivity (how outcomes change when demographics/beliefs are counterfactually varied).
AI.result: Across datasets and both modeling strategies, belief conditioning substantially improves simulation fidelity, with susceptibility prediction accuracy reported up to 92% and meaningful counterfactual sensitivity to demographic-belief changes.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling demographic beliefs provides a powerful prior that enables LLMs to simulate demographic differences in misinformation susceptibility. This suggests belief-aware conditioning is an effective path for behavior simulation, though implications for fairness and ethical use require care.

title: N-gram Injection into Transformers for Dynamic Language Model Adaptation in Handwritten Text Recognition
summary: Transformer-based encoder-decoder networks have recently achieved impressive results in handwritten text recognition, partly thanks to their auto-regressive decoder which implicitly learns a language model. However, such networks suffer from a large performance drop when evaluated on a target corpus whose language distribution is shifted from the source text seen during training. To retain recognition accuracy despite this language shift, we propose an external n-gram injection (NGI) for dynamic adaptation of the network's language modeling at inference time. Our method allows switching to an n-gram language model estimated on a corpus close to the target distribution, therefore mitigating bias without any extra training on target image-text pairs. We opt for an early injection of the n-gram into the transformer decoder so that the network learns to fully leverage text-only data at the low additional cost of n-gram inference. Experiments on three handwritten datasets demonstrate that the proposed NGI significantly reduces the performance gap between source and target corpora.

AI.tldr: They plug an external n-gram language model into a transformer decoder at inference to adapt handwritten text recognition to new language distributions, closing much of the source–target performance gap without retraining.
AI.motivation: Transformer HTR models rely on an implicit language model in the autoregressive decoder, which causes accuracy to drop when test-time text statistics differ from the training corpus. A way to adapt language modeling on-the-fly using only text from the target domain is needed.
AI.method: External n-gram injection (NGI): estimate an n-gram LM on text close to the target distribution and inject its probabilities early in the transformer decoder during inference. This early fusion steers token prediction with minimal overhead and no additional training on image–text pairs, enabling dynamic language adaptation using text-only data.
AI.result: On three handwritten datasets, NGI markedly narrows the accuracy gap between source-trained models and target-domain evaluation under language shift, demonstrating improved robustness with low computational cost. (Exact metrics not provided in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: Early, inference-time fusion of an external n-gram LM effectively adapts transformer HTR to domain-shifted language distributions, reducing reliance on the decoder’s biased implicit LM and improving accuracy without retraining or paired target data.

title: A Neural Topic Method Using a Large-Language-Model-in-the-Loop for Business Research
summary: The growing use of unstructured text in business research makes topic modeling a central tool for constructing explanatory variables from reviews, social media, and open-ended survey responses, yet existing approaches function poorly as measurement instruments. Prior work shows that textual content predicts outcomes such as sales, satisfaction, and firm performance, but probabilistic models often generate conceptually diffuse topics, neural topic models are difficult to interpret in theory-driven settings, and large language model approaches lack standardization, stability, and alignment with document-level representations. We introduce LX Topic, a neural topic method that conceptualizes topics as latent linguistic constructs and produces calibrated document-level topic proportions for empirical analysis. LX Topic builds on FASTopic to ensure strong document representativeness and integrates large language model refinement at the topic-word level using alignment and confidence-weighting mechanisms that enhance semantic coherence without distorting document-topic distributions. Evaluations on large-scale Amazon and Yelp review datasets demonstrate that LX Topic achieves the highest overall topic quality relative to leading models while preserving clustering and classification performance. By unifying topic discovery, refinement, and standardized output in a web-based system, LX Topic establishes topic modeling as a reproducible, interpretable, and measurement-oriented instrument for marketing research and practice.

AI.tldr: LX Topic is a neural topic modeling approach that refines topic-word distributions with LLM guidance while preserving calibrated document-level topic proportions, yielding higher topic quality and stable, interpretable measurements for empirical marketing research.
AI.motivation: Business researchers increasingly rely on unstructured text (reviews, social media, open-ended surveys) but existing topic models underperform as measurement instruments: probabilistic models produce diffuse topics, neural models can be hard to interpret in theory-driven work, and LLM-based approaches lack standardization, stability, and alignment with document-level representations.
AI.method: Conceptualizes topics as latent linguistic constructs and builds on FASTopic to maintain strong document representativeness. Integrates LLM refinement at the topic–word level using alignment and confidence-weighting so semantic coherence is improved without distorting document–topic distributions. Produces calibrated document-level topic proportions and delivers a unified, web-based pipeline for discovery, refinement, and standardized outputs.
AI.result: On large Amazon and Yelp review datasets, LX Topic attains the highest overall topic quality versus leading baselines while preserving clustering and classification performance, indicating improved coherence without sacrificing downstream utility.
AI.conclusion: LX Topic offers a reproducible, interpretable, and measurement-oriented topic modeling instrument for marketing research by unifying discovery, refinement, and standardized outputs, enabling calibrated document-level variables suitable for empirical analysis.

title: DISC: Dense Integrated Semantic Context for Large-Scale Open-Set Semantic Mapping
summary: Open-set semantic mapping enables language-driven robotic perception, but current instance-centric approaches are bottlenecked by context-depriving and computationally expensive crop-based feature extraction. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we introduce DISC (Dense Integrated Semantic Context), featuring a novel single-pass, distance-weighted extraction mechanism. By deriving high-fidelity CLIP embeddings directly from the vision transformer's intermediate layers, our approach eliminates the latency and domain-shift artifacts of traditional image cropping, yielding pure, mask-aligned semantic representations. To fully leverage these features in large-scale continuous mapping, DISC is built upon a fully GPU-accelerated architecture that replaces periodic offline processing with precise, on-the-fly voxel-level instance refinement. We evaluate our approach on standard benchmarks (Replica, ScanNet) and a newly generated large-scale-mapping dataset based on Habitat-Matterport 3D (HM3DSEM) to assess scalability across complex scenes in multi-story buildings. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that DISC significantly surpasses current state-of-the-art zero-shot methods in both semantic accuracy and query retrieval, providing a robust, real-time capable framework for robotic deployment. The full source code, data generation and evaluation pipelines will be made available at https://github.com/DFKI-NI/DISC.

AI.tldr: DISC introduces a single-pass, context-preserving, GPU-accelerated pipeline that extracts mask-aligned CLIP embeddings from ViT intermediate layers to enable real-time, open-set semantic mapping, outperforming zero-shot SOTA on Replica, ScanNet, and a new HM3DSEM dataset.
AI.motivation: Open-set, language-driven mapping in robots is hindered by instance-centric, crop-based feature extraction that strips contextual cues, is computationally expensive, induces latency, and can cause domain-shift artifacts—limiting accuracy and scalability for large-scale environments.
AI.method: DISC (Dense Integrated Semantic Context) derives high-fidelity, mask-aligned CLIP embeddings directly from ViT intermediate features using a single-pass, distance-weighted extraction strategy, avoiding image cropping. It couples this with a fully GPU-accelerated mapping stack that performs precise, on-the-fly voxel-level instance refinement instead of periodic offline processing.
AI.result: Across Replica, ScanNet, and a newly curated large-scale HM3DSEM benchmark for multi-story buildings, DISC significantly outperforms state-of-the-art zero-shot methods in semantic accuracy and text query retrieval, while operating in real time.
AI.conclusion: By preserving dense semantic context and eliminating crop-induced latency and artifacts, DISC delivers a robust, scalable, real-time open-set semantic mapping framework suitable for robotic deployment; code and pipelines will be released for reproducibility.

title: Linguistically Informed Graph Model and Semantic Contrastive Learning for Korean Short Text Classification
comment: 16 pages, 1 Figure, Accepted at DASFAA 2026 (Full Research Paper)
summary: Short text classification (STC) remains a challenging task due to the scarcity of contextual information and labeled data. However, existing approaches have pre-dominantly focused on English because most benchmark datasets for the STC are primarily available in English. Consequently, existing methods seldom incorporate the linguistic and structural characteristics of Korean, such as its agglutinative morphology and flexible word order. To address these limitations, we propose LIGRAM, a hierarchical heterogeneous graph model for Korean short-text classification. The proposed model constructs sub-graphs at the morpheme, part-of-speech, and named-entity levels and hierarchically integrates them to compensate for the limited contextual information in short texts while precisely capturing the grammatical and semantic dependencies inherent in Korean. In addition, we apply Semantics-aware Contrastive Learning (SemCon) to reflect semantic similarity across documents, enabling the model to establish clearer decision boundaries even in short texts where class distinctions are often ambiguous. We evaluate LIGRAM on four Korean short-text datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing baseline models. These outcomes validate that integrating language-specific graph representations with SemCon provides an effective solution for short text classification in agglutinative languages such as Korean.

AI.tldr: LIGRAM is a Korean short-text classifier that builds hierarchical heterogeneous graphs (morpheme, POS, NER) and trains with semantics-aware contrastive learning to compensate for limited context; it consistently outperforms baselines on four Korean datasets.
AI.motivation: Short texts lack context and labeled data, and most STC methods target English, overlooking Korean’s agglutinative morphology and flexible word order. The authors aim to exploit Korean-specific linguistic structure to improve classification and create clearer decision boundaries in ambiguous short texts.
AI.method: Construct language-aware subgraphs at morpheme, part-of-speech, and named-entity levels for each document, then hierarchically integrate them into a heterogeneous graph that captures grammatical and semantic dependencies. Employ Semantics-aware Contrastive Learning (SemCon) to leverage inter-document semantic similarity and enforce better class separation during training. Evaluate on four Korean STC datasets against strong baselines.
AI.result: Across all four Korean short-text datasets, LIGRAM consistently surpasses baseline models (no specific metrics reported in the abstract), indicating improved classification performance due to the hierarchical graph design and SemCon.
AI.conclusion: Combining Korean-specific hierarchical graph representations with semantics-aware contrastive learning effectively addresses short-text classification in agglutinative languages, yielding robust gains over existing approaches and suggesting broader applicability beyond English.

title: Cross-Modal Mapping and Dual-Branch Reconstruction for 2D-3D Multimodal Industrial Anomaly Detection
summary: Multimodal industrial anomaly detection benefits from integrating RGB appearance with 3D surface geometry, yet existing \emph{unsupervised} approaches commonly rely on memory banks, teacher-student architectures, or fragile fusion schemes, limiting robustness under noisy depth, weak texture, or missing modalities. This paper introduces \textbf{CMDR-IAD}, a lightweight and modality-flexible unsupervised framework for reliable anomaly detection in 2D+3D multimodal as well as single-modality (2D-only or 3D-only) settings. \textbf{CMDR-IAD} combines bidirectional 2D$\leftrightarrow$3D cross-modal mapping to model appearance-geometry consistency with dual-branch reconstruction that independently captures normal texture and geometric structure. A two-part fusion strategy integrates these cues: a reliability-gated mapping anomaly highlights spatially consistent texture-geometry discrepancies, while a confidence-weighted reconstruction anomaly adaptively balances appearance and geometric deviations, yielding stable and precise anomaly localization even in depth-sparse or low-texture regions. On the MVTec 3D-AD benchmark, CMDR-IAD achieves state-of-the-art performance while operating without memory banks, reaching 97.3\% image-level AUROC (I-AUROC), 99.6\% pixel-level AUROC (P-AUROC), and 97.6\% AUPRO. On a real-world polyurethane cutting dataset, the 3D-only variant attains 92.6\% I-AUROC and 92.5\% P-AUROC, demonstrating strong effectiveness under practical industrial conditions. These results highlight the framework's robustness, modality flexibility, and the effectiveness of the proposed fusion strategies for industrial visual inspection. Our source code is available at https://github.com/ECGAI-Research/CMDR-IAD/

AI.tldr: CMDR-IAD is a lightweight, unsupervised anomaly detection framework that fuses RGB appearance and 3D geometry via bidirectional cross-modal mapping and dual-branch reconstruction with reliability/confidence gating, delivering state-of-the-art performance without memory banks and remaining robust when modalities are noisy, sparse, or missing.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised multimodal IAD methods often depend on memory banks, teacher–student setups, or fragile fusion, which reduces robustness under noisy depth, weak texture, or absent modalities. A modality-flexible, more reliable fusion and detection approach is needed.
AI.method: The framework learns bidirectional 2D↔3D cross-modal mappings to enforce appearance–geometry consistency and employs two independent reconstruction branches for texture (2D) and geometry (3D). A two-part fusion computes: (1) a reliability-gated mapping anomaly emphasizing spatially consistent texture–geometry discrepancies, and (2) a confidence-weighted reconstruction anomaly that adaptively balances appearance and geometric deviations. The design operates in 2D+3D or single-modality modes without memory banks or teacher–student architectures.
AI.result: On MVTec 3D-AD, CMDR-IAD achieves 97.3% image-level AUROC, 99.6% pixel-level AUROC, and 97.6% AUPRO, reaching SOTA. On a real-world polyurethane cutting dataset, the 3D-only variant attains 92.6% I-AUROC and 92.5% P-AUROC, showing strong practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: CMDR-IAD provides robust, modality-flexible unsupervised anomaly detection with stable, precise localization. Its reliability/confidence-gated fusion and cross-modal consistency modeling enable SOTA benchmark results and solid real-world performance; it works even when a modality is noisy or missing. Source code is publicly available.

title: MIND: Unified Inquiry and Diagnosis RL with Criteria Grounded Clinical Supports for Psychiatric Consultation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have advanced medical dialogue systems, yet psychiatric consultation poses substantially higher demands due to subjective ambiguity and comorbidity complexity: an agent must continuously extract psychopathological cues from incomplete and inconsistent patient reports in multi-turn interactions and perform rigorous differential diagnostic reasoning. However, existing methods face two fundamental challenges. First, without criteria-grounded clinical supports, they are prone to unsupported clinical assertions when symptoms are atypical or underspecified. Second, in multi-turn interactions, they struggle to mitigate inquiry drift (off-topic or low-yield questioning) and optimize questioning strategies. To address these challenges, we propose MIND, a unified inquiry--diagnosis reinforcement learning framework for psychiatric consultation. Specifically, we build a Criteria-Grounded Psychiatric Reasoning Bank (PRB) that summarizes dialogue context into clinical retrieval states, retrieves semantically similar reference consultations, and distills reusable criteria-grounded clinical supports to guide criteria-aligned inquiry and reasoning. Building on this foundation, MIND enforces explicit clinical reasoning with rubric-based process rewards to provide fine-grained supervision over intermediate decision steps, and incorporates a value-aware trajectory rectification mechanism to jointly improve information acquisition and diagnostic decision-making across turns. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MIND consistently outperforms strong baselines in diagnostic accuracy, empathetic interaction quality, interpretability, and generalization.

AI.tldr: MIND is a reinforcement-learning framework for psychiatric consultations that grounds inquiry and diagnosis in a curated psychiatric reasoning bank, enforces explicit clinical reasoning with process rewards, and rectifies trajectories to jointly improve questioning and differential diagnosis, outperforming strong baselines across accuracy, empathy, interpretability, and generalization.
AI.motivation: Psychiatric dialogue is uniquely hard for LLMs due to subjective, incomplete, and inconsistent symptom reports and complex comorbidities. Existing systems make unsupported clinical claims without criteria-based support and suffer inquiry drift during multi-turn interactions, reducing diagnostic quality.
AI.method: Build a Criteria-Grounded Psychiatric Reasoning Bank (PRB) that summarizes dialogue into retrieval states, retrieves semantically similar consultations, and distills reusable, criteria-aligned clinical supports. Train with reinforcement learning that (1) enforces explicit step-by-step clinical reasoning via rubric-based process rewards, and (2) uses value-aware trajectory rectification to co-optimize information gathering (questioning) and diagnostic decisions over turns.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, MIND consistently surpasses strong baselines in diagnostic accuracy, empathetic interaction quality, interpretability (transparent reasoning aligned to clinical criteria), and generalization to new cases.
AI.conclusion: Grounding psychiatric consultation in a criteria-based reasoning bank and supervising intermediate reasoning with RL enables more focused inquiries, reduced drift, and more reliable differential diagnosis; MIND offers a unified, effective framework for safer and more interpretable psychiatric dialogue systems.

title: Slice-wise quality assessment of high b-value breast DWI via deep learning-based artifact detection
summary: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) can support lesion detection and characterization in breast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), however especially high b-value diffusion-weighted acquisitions can be prone to intensity artifacts that can affect diagnostic image assessment. This study aims to detect both hyper- and hypointense artifacts on high b-value diffusion-weighted images (b=1500 s/mm2) using deep learning, employing either a binary classification (artifact presence) or a multiclass classification (artifact intensity) approach on a slice-wise dataset.This IRB-approved retrospective study used the single-center dataset comprising n=11806 slices from routine 3T breast MRI examinations performed between 2022 and mid-2023. Three convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures (DenseNet121, ResNet18, and SEResNet50) were trained for binary classification of hyper- and hypointense artifacts. The best performing model (DenseNet121) was applied to an independent holdout test set and was further trained separately for multiclass classification. Evaluation included area under receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC), area under precision recall curve (AUPRC), precision, and recall, as well as analysis of predicted bounding box positions, derived from the network Grad-CAM heatmaps. DenseNet121 achieved AUROCs of 0.92 and 0.94 for hyper- and hypointense artifact detection, respectively, and weighted AUROCs of 0.85 and 0.88 for multiclass classification on single-slice high b-value diffusion-weighted images. A radiologist evaluated bounding box precision on a 1-5 Likert-like scale across 200 slices, achieving mean scores of 3.33+-1.04 for hyperintense artifacts and 2.62+-0.81 for hypointense artifacts. Hyper- and hypointense artifact detection in slice-wise breast DWI MRI dataset (b=1500 s/mm2) using CNNs particularly DenseNet121, seems promising and requires further validation.

AI.tldr: CNNs, particularly DenseNet121, detect hyper- and hypointense artifacts on high b-value (b=1500 s/mm²) breast DWI slices with strong discrimination (AUROC ~0.92–0.94 for binary; ~0.85–0.88 for multiclass). Grad-CAM-based localization is moderate. Single-center, slice-wise study; further validation is needed.
AI.motivation: High b-value breast DWI aids lesion detection/characterization but is susceptible to intensity artifacts that impair diagnostic assessment. Automated detection could support image quality assurance and reduce reader burden/errors.
AI.method: IRB-approved retrospective, single-center dataset of 11,806 slices from routine 3T breast MRI (2022–mid-2023). Trained CNNs (DenseNet121, ResNet18, SEResNet50) for binary detection of hyper- and hypointense artifacts; best model (DenseNet121) evaluated on an independent holdout set and retrained for multiclass artifact-intensity classification. Metrics: AUROC, AUPRC, precision, recall. Grad-CAM heatmaps produced bounding boxes; a radiologist rated localization quality on a 1–5 Likert scale for 200 slices.
AI.result: DenseNet121 achieved AUROCs of 0.92 (hyperintense) and 0.94 (hypointense) for binary detection; weighted AUROCs of 0.85 (hyper) and 0.88 (hypo) for multiclass classification. Radiologist-scored Grad-CAM box precision averaged 3.33±1.04 (hyper) and 2.62±0.81 (hypo).
AI.conclusion: CNN-based detection of hyper-/hypointense artifacts on high b-value breast DWI is feasible and shows promising accuracy; localization is moderate. Broader, prospective, and multi-center validation and integration into QA workflows are warranted.

title: Order Is Not Layout: Order-to-Space Bias in Image Generation
summary: We study a systematic bias in modern image generation models: the mention order of entities in text spuriously determines spatial layout and entity--role binding. We term this phenomenon Order-to-Space Bias (OTS) and show that it arises in both text-to-image and image-to-image generation, often overriding grounded cues and causing incorrect layouts or swapped assignments. To quantify OTS, we introduce OTS-Bench, which isolates order effects with paired prompts differing only in entity order and evaluates models along two dimensions: homogenization and correctness. Experiments show that Order-to-Space Bias (OTS) is widespread in modern image generation models, and provide evidence that it is primarily data-driven and manifests during the early stages of layout formation. Motivated by this insight, we show that both targeted fine-tuning and early-stage intervention strategies can substantially reduce OTS, while preserving generation quality.

AI.tldr: Identifies and quantifies a systemic “order-to-space” bias in image generation where the textual mention order spuriously dictates spatial layout and role binding; proposes a benchmark and shows early-stage, data-driven origins, with effective mitigations that retain image quality.
AI.motivation: Despite prompts intending only to specify entities and roles, modern image generators often map the textual order of mentions to spatial positions or assignments, causing swapped roles or incorrect layouts. The field lacks a standardized way to measure and remediate this spurious correlation.
AI.method: Define Order-to-Space Bias (OTS). Build OTS-Bench with paired prompts that only differ in entity order; evaluate along homogenization and correctness dimensions. Conduct experiments across text-to-image and image-to-image models; analyze when OTS emerges in the generation pipeline. Propose and test targeted fine-tuning and early-stage intervention strategies.
AI.result: OTS is prevalent across modern image generation systems and can override grounded cues, leading to incorrect layouts or role swaps. Evidence indicates OTS is primarily data-driven and appears during early layout formation. Both targeted fine-tuning and early-stage interventions substantially reduce OTS while preserving overall generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Order-driven layout and role assignment is a systematic, early-stage, data-induced failure mode in image generation. OTS-Bench enables reproducible measurement. Practical mitigations—targeted fine-tuning and early-stage interventions—can curb OTS without degrading quality, pointing toward better datasets and training strategies to decouple entity order from spatial arrangement.

title: Spatial Causal Prediction in Video
summary: Spatial reasoning, the ability to understand spatial relations, causality, and dynamic evolution, is central to human intelligence and essential for real-world applications such as autonomous driving and robotics. Existing studies, however, primarily assess models on visible spatio-temporal understanding, overlooking their ability to infer unseen past or future spatial states. In this work, we introduce Spatial Causal Prediction (SCP), a new task paradigm that challenges models to reason beyond observation and predict spatial causal outcomes. We further construct SCP-Bench, a benchmark comprising 2,500 QA pairs across 1,181 videos spanning diverse viewpoints, scenes, and causal directions, to support systematic evaluation. Through comprehensive experiments on {23} state-of-the-art models, we reveal substantial gaps between human and model performance, limited temporal extrapolation, and weak causal grounding. We further analyze key factors influencing performance and propose perception-enhancement and reasoning-guided strategies toward advancing spatial causal intelligence. The project page is https://guangstrip.github.io/SCP-Bench.

AI.tldr: They introduce Spatial Causal Prediction (SCP)—a task and benchmark (SCP-Bench) for predicting unseen past/future spatial states from video—and show current SOTA models lag far behind humans, struggle with temporal extrapolation, and lack causal grounding; they analyze factors and propose perception- and reasoning-focused improvements.
AI.motivation: Spatial reasoning underpins human intelligence and applications like driving/robotics, yet evaluations mostly test visible spatio-temporal understanding rather than inferring unobserved past/future states. The paper aims to fill this gap and push models toward causal spatial intelligence.
AI.method: 1) Define the SCP task to require inferring causal spatial outcomes beyond observed frames. 2) Build SCP-Bench: 2,500 QA pairs over 1,181 videos spanning diverse viewpoints, scenes, and causal directions. 3) Evaluate 23 state-of-the-art models. 4) Analyze factors affecting performance. 5) Propose perception-enhancement and reasoning-guided strategies.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, models show large human-model performance gaps, limited ability to extrapolate temporally, and weak causal grounding, as revealed by systematic evaluation on 23 SOTA systems.
AI.conclusion: Current vision-language/video models are not yet capable of robust spatial causal prediction. SCP-Bench provides a rigorous testbed, and improving low-level perception and structured reasoning is a promising path to advance spatial causal intelligence.

title: ErrorLLM: Modeling SQL Errors for Text-to-SQL Refinement
summary: Despite the remarkable performance of large language models (LLMs) in text-to-SQL (SQL generation), correctly producing SQL queries remains challenging during initial generation. The SQL refinement task is subsequently introduced to correct syntactic and semantic errors in generated SQL queries. However, existing paradigms face two major limitations: (i) self-debugging becomes increasingly ineffective as modern LLMs rarely produce explicit execution errors that can trigger debugging signals; (ii) self-correction exhibits low detection precision due to the lack of explicit error modeling grounded in the question and schema, and suffers from severe hallucination that frequently corrupts correct SQLs. In this paper, we propose ErrorLLM, a framework that explicitly models text-to-SQL Errors within a dedicated LLM for text-to-SQL refinement. Specifically, we represent the user question and database schema as structural features, employ static detection to identify execution failures and surface mismatches, and extend ErrorLLM's semantic space with dedicated error tokens that capture categorized implicit semantic error types. Through a well-designed training strategy, we explicitly model these errors with structural representations, enabling the LLM to detect complex implicit errors by predicting dedicated error tokens. Guided by the detected errors, we perform error-guided refinement on the SQL structure by prompting LLMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ErrorLLM achieves the most significant improvements over backbone initial generation. Further analysis reveals that detection quality directly determines refinement effectiveness, and ErrorLLM addresses both sides by high detection F1 score while maintain refinement effectiveness.

AI.tldr: ErrorLLM introduces an error-aware LLM for text-to-SQL refinement that explicitly models and detects both explicit and implicit SQL errors using structural signals and learned error tokens, then performs targeted refinements, yielding higher error-detection F1 and stronger post-generation SQL accuracy over backbones.
AI.motivation: Initial LLM-generated SQL often contains subtle semantic mistakes that don’t trigger runtime errors, making self-debugging ineffective. Existing self-correction lacks explicit error modeling grounded in the question and schema, causing low-precision detection and hallucinations that can damage correct SQL.
AI.method: Represent question and schema as structural features; use static detection to catch execution failures and surface mismatches; expand the LLM’s semantic space with dedicated error tokens that categorize implicit semantic errors; train the LLM to predict these tokens conditioned on structural representations; refine SQL via prompts guided by the detected error categories to adjust the query’s structure.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, ErrorLLM delivers the largest gains over baseline initial generation, achieves high error-detection F1, and shows that better detection quality directly improves refinement effectiveness while maintaining or improving final SQL correctness.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, structured error modeling with dedicated tokens enables reliable detection of implicit SQL errors and targeted refinements, overcoming the limitations of self-debugging and unguided self-correction and leading to stronger text-to-SQL refinement performance.

title: Image-based Prompt Injection: Hijacking Multimodal LLMs through Visually Embedded Adversarial Instructions
comment: 7 pages, published in 2025 3rd International Conference on Foundation and Large Language Models (FLLM), Vienna, Austria
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) integrate vision and text to power applications, but this integration introduces new vulnerabilities. We study Image-based Prompt Injection (IPI), a black-box attack in which adversarial instructions are embedded into natural images to override model behavior. Our end-to-end IPI pipeline incorporates segmentation-based region selection, adaptive font scaling, and background-aware rendering to conceal prompts from human perception while preserving model interpretability. Using the COCO dataset and GPT-4-turbo, we evaluate 12 adversarial prompt strategies and multiple embedding configurations. The results show that IPI can reliably manipulate the output of the model, with the most effective configuration achieving up to 64\% attack success under stealth constraints. These findings highlight IPI as a practical threat in black-box settings and underscore the need for defenses against multimodal prompt injection.

AI.tldr: Adversarial instructions, rendered imperceptibly inside natural images, can reliably steer multimodal LLMs in black-box settings; with a stealthy rendering pipeline, attacks attain up to 64% success on GPT‑4‑turbo over COCO, highlighting urgent needs for defenses.
AI.motivation: As vision–language integration grows, MLLMs become susceptible to prompt injection through the visual channel. The authors aim to demonstrate and quantify a practical, black-box image-based prompt injection (IPI) that remains hidden from humans yet interpretable by models, to assess real-world risk.
AI.method: They build an end-to-end IPI pipeline that: (1) chooses image regions via segmentation, (2) adaptively scales font, and (3) uses background-aware rendering to conceal text while keeping it machine-interpretable. They test 12 adversarial prompt strategies and various embedding configurations on COCO using GPT‑4‑turbo.
AI.result: Across settings, IPI reliably manipulates model outputs; the most effective configuration achieves up to 64% attack success under stealth constraints, indicating substantial vulnerability in black-box conditions.
AI.conclusion: IPI constitutes a practical threat to MLLMs even without white-box access. The work underscores the need for multimodal prompt-injection defenses (e.g., input sanitization, rendering-aware detection, robust training, or safety filters).

title: Towards Generalized Multimodal Homography Estimation
summary: Supervised and unsupervised homography estimation methods depend on image pairs tailored to specific modalities to achieve high accuracy. However, their performance deteriorates substantially when applied to unseen modalities. To address this issue, we propose a training data synthesis method that generates unaligned image pairs with ground-truth offsets from a single input image. Our approach renders the image pairs with diverse textures and colors while preserving their structural information. These synthetic data empower the trained model to achieve greater robustness and improved generalization across various domains. Additionally, we design a network to fully leverage cross-scale information and decouple color information from feature representations, thus improving estimation accuracy. Extensive experiments show that our training data synthesis method improves generalization performance. The results also confirm the effectiveness of the proposed network.

AI.tldr: Generate synthetic, structurally consistent but geometrically offset image pairs from single images and train a cross‑scale, color‑decoupled network to achieve robust, modality‑agnostic homography estimation with improved generalization and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Conventional supervised/unsupervised homography estimators rely on modality‑specific paired data and degrade badly on unseen modalities, highlighting a need for training that emphasizes structure over appearance to generalize across domains.
AI.method: 1) A training data synthesis pipeline that, from a single image, renders unaligned image pairs with known ground‑truth offsets while diversifying textures and colors but preserving structural layouts. 2) A homography network that fully exploits cross‑scale information and explicitly decouples color/appearance from feature representations to focus on structural cues.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, the synthesized training data substantially boosts cross‑domain robustness and generalization of homography estimation, and the proposed cross‑scale, color‑decoupled network further improves estimation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: By replacing modality‑specific paired data with structure‑preserving synthetic pairs and using a network architecture that isolates color from geometry and aggregates multi‑scale features, the approach delivers stronger, more generalizable homography estimation on unseen modalities.

title: Confidence-Calibrated Small-Large Language Model Collaboration for Cost-Efficient Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate superior reasoning capabilities compared to small language models (SLMs), but incur substantially higher costs. We propose COllaborative REAsoner (COREA), a system that cascades an SLM with an LLM to achieve a balance between accuracy and cost in complex reasoning tasks. COREA first attempts to answer questions using the SLM, which outputs both an answer and a verbalized confidence score. Questions with confidence below a predefined threshold are deferred to the LLM for more accurate resolution. We introduce a reinforcement learning-based training algorithm that aligns the SLM's confidence through an additional confidence calibration reward. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method jointly improves the SLM's reasoning ability and confidence calibration across diverse datasets and model backbones. Compared to using the LLM alone, COREA reduces cost by 21.5% and 16.8% on out-of-domain math and non-math datasets, respectively, with only an absolute pass@1 drop within 2%.

AI.tldr: COREA cascades an SLM with an LLM, using an RL-calibrated, self-reported confidence to gate queries; it trims inference cost by ~17–22% with ≤2% pass@1 drop while improving the SLM’s reasoning and calibration.
AI.motivation: LLMs have superior reasoning but high inference cost. To reduce spend without sacrificing accuracy on hard problems, a system needs the cheaper SLM to solve easy cases and reliably recognize when to defer—requiring well-calibrated confidence estimates.
AI.method: COREA has the SLM answer first and verbalize a confidence score; if it falls below a threshold, the query is escalated to the LLM. The SLM is trained with reinforcement learning that adds a confidence calibration reward to align stated confidence with correctness, enabling effective thresholding. Evaluations span diverse datasets and model backbones.
AI.result: Against an LLM-only baseline, COREA cuts cost by 21.5% on out-of-domain math and 16.8% on non-math datasets, with an absolute pass@1 decrease no greater than 2%. The SLM’s reasoning ability and confidence calibration both improve.
AI.conclusion: Confidence-gated SLM→LLM cascades provide a practical, general approach to balance cost and accuracy in complex reasoning, yielding substantial savings with minimal accuracy loss.

title: Field imaging framework for morphological characterization of aggregates with computer vision: Algorithms and applications
summary: Construction aggregates, including sand and gravel, crushed stone and riprap, are the core building blocks of the construction industry. State-of-the-practice characterization methods mainly relies on visual inspection and manual measurement. State-of-the-art aggregate imaging methods have limitations that are only applicable to regular-sized aggregates under well-controlled conditions. This dissertation addresses these major challenges by developing a field imaging framework for the morphological characterization of aggregates as a multi-scenario solution. For individual and non-overlapping aggregates, a field imaging system was designed and the associated segmentation and volume estimation algorithms were developed. For 2D image analyses of aggregates in stockpiles, an automated 2D instance segmentation and morphological analysis approach was established. For 3D point cloud analyses of aggregate stockpiles, an integrated 3D Reconstruction-Segmentation-Completion (RSC-3D) approach was established: 3D reconstruction procedures from multi-view images, 3D stockpile instance segmentation, and 3D shape completion to predict the unseen sides. First, a 3D reconstruction procedure was developed to obtain high-fidelity 3D models of collected aggregate samples, based on which a 3D aggregate particle library was constructed. Next, two datasets were derived from the 3D particle library for 3D learning: a synthetic dataset of aggregate stockpiles with ground-truth instance labels, and a dataset of partial-complete shape pairs, developed with varying-view raycasting schemes. A state-of-the-art 3D instance segmentation network and a 3D shape completion network were trained on the datasets, respectively. The application of the integrated approach was demonstrated on real stockpiles and validated with ground-truth, showing good performance in capturing and predicting the unseen sides of aggregates.

AI.tldr: A multi-scenario, field-ready imaging framework is proposed to characterize construction aggregates by unifying 2D/3D imaging, instance segmentation, and 3D shape completion; it builds synthetic training datasets from a high-fidelity 3D particle library and demonstrates strong performance on real stockpiles, including predicting occluded particle geometry.
AI.motivation: Conventional aggregate characterization depends on manual/visual inspection or controlled-lab imaging and does not scale to irregular, field stockpiles with occlusions. There is a need for accurate, automated morphology and volume estimation under uncontrolled outdoor conditions.
AI.method: Three complementary tracks: (1) Individual, non-overlapping aggregates—custom field imaging system with segmentation and volume estimation; (2) 2D stockpile analysis—automated instance segmentation and morphological measurements from images; (3) 3D stockpile analysis—an integrated Reconstruction–Segmentation–Completion (RSC-3D) pipeline that performs multi-view 3D reconstruction, 3D instance segmentation, and 3D shape completion to infer unseen sides. A high-fidelity 3D particle library was built via reconstruction; from it, two datasets were derived: synthetic stockpiles with ground-truth instance labels, and partial–complete shape pairs via varying-view raycasting. State-of-the-art 3D instance segmentation and 3D shape completion networks were trained and deployed on real stockpiles.
AI.result: The approach yields high-quality 3D models and a reusable 3D particle library. Trained networks segment individual particles in stockpiles and accurately predict occluded geometry. Validation against ground truth on real stockpiles shows good performance, particularly in capturing and completing unseen surfaces, though no quantitative metrics are provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The framework overcomes limitations of manual or lab-only methods by delivering robust, field-deployable aggregate morphology characterization across scenarios. The constructed datasets and trained models generalize to real stockpiles, enabling accurate instance-level analysis and completion suitable for practical deployment.

title: ProFound: A moderate-sized vision foundation model for multi-task prostate imaging
summary: Many diagnostic and therapeutic clinical tasks for prostate cancer increasingly rely on multi-parametric MRI. Automating these tasks is challenging because they necessitate expert interpretations, which are difficult to scale to capitalise on modern deep learning. Although modern automated systems achieve expert-level performance in isolated tasks, their general clinical utility remains limited by the requirement of large task-specific labelled datasets. In this paper, we present ProFound, a domain-specialised vision foundation model for volumetric prostate mpMRI. ProFound is pre-trained using several variants of self-supervised approaches on a diverse, multi-institutional collection of 5,000 patients, with a total of over 22,000 unique 3D MRI volumes (over 1,800,000 2D image slices). We conducted a systematic evaluation of ProFound across a broad spectrum of $11$ downstream clinical tasks on over 3,000 independent patients, including prostate cancer detection, Gleason grading, lesion localisation, gland volume estimation, zonal and surrounding structure segmentation. Experimental results demonstrate that finetuned ProFound consistently outperforms or remains competitive with state-of-the-art specialised models and existing medical vision foundation models trained/finetuned on the same data.

AI.tldr: ProFound is a domain-specialized vision foundation model for 3D prostate mpMRI, pre-trained self-supervised on 22k volumes from 5k patients and finetuned to 11 clinical tasks, where it matches or surpasses state-of-the-art specialized and foundation baselines.
AI.motivation: Prostate-cancer care increasingly depends on mpMRI, but automating diverse clinical tasks is hampered by the need for large task-specific expert labels. A generalizable model that learns from unlabeled data and adapts to many tasks could reduce labeling burden and improve clinical utility.
AI.method: Build ProFound, a volumetric prostate mpMRI foundation model. Pre-train with multiple self-supervised learning variants on a multi-institutional dataset (≈5,000 patients; >22,000 unique 3D volumes; >1.8M slices). Evaluate by finetuning on 11 downstream tasks: cancer detection, Gleason grading, lesion localization, gland volume estimation, zonal and surrounding-structure segmentation, across >3,000 independent patients.
AI.result: Across the 11 tasks, finetuned ProFound consistently outperforms or remains competitive with state-of-the-art specialized models and existing medical vision foundation models when trained/finetuned on the same data.
AI.conclusion: A prostate mpMRI–specialized foundation model pre-trained self-supervised at scale can generalize across many downstream clinical tasks and deliver superior or competitive performance, suggesting a scalable path beyond task-specific labeled datasets for broader clinical utility.

title: T2S-Bench & Structure-of-Thought: Benchmarking and Prompting Comprehensive Text-to-Structure Reasoning
comment: Dataset and Code have been released at https://t2s-bench.github.io/T2S-Bench-Page/
summary: Think about how human handles complex reading tasks: marking key points, inferring their relationships, and structuring information to guide understanding and responses. Likewise, can a large language model benefit from text structure to enhance text-processing performance? To explore it, in this work, we first introduce Structure of Thought (SoT), a prompting technique that explicitly guides models to construct intermediate text structures, consistently boosting performance across eight tasks and three model families. Building upon this insight, we present T2S-Bench, the first benchmark designed to evaluate and improve text-to-structure capabilities of models. T2S-Bench includes 1.8K samples across 6 scientific domains and 32 structural types, rigorously constructed to ensure accuracy, fairness, and quality. Evaluation on 45 mainstream models reveals substantial improvement potential: the average accuracy on the multi-hop reasoning task is only 52.1%, and even the most advanced model achieves 58.1% node accuracy in end-to-end extraction. Furthermore, on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, SoT alone yields an average +5.7% improvement across eight diverse text-processing tasks, and fine-tuning on T2S-Bench further increases this gain to +8.6%. These results highlight the value of explicit text structuring and the complementary contributions of SoT and T2S-Bench. Dataset and eval code have been released at https://t2s-bench.github.io/T2S-Bench-Page/.

AI.tldr: They propose Structure of Thought (SoT), a prompting method that makes LLMs explicitly build intermediate text structures, and introduce T2S-Bench to evaluate text-to-structure abilities. SoT improves performance across tasks, and fine-tuning on T2S-Bench further boosts gains, though overall accuracy shows large headroom.
AI.motivation: Humans read complex text by marking key points and organizing structure; LLMs may similarly benefit from explicit structuring. There is no dedicated benchmark to systematically assess and train models on converting text into structured representations.
AI.method: 1) SoT: a prompting technique that guides models to construct intermediate structures during reasoning/processing. 2) T2S-Bench: a 1.8K-sample benchmark spanning 6 scientific domains and 32 structure types, designed for accuracy and fairness. 3) Evaluation: test 45 models on multi-hop reasoning and end-to-end structure extraction; assess SoT’s effect and the impact of fine-tuning on T2S-Bench (e.g., with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct).
AI.result: Average multi-hop reasoning accuracy across models is 52.1%; the best model reaches 58.1% node accuracy in end-to-end extraction. On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, SoT yields +5.7% average improvement over eight text-processing tasks; fine-tuning on T2S-Bench increases average gains to +8.6%.
AI.conclusion: Explicit text structuring materially benefits LLM text-processing. SoT and T2S-Bench are complementary: SoT provides immediate prompting gains, while T2S-Bench enables further improvements via training. Significant room for progress remains; dataset and evaluation code are publicly available.

title: InEdit-Bench: Benchmarking Intermediate Logical Pathways for Intelligent Image Editing Models
summary: Multimodal generative models have made significant strides in image editing, demonstrating impressive performance on a variety of static tasks. However, their proficiency typically does not extend to complex scenarios requiring dynamic reasoning, leaving them ill-equipped to model the coherent, intermediate logical pathways that constitute a multi-step evolution from an initial state to a final one. This capacity is crucial for unlocking a deeper level of procedural and causal understanding in visual manipulation. To systematically measure this critical limitation, we introduce InEdit-Bench, the first evaluation benchmark dedicated to reasoning over intermediate pathways in image editing. InEdit-Bench comprises meticulously annotated test cases covering four fundamental task categories: state transition, dynamic process, temporal sequence, and scientific simulation. Additionally, to enable fine-grained evaluation, we propose a set of assessment criteria to evaluate the logical coherence and visual naturalness of the generated pathways, as well as the model's fidelity to specified path constraints. Our comprehensive evaluation of 14 representative image editing models on InEdit-Bench reveals significant and widespread shortcomings in this domain. By providing a standardized and challenging benchmark, we aim for InEdit-Bench to catalyze research and steer development towards more dynamic, reason-aware, and intelligent multimodal generative models.

AI.tldr: They introduce InEdit-Bench, a benchmark to test whether image editing models can generate coherent intermediate steps—not just end results—across four dynamic reasoning tasks, with criteria for logical consistency, visual realism, and adherence to path constraints; testing 14 models exposes broad shortcomings.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal generative models perform well on static edits but struggle with dynamic, multi-step reasoning that requires modeling coherent intermediate states. There is no systematic way to evaluate this capability, which is essential for procedural and causal understanding in visual manipulation.
AI.method: Create InEdit-Bench with carefully annotated test cases spanning four categories: state transition, dynamic process, temporal sequence, and scientific simulation. Define fine-grained assessment criteria covering logical coherence, visual naturalness, and fidelity to specified path constraints. Use the benchmark to evaluate 14 representative image editing models.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, all or most evaluated models exhibit substantial deficits in reasoning about and generating coherent intermediate pathways, as reflected by the proposed criteria.
AI.conclusion: InEdit-Bench establishes a standardized, challenging evaluation for dynamic, reasoning-aware image editing and is intended to drive research toward models that can plan and realize multi-step, causally consistent visual edits.

title: BLOCK: An Open-Source Bi-Stage MLLM Character-to-Skin Pipeline for Minecraft
summary: We present \textbf{BLOCK}, an open-source bi-stage character-to-skin pipeline that generates pixel-perfect Minecraft skins from arbitrary character concepts. BLOCK decomposes the problem into (i) a \textbf{3D preview synthesis stage} driven by a large multimodal model (MLLM) with a carefully designed prompt-and-reference template, producing a consistent dual-panel (front/back) oblique-view Minecraft-style preview; and (ii) a \textbf{skin decoding stage} based on a fine-tuned FLUX.2 model that translates the preview into a skin atlas image. We further propose \textbf{EvolveLoRA}, a progressive LoRA curriculum (text-to-image $\rightarrow$ image-to-image $\rightarrow$ preview-to-skin) that initializes each phase from the previous adapter to improve stability and efficiency. BLOCK is released with all prompt templates and fine-tuned weights to support reproducible character-to-skin generation.

AI.tldr: BLOCK is an open-source, two-stage pipeline that turns arbitrary character concepts into pixel-accurate Minecraft skins: an MLLM creates a consistent dual-panel 3D preview (front/back), and a fine-tuned FLUX.2 decodes that preview into a skin atlas, trained with a progressive EvolveLoRA curriculum for stability and efficiency; prompts and weights are released for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Generating Minecraft skins directly from text or high-level concepts is difficult due to the discrete, topology-constrained skin atlas and the need for consistent multi-view character depiction. The authors aim to ensure pixel-accurate, consistent skins while improving training stability/efficiency and enabling reproducible pipelines.
AI.method: A bi-stage pipeline: (1) 3D preview synthesis via an MLLM using a carefully designed prompt-and-reference template to produce a consistent dual-panel oblique-view (front/back) Minecraft-style preview; (2) skin decoding with a fine-tuned FLUX.2 model that maps the preview to a skin atlas. They introduce EvolveLoRA, a progressive LoRA curriculum (text-to-image → image-to-image → preview-to-skin) that initializes each stage from the prior adapter, improving stability and data/compute efficiency. All prompt templates and fine-tuned weights are released.
AI.result: The system generates pixel-perfect Minecraft skins from arbitrary character concepts, producing consistent previews and accurate atlas decodings. EvolveLoRA yields improved stability and efficiency across stages. The work provides open-source assets (prompts and weights) to enable reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing character-to-skin generation into preview synthesis and atlas decoding, combined with a progressive LoRA curriculum, delivers stable, efficient, and reproducible creation of high-fidelity Minecraft skins from diverse character inputs.

title: Semantic Bridging Domains: Pseudo-Source as Test-Time Connector
summary: Distribution shifts between training and testing data are a critical bottleneck limiting the practical utility of models, especially in real-world test-time scenarios. To adapt models when the source domain is unknown and the target domain is unlabeled, previous works constructed pseudo-source domains via data generation and translation, then aligned the target domain with them. However, significant discrepancies exist between the pseudo-source and the original source domain, leading to potential divergence when correcting the target directly. From this perspective, we propose a Stepwise Semantic Alignment (SSA) method, viewing the pseudo-source as a semantic bridge connecting the source and target, rather than a direct substitute for the source. Specifically, we leverage easily accessible universal semantics to rectify the semantic features of the pseudo-source, and then align the target domain using the corrected pseudo-source semantics. Additionally, we introduce a Hierarchical Feature Aggregation (HFA) module and a Confidence-Aware Complementary Learning (CACL) strategy to enhance the semantic quality of the SSA process in the absence of source and ground truth of target domains. We evaluated our approach on tasks like semantic segmentation and image classification, achieving a 5.2% performance boost on GTA2Cityscapes over the state-of-the-art.

AI.tldr: They propose Stepwise Semantic Alignment (SSA), which treats a generated pseudo-source as a semantic bridge, corrects it using universal semantics, and then aligns the target to this corrected space; with HFA and CACL modules, it outperforms prior work (+5.2% on GTA→Cityscapes).
AI.motivation: Distribution shifts between train and test hinder real-world deployment. Prior source-free approaches align targets directly to pseudo-sources, but large gaps between pseudo- and true-source semantics can cause drift and poor adaptation when the source is unknown and the target is unlabeled.
AI.method: SSA proceeds in two steps: (1) rectify pseudo-source semantic features using broadly available universal semantics; (2) align the unlabeled target to these corrected pseudo-source semantics. It adds a Hierarchical Feature Aggregation (HFA) module to strengthen semantic representations and a Confidence-Aware Complementary Learning (CACL) strategy to handle uncertainty without source data or target labels.
AI.result: Across semantic segmentation and image classification, the method improves over state-of-the-art baselines; notably, it yields a 5.2% gain on GTA→Cityscapes.
AI.conclusion: Viewing the pseudo-source as a bridge and correcting it stepwise, augmented by HFA and CACL, yields more stable and effective source-free adaptation under distribution shift and generalizes across tasks.

title: UniRain: Unified Image Deraining with RAG-based Dataset Distillation and Multi-objective Reweighted Optimization
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026; Project Page: https://github.com/QianfengY/UniRain
summary: Despite significant progress has been made in image deraining, we note that most existing methods are often developed for only specific types of rain degradation and fail to generalize across diverse real-world rainy scenes. How to effectively model different rain degradations within a universal framework is important for real-world image deraining. In this paper, we propose UniRain, an effective unified image deraining framework capable of restoring images degraded by rain streak and raindrop under both daytime and nighttime conditions. To better enhance unified model generalization, we construct an intelligent retrieval augmented generation (RAG)-based dataset distillation pipeline that selects high-quality training samples from all public deraining datasets for better mixed training. Furthermore, we incorporate a simple yet effective multi-objective reweighted optimization strategy into the asymmetric mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture to facilitate consistent performance and improve robustness across diverse scenes. Extensive experiments show that our framework performs favorably against the state-of-the-art models on our proposed benchmarks and multiple public datasets.

AI.tldr: UniRain is a unified image deraining framework that handles both rain streaks and raindrops in day and night, boosted by RAG-based dataset distillation and a reweighted multi-objective MoE, achieving state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Most deraining models specialize in one rain type or condition and fail to generalize to diverse real-world rainy scenes. A universal, robust approach that covers different degradations (streaks/raindrops) and lighting conditions (day/night) is needed.
AI.method: 1) A single unified deraining model (UniRain) that addresses rain streaks and raindrops under daytime and nighttime. 2) An intelligent retrieval-augmented (RAG) dataset distillation pipeline that selects high-quality, complementary samples from public deraining datasets for better mixed training. 3) An asymmetric mixture-of-experts (MoE) architecture enhanced with a simple multi-objective reweighted optimization strategy to balance tasks and improve robustness across scenes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show UniRain performs favorably against state-of-the-art methods on newly proposed benchmarks and multiple public datasets, with consistent robustness across varied rainy conditions.
AI.conclusion: A practical, generalizable deraining solution: by unifying tasks, curating training data via RAG-based distillation, and stabilizing learning with reweighted MoE optimization, UniRain achieves strong, consistent performance across diverse real-world rainy scenarios.

title: Benchmarking Motivational Interviewing Competence of Large Language Models
summary: Motivational interviewing (MI) promotes behavioural change in substance use disorders. Its fidelity is measured using the Motivational Interviewing Treatment Integrity (MITI) framework. While large language models (LLMs) can potentially generate MI-consistent therapist responses, their competence using MITI is not well-researched, especially in real world clinical transcripts. We aim to benchmark MI competence of proprietary and open-source models compared to human therapists in real-world transcripts and assess distinguishability from human therapists. Methods: We shortlisted 3 proprietary and 7 open-source LLMs from LMArena, evaluated performance using MITI 4.2 framework on two datasets (96 handcrafted model transcripts, 34 real-world clinical transcripts). We generated parallel LLM-therapist utterances iteratively for each transcript while keeping client responses static, and ranked performance using a composite ranking system with MITI components and verbosity. We conducted a distinguishability experiment with two independent psychiatrists to identify human-vs-LLM responses. Results: All 10 tested LLMs had fair (MITI global scores >3.5) to good (MITI global scores >4) competence across MITI measures, and three best-performing models (gemma-3-27b-it, gemini-2.5-pro, grok-3) were tested on real-world transcripts. All showed good competence, with LLMs outperforming human-expert in Complex Reflection percentage (39% vs 96%) and Reflection-Question ratio (1.2 vs >2.8). In the distinguishability experiment, psychiatrists identified LLM responses with only 56% accuracy, with d-prime: 0.17 and 0.25 for gemini-2.5-pro and gemma-3-27b-it respectively. Conclusion: LLMs can achieve good MI proficiency in real-world clinical transcripts using MITI framework. These findings suggest that even open-source LLMs are viable candidates for expanding MI counselling sessions in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: Ten LLMs achieved fair-to-good Motivational Interviewing (MI) proficiency per MITI 4.2, with some surpassing a human expert on specific metrics; clinicians could barely distinguish LLM from human responses, suggesting LLMs—including open-source—may help scale MI in low-resource settings.
AI.motivation: Assess whether proprietary and open-source LLMs can produce MI-consistent therapist utterances with adequate fidelity on real-world clinical transcripts and whether experts can tell LLM outputs from human therapists.
AI.method: Benchmarked 3 proprietary and 7 open-source LLMs using MITI 4.2 on 96 handcrafted transcripts and 34 real-world clinical transcripts. For each transcript, generated parallel LLM therapist utterances while holding client turns constant, then ranked models via a composite of MITI components and verbosity. Ran a distinguishability study with two psychiatrists judging human vs LLM responses.
AI.result: All 10 models scored fair-to-good on MITI global (>3.5 to >4). Top models: gemma-3-27b-it, gemini-2.5-pro, grok-3. On real-world transcripts, models showed good competence and exceeded a human expert in Complex Reflection percentage (96% vs 39%) and Reflection–Question ratio (>2.8 vs 1.2). Psychiatrists’ identification accuracy was ~56% (d′=0.17 and 0.25).
AI.conclusion: LLMs can reach good MI proficiency per MITI in real-world transcripts; open-source models appear viable to extend MI counseling capacity, particularly in low-resource contexts.

title: EvoPrune: Early-Stage Visual Token Pruning for Efficient MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong performance in vision-language tasks, but their inference efficiency is severely limited by the exponential growth of visual tokens in complex scenarios such as high-resolution images and videos. Existing visual token pruning methods mainly operate after visual encoding, overlooking the substantial computational cost incurred during the encoding stage. To address this issue, we propose EvoPrune, an early-stage visual token pruning method for MLLMs that performs pruning directly during visual encoding. Specifically, EvoPrune employs a layer-wise pruning strategy guided by token similarity, diversity, and attention-based importance to retain the most informative visual tokens at selected encoding layers. Extensive experiments on image and video benchmarks validate the effectiveness of EvoPrune. In particular, on the VideoMME dataset, EvoPrune achieves 2$\times$ inference speedup with less than 1% performance degradation, demonstrating its potential for latency-sensitive MLLM deployment.

AI.tldr: EvoPrune prunes visual tokens early inside the visual encoder of multimodal LLMs, using similarity, diversity, and attention-based importance to keep only informative tokens, delivering up to 2× faster inference with ~<1% accuracy loss on VideoMME and competitive results on image/video tasks.
AI.motivation: Visual tokens scale rapidly with input complexity (high‑res images, videos), making inference slow. Existing pruning occurs after visual encoding, leaving the most expensive computation (encoding) untouched. The goal is to cut compute and latency where it matters most—during encoding—while preserving performance.
AI.method: Introduce EvoPrune, a layer‑wise early pruning scheme embedded in the visual encoder. At selected layers, tokens are scored using (1) similarity (to remove redundancies), (2) diversity (to preserve coverage), and (3) attention‑based importance (to keep salient content). The model retains top tokens per these criteria and discards the rest, reducing token count progressively through the encoder.
AI.result: Across image and video benchmarks, EvoPrune maintains accuracy while reducing compute; notably, on VideoMME it achieves ~2× inference speedup with <1% performance degradation, indicating strong efficiency–accuracy trade‑offs for latency‑sensitive applications.
AI.conclusion: Early, layer‑wise token pruning during visual encoding is an effective way to accelerate MLLMs with minimal accuracy loss. EvoPrune shows practical promise for deploying MLLMs under strict latency constraints and may generalize across visual domains.

title: Scaling Dense Event-Stream Pretraining from Visual Foundation Models
summary: Learning versatile, fine-grained representations from irregular event streams is pivotal yet nontrivial, primarily due to the heavy annotation that hinders scalability in dataset size, semantic richness, and application scope. To mitigate this dilemma, we launch a novel self-supervised pretraining method that distills visual foundation models (VFMs) to push the boundaries of event representation at scale. Specifically, we curate an extensive synchronized image-event collection to amplify cross-modal alignment. Nevertheless, due to inherent mismatches in sparsity and granularity between image-event domains, existing distillation paradigms are prone to semantic collapse in event representations, particularly at high resolutions. To bridge this gap, we propose to extend the alignment objective to semantic structures provided off-the-shelf by VFMs, indicating a broader receptive field and stronger supervision. The key ingredient of our method is a structure-aware distillation loss that grounds higher-quality image-event correspondences for alignment, optimizing dense event representations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach takes a great leap in downstream benchmarks, significantly surpassing traditional methods and existing pretraining techniques. This breakthrough manifests in enhanced generalization, superior data efficiency and elevated transferability.

AI.tldr: They propose a self-supervised pretraining framework that distills semantic structures from visual foundation models into event-based encoders using a structure-aware distillation loss, overcoming image–event sparsity/granularity mismatch and preventing semantic collapse, yielding strong dense event representations and state-of-the-art downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Event streams are irregular and fine-grained but costly to label, restricting dataset scale and semantics. Directly distilling image features to events fails due to cross-modal sparsity/granularity mismatches, causing semantic collapse—especially at high resolutions. A scalable, annotation-free way to align events with rich visual semantics is needed.
AI.method: Curate a large synchronized image–event dataset and perform self-supervised cross-modal distillation from visual foundation models (VFMs) to an event representation network. Extend alignment beyond pointwise feature matching to VFM-provided semantic structures (e.g., regions/segments with broader receptive fields). Introduce a structure-aware distillation loss that grounds higher-quality image–event correspondences and optimizes dense event representations.
AI.result: Across multiple downstream benchmarks, the pretrained event representations deliver large performance gains over traditional training and prior pretraining approaches, with better generalization, data efficiency, and transfer across tasks and resolutions.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging VFM semantic structures via a structure-aware distillation objective enables scalable, label-free learning of high-quality dense event representations, mitigates semantic collapse from cross-modal mismatches, and sets a new performance bar with improved generalization and transferability.

title: Coupling Local Context and Global Semantic Prototypes via a Hierarchical Architecture for Rhetorical Roles Labeling
summary: Rhetorical Role Labeling (RRL) identifies the functional role of each sentence in a document, a key task for discourse understanding in domains such as law and medicine. While hierarchical models capture local dependencies effectively, they are limited in modeling global, corpus-level features. To address this limitation, we propose two prototype-based methods that integrate local context with global representations. Prototype-Based Regularization (PBR) learns soft prototypes through a distance-based auxiliary loss to structure the latent space, while Prototype-Conditioned Modulation (PCM) constructs corpus-level prototypes and injects them during training and inference. Given the scarcity of RRL resources, we introduce SCOTUS-Law, the first dataset of U.S. Supreme Court opinions annotated with rhetorical roles at three levels of granularity: category, rhetorical function, and step. Experiments on legal, medical, and scientific benchmarks show consistent improvements over strong baselines, with 4 Macro-F1 gains on low-frequency roles. We further analyze the implications in the era of Large Language Models and complement our findings with expert evaluation.

AI.tldr: They introduce two prototype-based techniques (PBR, PCM) that fuse local hierarchical context with global corpus-level signals for rhetorical role labeling and release a new Supreme Court dataset; methods outperform strong baselines, especially on rare roles (+4 Macro-F1), with additional LLM analyses and expert validation.
AI.motivation: Hierarchical RRL models capture local dependencies but fail to encode global, corpus-level regularities; resources for RRL—especially in law—are scarce, limiting generalization and performance on infrequent roles.
AI.method: Two complementary approaches: (1) Prototype-Based Regularization (PBR) learns soft prototypes via a distance-based auxiliary loss to shape the latent space; (2) Prototype-Conditioned Modulation (PCM) builds corpus-level prototypes and conditions the model on them during training and inference. They also curate SCOTUS-Law, a U.S. Supreme Court opinions dataset annotated with rhetorical roles at three granularities (category, rhetorical function, step).
AI.result: Across legal, medical, and scientific benchmarks, both methods consistently beat strong baselines, yielding notable gains on low-frequency roles (about +4 Macro-F1). They provide analyses in the context of LLMs and report expert evaluation supporting the approach.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating corpus-level prototype knowledge alongside local context significantly improves RRL, particularly for rare roles. The SCOTUS-Law dataset fills a critical gap for legal discourse research, and results suggest promising synergy with or robustness against LLM-era methods.

title: Error as Signal: Stiffness-Aware Diffusion Sampling via Embedded Runge-Kutta Guidance
summary: Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) has established the foundation for guidance mechanisms in diffusion models, showing that well-designed guidance proxies significantly improve conditional generation and sample quality. Autoguidance (AG) has extended this idea, but it relies on an auxiliary network and leaves solver-induced errors unaddressed. In stiff regions, the ODE trajectory changes sharply, where local truncation error (LTE) becomes a critical factor that deteriorates sample quality. Our key observation is that these errors align with the dominant eigenvector, motivating us to leverage the solver-induced error as a guidance signal.
