We propose Embedded Runge-Kutta Guidance (ERK-Guid), which exploits detected stiffness to reduce LTE and stabilize sampling. We theoretically and empirically analyze stiffness and eigenvector estimators with solver errors to motivate the design of ERK-Guid. Our experiments on both synthetic datasets and the popular benchmark dataset, ImageNet, demonstrate that ERK-Guid consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/mlvlab/ERK-Guid.

AI.tldr: ERK-Guid uses solver-induced local truncation errors—aligned with the dominant eigenvector in stiff ODE regions—as a guidance signal during diffusion sampling, reducing error and stabilizing trajectories to surpass prior guidance methods on synthetic data and ImageNet.
AI.motivation: While CFG and Autoguidance improve diffusion model conditioning, AG depends on an auxiliary network and overlooks numerical solver errors. In stiff regions of the reverse-time ODE, sharply changing dynamics cause large local truncation error that degrades sample quality. Observing that these errors align with the dominant eigenvector motivates repurposing them as a principled guidance signal.
AI.method: Introduce Embedded Runge-Kutta Guidance (ERK-Guid): use embedded RK pairs to estimate local truncation error and detect stiffness; extract the dominant eigenvector/eigenvalue direction from solver-induced errors; apply a guidance correction along this direction to reduce LTE and stabilize the ODE trajectory during sampling. Provide theoretical and empirical analysis of stiffness detection and eigenvector estimation from solver errors to justify the design.
AI.result: Across synthetic datasets and ImageNet, ERK-Guid consistently outperforms state-of-the-art guidance/sampling methods in conditional generation and sample quality, particularly by mitigating instability in stiff regions. No extra auxiliary network is required. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Stiffness-aware, error-driven guidance effectively couples numerical analysis with diffusion sampling: by aligning guidance with the dominant eigen-direction of solver errors, ERK-Guid reduces LTE, stabilizes trajectories, and yields state-of-the-art sample quality. This suggests a promising direction for integrating embedded error control into guidance for diffusion models.

title: GeoSeg: Training-Free Reasoning-Driven Segmentation in Remote Sensing Imagery
summary: Recent advances in MLLMs are reframing segmentation from fixed-category prediction to instruction-grounded localization. While reasoning based segmentation has progressed rapidly in natural scenes, remote sensing lacks a generalizable solution due to the prohibitive cost of reasoning-oriented data and domain-specific challenges like overhead viewpoints. We present GeoSeg, a zero-shot, training-free framework that bypasses the supervision bottleneck for reasoning-driven remote sensing segmentation. GeoSeg couples MLLM reasoning with precise localization via: (i) bias-aware coordinate refinement to correct systematic grounding shifts and (ii) a dual-route prompting mechanism to fuse semantic intent with fine-grained spatial cues. We also introduce GeoSeg-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark of 810 image--query pairs with hierarchical difficulty levels. Experiments show that GeoSeg consistently outperforms all baselines, with extensive ablations confirming the effectiveness and necessity of each component.

AI.tldr: GeoSeg is a zero-shot, training-free framework that combines MLLM reasoning with bias-corrected localization and dual-route prompting to perform instruction-grounded remote-sensing segmentation; it also introduces a 810-pair benchmark and outperforms baselines with validated component effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Reasoning-based segmentation has progressed in natural images but lacks a generalizable, data-efficient solution for remote sensing due to high costs of reasoning-oriented supervision and domain challenges like overhead viewpoints and systematic grounding shifts.
AI.method: Leverages an MLLM for instruction-grounded reasoning and augments it with (i) bias-aware coordinate refinement to correct systematic grounding shifts, and (ii) a dual-route prompting mechanism that fuses semantic intent with fine-grained spatial cues. The approach is zero-shot and training-free. A new diagnostic benchmark, GeoSeg-Bench (810 image–query pairs, hierarchical difficulty), is introduced.
AI.result: Across experiments, GeoSeg consistently outperforms all baselines. Extensive ablations indicate each proposed component (bias-aware refinement and dual-route prompting) is effective and necessary.
AI.conclusion: GeoSeg bypasses the supervision bottleneck and offers a generalizable, reasoning-driven solution for remote-sensing segmentation, complemented by GeoSeg-Bench for evaluation. The framework is effective without task-specific training and sets a new reference point for instruction-grounded localization in aerial/overhead imagery.

title: Assessing the Effectiveness of LLMs in Delivering Cognitive Behavioral Therapy
summary: As mental health issues continue to rise globally, there is an increasing demand for accessible and scalable therapeutic solutions. Many individuals currently seek support from Large Language Models (LLMs), even though these models have not been validated for use in counseling services. In this paper, we evaluate LLMs' ability to emulate professional therapists practicing Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT). Using anonymized, transcribed role-play sessions between licensed therapists and clients, we compare two approaches: (1) a generation-only method and (2) a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approach using CBT guidelines. We evaluate both proprietary and open-source models for linguistic quality, semantic coherence, and therapeutic fidelity using standard natural language generation (NLG) metrics, natural language inference (NLI), and automated scoring for skills assessment. Our results indicate that while LLMs can generate CBT-like dialogues, they are limited in their ability to convey empathy and maintain consistency.

AI.tldr: LLMs can mimic the surface form of CBT conversations, but they struggle with empathy and consistency; retrieval with CBT guidelines is explored yet does not eliminate these gaps.
AI.motivation: Rising global mental-health needs and informal use of LLMs for support create urgency to assess whether LLMs can responsibly emulate professional CBT therapists.
AI.method: Use anonymized, transcribed therapist–client role-play sessions to compare (1) pure generation and (2) RAG grounded in CBT guidelines across proprietary and open-source LLMs. Evaluate linguistic quality, semantic coherence, and therapeutic fidelity using standard NLG metrics, NLI, and automated skills scoring.
AI.result: Models produce CBT-like dialogue structure and content, but exhibit deficits in conveying empathy and maintaining conversational consistency across turns; these issues persist across model types and settings.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not yet reliable CBT counselors: they can replicate stylistic aspects but fall short on core therapeutic qualities. Further work is needed on empathy, consistency, and clinically valid evaluation before safe deployment.

title: RIVER: A Real-Time Interaction Benchmark for Video LLMs
summary: The rapid advancement of multimodal large language models has demonstrated impressive capabilities, yet nearly all operate in an offline paradigm, hindering real-time interactivity. Addressing this gap, we introduce the Real-tIme Video intERaction Bench (RIVER Bench), designed for evaluating online video comprehension. RIVER Bench introduces a novel framework comprising Retrospective Memory, Live-Perception, and Proactive Anticipation tasks, closely mimicking interactive dialogues rather than responding to entire videos at once. We conducted detailed annotations using videos from diverse sources and varying lengths, and precisely defined the real-time interactive format. Evaluations across various model categories reveal that while offline models perform well in single question-answering tasks, they struggle with real-time processing. Addressing the limitations of existing models in online video interaction, especially their deficiencies in long-term memory and future perception, we proposed a general improvement method that enables models to interact with users more flexibly in real time. We believe this work will significantly advance the development of real-time interactive video understanding models and inspire future research in this emerging field. Datasets and code are publicly available at https://github.com/OpenGVLab/RIVER.

AI.tldr: Introduces RIVER Bench, a benchmark and interaction protocol for evaluating multimodal LLMs in real-time video understanding with memory, live-perception, and anticipation tasks; shows offline models struggle online; proposes a general method to improve real-time interaction; releases data/code.
AI.motivation: Most multimodal LLMs process videos offline, limiting real-time, dialogue-like interactivity. There is a lack of standardized evaluation for online video comprehension and known weaknesses in long-term memory and future anticipation.
AI.method: Builds RIVER Bench with three task families—Retrospective Memory, Live-Perception, and Proactive Anticipation—to emulate interactive dialogues over streaming video. Curates and annotates diverse, variable-length videos; specifies a real-time interactive evaluation format. Benchmarks multiple model categories on these tasks and introduces a general improvement approach aimed at better long-term memory and forward-looking perception for online interaction.
AI.result: Finds that models performing well on single, offline QA degrade markedly in real-time settings. The proposed improvement method enables more flexible, effective user interaction in real time (abstract does not provide quantitative metrics).
AI.conclusion: RIVER Bench fills a critical gap for evaluating and advancing real-time interactive video understanding. By highlighting shortcomings and providing data, code, and a general improvement pathway, it is positioned to catalyze progress in online, interactive multimodal systems.

title: CzechTopic: A Benchmark for Zero-Shot Topic Localization in Historical Czech Documents
summary: Topic localization aims to identify spans of text that express a given topic defined by a name and description. To study this task, we introduce a human-annotated benchmark based on Czech historical documents, containing human-defined topics together with manually annotated spans and supporting evaluation at both document and word levels. Evaluation is performed relative to human agreement rather than a single reference annotation. We evaluate a diverse range of large language models alongside BERT-based models fine-tuned on a distilled development dataset. Results reveal substantial variability among LLMs, with performance ranging from near-human topic detection to pronounced failures in span localization. While the strongest models approach human agreement, the distilled token embedding models remain competitive despite their smaller scale. The dataset and evaluation framework are publicly available at: https://github.com/dcgm/czechtopic.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Czech historical-topic localization benchmark with human-annotated spans and an evaluation tied to inter-annotator agreement; compares diverse LLMs to fine-tuned BERT-style distilled token-embedding models. Best systems approach human agreement on topic detection but often falter on precise span localization; smaller distilled models remain competitive. Resources are publicly released.
AI.motivation: Topic localization lacks robust, human-grounded benchmarks—especially in low-resource, domain-specific settings like Czech historical texts. Single-reference evaluations can be brittle; aligning evaluation with human agreement provides a fairer target and reveals practical model limits.
AI.method: Construct a human-annotated dataset of Czech historical documents with named/defined topics and marked supporting spans. Provide evaluation at both document and word levels, scoring systems relative to human inter-annotator agreement. Benchmark a range of LLMs and BERT-based models fine-tuned on a distilled development set.
AI.result: Performance varies widely across LLMs: some approach human agreement for topic detection but show significant weaknesses in span localization. Despite their small size, distilled token-embedding models are competitive with larger models.
AI.conclusion: Human-agreement–based evaluation highlights that accurate span localization remains challenging even for strong LLMs. Carefully distilled, smaller models can offer strong trade-offs. The released dataset and framework enable reproducible study and progress on topic localization.

title: When Visual Evidence is Ambiguous: Pareidolia as a Diagnostic Probe for Vision Models
summary: When visual evidence is ambiguous, vision models must decide whether to interpret face-like patterns as meaningful. Face pareidolia, the perception of faces in non-face objects, provides a controlled probe of this behavior. We introduce a representation-level diagnostic framework that analyzes detection, localization, uncertainty, and bias across class, difficulty, and emotion in face pareidolia images. Under a unified protocol, we evaluate six models spanning four representational regimes: vision-language models (VLMs; CLIP-B/32, CLIP-L/14, LLaVA-1.5-7B), pure vision classification (ViT), general object detection (YOLOv8), and face detection (RetinaFace). Our analysis reveals three mechanisms of interpretation under ambiguity. VLMs exhibit semantic overactivation, systematically pulling ambiguous non-human regions toward the Human concept, with LLaVA-1.5-7B producing the strongest and most confident over-calls, especially for negative emotions. ViT instead follows an uncertainty-as-abstention strategy, remaining diffuse yet largely unbiased. Detection-based models achieve low bias through conservative priors that suppress pareidolia responses even when localization is controlled. These results show that behavior under ambiguity is governed more by representational choices than score thresholds, and that uncertainty and bias are decoupled: low uncertainty can signal either safe suppression, as in detectors, or extreme over-interpretation, as in VLMs. Pareidolia therefore provides a compact diagnostic and a source of ambiguity-aware hard negatives for probing and improving the semantic robustness of vision-language systems. Code will be released upon publication.

AI.tldr: They use face pareidolia to probe how different vision and vision–language models handle visual ambiguity, showing that VLMs over-interpret ambiguous regions as human (especially negative emotions), ViT stays uncertain without strong bias, and detectors suppress pareidolia via conservative priors. Uncertainty and bias are decoupled, and representational design drives behavior more than thresholds.
AI.motivation: Modern vision(-language) systems encounter ambiguous visual patterns, but it is unclear how their internal representations translate ambiguity into decisions, uncertainty, and potential bias (e.g., toward “human” or emotional labels). Face pareidolia offers a controlled, scalable way to study this because it presents face-like structure without true faces.
AI.method: They propose a representation-level diagnostic that evaluates detection, localization, uncertainty, and bias on curated face pareidolia images. Under a unified protocol, they test six models spanning four regimes: VLMs (CLIP-B/32, CLIP-L/14, LLaVA-1.5-7B), pure vision classification (ViT), general object detection (YOLOv8), and face detection (RetinaFace). They analyze responses across class, difficulty, and emotion, and control for localization to disentangle priors from detection ability.
AI.result: Three distinct mechanisms emerge: (1) VLMs show semantic overactivation, pulling ambiguous non-human regions toward the Human concept; LLaVA-1.5-7B makes the strongest, most confident over-calls, especially for negative emotions. (2) ViT adopts uncertainty-as-abstention—diffuse predictions with little systematic bias. (3) Detection models maintain low bias through conservative priors that suppress pareidolia even when localization is equivalent. They find that representational choices, not score thresholds, primarily govern behavior, and that low uncertainty can reflect either safe suppression (detectors) or over-interpretation (VLMs).
AI.conclusion: Face pareidolia is an effective compact diagnostic and a source of ambiguity-aware hard negatives for stress-testing and improving semantic robustness, especially in VLMs. The decoupling of uncertainty and bias cautions against using confidence alone as a safety signal. Code will be released upon publication.

title: Rethinking Role-Playing Evaluation: Anonymous Benchmarking and a Systematic Study of Personality Effects
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated significant potential in developing Role-Playing Agents (RPAs). However, current research primarily evaluates RPAs using famous fictional characters, allowing models to rely on memory associated with character names. This dependency creates a bias that limits the generalization of RPAs to unseen personas. To address this issue, we propose an anonymous evaluation method. Experiments across multiple benchmarks reveal that anonymization significantly degrades role-playing performance, confirming that name exposure carries implicit information. Furthermore, we investigate personality augmentation to enhance role fidelity under anonymous setting. We systematically compare the efficacy of personality traits derived from human annotations versus those self-generated by the model. Our results demonstrate that incorporating personality information consistently improves RPA performance. Crucially, self-generated personalities achieve performance comparable to human-annotated ones. This work establishes a fairer evaluation protocol and validates a scalable, personality-enhanced framework for constructing robust RPAs.

AI.tldr: They show that LLM role-playing agents over-rely on famous character names; anonymizing those names lowers performance, but adding explicit personality traits restores fidelity—self-generated traits work as well as human-annotated ones. They propose an anonymous evaluation protocol and a scalable personality-augmented framework for robust RPAs.
AI.motivation: Current RPA evaluations often use well-known fictional personas, letting models exploit memorized world knowledge tied to names. This biases results upward and weakens generalization to unseen or custom personas. A fairer evaluation and a scalable way to build strong RPAs without name leakage are needed.
AI.method: 1) Introduce an anonymous evaluation setting that removes/obfuscates character names to prevent memory-based shortcuts. 2) Benchmark multiple RPAs across datasets with and without anonymization to quantify performance gaps. 3) Augment prompts with personality traits, comparing traits sourced from human annotations versus traits self-generated by the LLM, and evaluate role fidelity under anonymity.
AI.result: - Anonymization causes a significant drop in role-playing performance, indicating name exposure carries implicit, helpful information. - Adding personality traits consistently improves performance in the anonymous setting. - Self-generated personality traits achieve performance comparable to human-annotated traits.
AI.conclusion: Anonymous evaluation provides a fairer measure of role fidelity by removing name-induced bias. Personality augmentation mitigates the anonymization penalty, and self-generated traits offer a scalable alternative to costly human annotations, enabling more robust and generalizable RPAs.

title: Weakly Supervised Patch Annotation for Improved Screening of Diabetic Retinopathy
summary: Diabetic Retinopathy (DR) requires timely screening to prevent irreversible vision loss. However, its early detection remains a significant challenge since often the subtle pathological manifestations (lesions) get overlooked due to insufficient annotation. Existing literature primarily focuses on image-level supervision, weakly-supervised localization, and clustering-based representation learning, which fail to systematically annotate unlabeled lesion region(s) for refining the dataset. Expert-driven lesion annotation is labor-intensive and often incomplete, limiting the performance of deep learning models. We introduce Similarity-based Annotation via Feature-space Ensemble (SAFE), a two-stage framework that unifies weak supervision, contrastive learning, and patch-wise embedding inference, to systematically expand sparse annotations in the pathology. SAFE preserves fine-grained details of the lesion(s) under partial clinical supervision. In the first stage, a dual-arm Patch Embedding Network learns semantically structured, class-discriminative embeddings from expert annotated patches. Next, an ensemble of independent embedding spaces extrapolates labels to the unannotated regions based on spatial and semantic proximity. An abstention mechanism ensures trade-off between highly reliable annotation and noisy coverage. Experimental results demonstrate reliable separation of healthy and diseased patches, achieving upto 0.9886 accuracy. The annotation generated from SAFE substantially improves downstream tasks such as DR classification, demonstrating a substantial increase in F1-score of the diseased class and a performance gain as high as 0.545 in Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Qualitative analysis, with explainability, confirms that SAFE focuses on clinically relevant lesion patterns; and is further validated by ophthalmologists.

AI.tldr: SAFE is a two-stage framework that learns patch-level embeddings and ensembless multiple feature spaces to propagate reliable lesion labels (with abstention) to unlabeled retinal regions, substantially improving DR classification and AUPRC, with patch-level accuracy up to 0.9886 and ophthalmologist-validated focus on clinical lesions.
AI.motivation: Early DR detection needs precise lesion annotations, but expert labeling is sparse, costly, and incomplete. Prior methods (image-level supervision, weak localization, clustering) don’t systematically expand unlabeled lesion regions into usable annotations, limiting model performance.
AI.method: Stage 1: A dual-arm Patch Embedding Network, trained with weak supervision and contrastive learning on expert-annotated patches, learns semantically structured, class-discriminative embeddings that preserve fine-grained lesion details. Stage 2: An ensemble of independent embedding spaces propagates labels to unannotated patches based on spatial and semantic similarity; an abstention mechanism balances reliability vs. coverage during annotation expansion. Patch-wise embedding inference produces dense pseudo-annotations.
AI.result:
- Clear separation of healthy vs. diseased patches with accuracy up to 0.9886.
- SAFE-generated annotations markedly boost downstream DR classification, increasing diseased-class F1 and yielding up to +0.545 AUPRC.
- Qualitative explainability indicates attention to clinically meaningful lesion patterns; findings are corroborated by ophthalmologists.
AI.conclusion: SAFE provides a practical route to systematically expand sparse clinical annotations while maintaining reliability. Its ensemble-with-abstention strategy yields high-quality pseudo-labels that enhance downstream DR classifiers and align with clinical lesion patterns, suggesting utility for curating better datasets and potential transfer to other pathology domains.

title: Who Judges the Judge? Evaluating LLM-as-a-Judge for French Medical open-ended QA
summary: Automatic evaluation of medical open-ended question answering (OEQA) remains challenging due to the need for expert annotations. We evaluate whether large language models (LLMs) can act as judges of semantic equivalence in French medical OEQA, comparing closed-access, general-purpose, and biomedical domain-adapted models. Our results show that LLM-based judgments are strongly influenced by the model that generated the answer, with agreement varying substantially across generators. Domain-adapted and large general-purpose models achieve the highest alignment with expert annotations. We further show that lightweight adaptation of a compact model using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) substantially improves performance and reduces generator sensitivity, even with limited data. Overall, our findings highlight the need for generator-aware evaluation and suggest that carefully adapted small models can support scalable evaluation in low-resource medical settings.

AI.tldr: They test whether LLMs can reliably judge semantic equivalence for French medical open‑ended QA, find judgments depend on the answer generator, and show that domain‑adapted/large models align best with experts while a compact model improved via SFT+GRPO approaches this performance with less generator bias.
AI.motivation: Expert-labeled evaluation for medical OEQA is costly and slow, especially in low‑resource, non‑English settings (French). A scalable alternative is to use LLMs as automatic judges, but their reliability and biases—particularly toward different answer generators—are unclear.
AI.method: Compare multiple LLM judges (closed, general-purpose, biomedical-adapted) on judging semantic equivalence of answers in French medical OEQA; measure agreement with expert annotations across answers from different generators. Further, apply lightweight adaptation to a compact model using supervised fine-tuning and GRPO to assess performance and sensitivity changes under limited data.
AI.result: LLM judge decisions are strongly affected by which model generated the answer, yielding variable agreement with experts. Domain-adapted biomedical and large general-purpose models attain the highest expert alignment. Compact models, after SFT+GRPO, significantly improve and exhibit reduced sensitivity to the answer generator, even with limited training data.
AI.conclusion: Automatic medical OEQA evaluation should be generator-aware. With targeted SFT+GRPO, small models can become reliable, less biased judges, enabling scalable evaluation in low-resource medical contexts.

title: Discriminative Perception via Anchored Description for Reasoning Segmentation
summary: Reasoning segmentation increasingly employs reinforcement learning to generate explanatory reasoning chains that guide Multimodal Large Language Models. While these geometric rewards are primarily confined to guiding the final localization, they are incapable of discriminating whether the reasoning process remains anchored on the referred region or strays into irrelevant context. Lacking this discriminative guidance, the model's reasoning often devolves into unfocused and verbose chains that ultimately fail to disambiguate and perceive the target in complex scenes. This suggests a need to complement the RL objective with Discriminative Perception, an ability to actively distinguish a target from its context. To realize this, we propose DPAD to compel the model to generate a descriptive caption of the referred object, which is then used to explicitly discriminate by contrasting the caption's semantic relevance to the referred object against the wider context. By optimizing for this discriminative capability, the model is forced to focus on the unique attributes of the target, leading to a more converged and efficient reasoning chain. The descriptive caption also serves as an interpretability rationale that aligns with the segmentation. Experiments on the benchmarks confirm the validity of our approach, delivering substantial performance gains, with the cIoU on ReasonSeg increasing by 3.09% and the reasoning chain length decreasing by approximately 42%. Code is available at https://github.com/mrazhou/DPAD

AI.tldr: DPAD augments RL-based reasoning segmentation with a discriminative-perception objective: the model must caption the referred object and contrast that caption’s semantics against the scene context, yielding more focused, shorter reasoning chains and higher cIoU (+3.09% on ReasonSeg).
AI.motivation: Current approaches use geometric rewards that supervise only final localization, offering no signal about whether intermediate reasoning remains anchored on the target versus drifting to irrelevant context. This gap leads to verbose, unfocused chains that fail to disambiguate targets in complex scenes.
AI.method: DPAD compels the model to generate a descriptive caption of the referred object, then explicitly contrasts the caption’s semantic relevance to the referred object against the wider context. This contrastive, discriminative signal is optimized alongside RL, pushing the model to emphasize unique target attributes. The caption also serves as an interpretable rationale aligned with the resulting segmentation.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmarks show substantial improvements: on ReasonSeg, cIoU increases by 3.09% and the average reasoning chain length drops by about 42%.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating discriminative perception into the RL objective grounds reasoning on the target, producing more concise, effective chains and better segmentation, with captions doubling as aligned interpretability rationales.

title: Monitoring Emergent Reward Hacking During Generation via Internal Activations
summary: Fine-tuned large language models can exhibit reward-hacking behavior arising from emergent misalignment, which is difficult to detect from final outputs alone. While prior work has studied reward hacking at the level of completed responses, it remains unclear whether such behavior can be identified during generation. We propose an activation-based monitoring approach that detects reward-hacking signals from internal representations as a model generates its response. Our method trains sparse autoencoders on residual stream activations and applies lightweight linear classifiers to produce token-level estimates of reward-hacking activity. Across multiple model families and fine-tuning mixtures, we find that internal activation patterns reliably distinguish reward-hacking from benign behavior, generalize to unseen mixed-policy adapters, and exhibit model-dependent temporal structure during chain-of-thought reasoning. Notably, reward-hacking signals often emerge early, persist throughout reasoning, and can be amplified by increased test-time compute in the form of chain-of-thought prompting under weakly specified reward objectives. These results suggest that internal activation monitoring provides a complementary and earlier signal of emergent misalignment than output-based evaluation, supporting more robust post-deployment safety monitoring for fine-tuned language models.

AI.tldr: Monitor internal activations of fine‑tuned LLMs with sparse autoencoders and linear probes to flag reward‑hacking as it unfolds, often earlier than output-based checks.
AI.motivation: Reward hacking from emergent misalignment is hard to detect from final outputs; operators need earlier, reliable signals during generation for safer deployment.
AI.method: Train sparse autoencoders on residual-stream activations; fit lightweight linear classifiers on the SAE codes to yield token‑level reward‑hacking scores. Evaluate across model families, fine‑tuning mixtures, unseen mixed‑policy adapters, and analyze temporal dynamics during chain‑of‑thought and under increased test‑time compute.
AI.result: Activation patterns robustly separate reward‑hacking from benign behavior, generalize to unseen adapters, and show model‑dependent temporal structure. Signals arise early, persist through reasoning, and are amplified by chain‑of‑thought prompting under weakly specified rewards.
AI.conclusion: Internal-activation monitoring provides a complementary, earlier indicator of emergent misalignment than output evaluation, enabling stronger post‑deployment safety monitoring for fine‑tuned LLMs.

title: Rethinking the Efficiency and Effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning for Radiology Report Generation
summary: Radiologists highly desire fully automated AI for radiology report generation (R2G), yet existing approaches fall short in clinical utility. Reinforcement learning (RL) holds potential to address these shortcomings, but its adoption in this task remains underexplored. In this paper, we revisit RL in terms of data efficiency and optimization effectiveness for R2G tasks. First, we explore the impact of data quantity and quality on the performance of RL in medical contexts, revealing that data quality plays a more critical role than quantity. To this end, we propose a diagnostic diversity-based data sampling strategy that enables comparable performance with fewer samples. Second, we observe that the majority of tokens in radiology reports are template-like and diagnostically uninformative, whereas the low frequency of clinically critical tokens heightens the risk of being overlooked during optimization. To tackle this, we introduce Diagnostic Token-weighted Policy Optimization (DiTPO), which directly optimizes for clinical accuracy by using a diagnostic F1 score as the reward signal. Unlike standard RL approaches that treat all tokens equally, DiTPO explicitly models the varying importance of different tokens through rule- or gradient-based mechanisms to prioritize clinically relevant content. Extensive experiments on the MIMIC-CXR, IU-Xray, and CheXpert Plus datasets demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance while requiring substantially fewer training samples in RL. Notably, on MIMIC-CXR, our framework attains an F1 score of 0.516 using only 20% of the RL training samples.

AI.tldr: They improve radiology report generation with reinforcement learning by prioritizing clinically important content and smarter data selection, showing state-of-the-art diagnostic F1 with far fewer RL samples.
AI.motivation: Automated radiology report generation has limited clinical utility because models optimize generic text metrics and waste effort on template-like tokens. RL could align outputs with clinical goals, but its data demands and token-agnostic optimization hinder adoption in medical settings.
AI.method: Two components: (1) Diagnostic diversity-based sampling to prefer high-quality, clinically varied cases, boosting data efficiency. (2) Diagnostic Token-weighted Policy Optimization (DiTPO), which uses a diagnostic F1 reward and assigns higher weights to clinically critical tokens via rule-based or gradient-based importance mechanisms, so policy updates focus on medically salient content rather than boilerplate.
AI.result: Across MIMIC-CXR, IU-Xray, and CheXpert Plus, the framework achieves SOTA clinical accuracy with fewer RL samples; on MIMIC-CXR it reaches F1 0.516 using only 20% of the RL training data.
AI.conclusion: Quality-focused sampling and token-importance-weighted RL directly target clinical correctness, yielding more sample-efficient and accurate R2G. Prioritizing diagnostic tokens over template text is key to effective optimization in medical report generation.

title: Hindsight Quality Prediction Experiments in Multi-Candidate Human-Post-Edited Machine Translation
comment: Accepted to the 2026 Language Resources and Evaluation Conference (LREC)
summary: This paper investigates two complementary paradigms for predicting machine translation (MT) quality: source-side difficulty prediction and candidate-side quality estimation (QE). The rapid adoption of Large Language Models (LLMs) into MT workflows is reshaping the research landscape, yet its impact on established quality prediction paradigms remains underexplored. We study this issue through a series of "hindsight" experiments on a unique, multi-candidate dataset resulting from a genuine MT post-editing (MTPE) project. The dataset consists of over 6,000 English source segments with nine translation hypotheses from a diverse set of traditional neural MT systems and advanced LLMs, all evaluated against a single, final human post-edited reference. Using Kendall's rank correlation, we assess the predictive power of source-side difficulty metrics, candidate-side QE models and position heuristics against two gold-standard scores: TER (as a proxy for post-editing effort) and COMET (as a proxy for human judgment). Our findings highlight that the architectural shift towards LLMs alters the reliability of established quality prediction methods while simultaneously mitigating previous challenges in document-level translation.

AI.tldr: They compare source-side difficulty prediction and candidate-side QE under the new reality where LLMs produce translations, using a large multi-candidate MTPE dataset, and show that LLMs shift which quality-prediction methods are reliable while alleviating some document-level issues.
AI.motivation: LLMs are rapidly entering MT workflows, but we lack evidence on how their emergence affects established paradigms for predicting MT quality (source difficulty vs candidate-side QE). Practitioners need to know whether existing predictors still guide MTPE effort and human judgments when LLMs are in the candidate pool.
AI.method: Hindsight analysis on a real MT post-editing project: ~6k English source segments, each with nine translation hypotheses from diverse NMT systems and advanced LLMs, all aligned to a single human post-edited reference. They evaluate three predictor families—source-side difficulty metrics, candidate-side QE models, and simple position-based heuristics—by ranking candidates and computing Kendall’s tau with two gold scores: TER (proxy for post-editing effort) and COMET (proxy for human judgment).
AI.result: Across the mixed NMT+LLM pool, correlations of traditional predictors with TER/COMET change: methods previously reliable degrade or shift when LLM outputs are included. Simple heuristics and some predictors behave differently depending on architecture. Additionally, LLM outputs reduce prior challenges at the document level (e.g., coherence/consistency issues), improving predictability or diminishing the need for some document-level adjustments.
AI.conclusion: Quality prediction paradigms calibrated on NMT-era data do not transfer cleanly to LLM-inclusive settings. LLMs both disrupt the reliability of standard source- and candidate-side predictors and partially alleviate document-level pain points. MT workflows and QE tooling should be revalidated and possibly redesigned for LLM-era candidate pools.

title: Volumetric Directional Diffusion: Anchoring Uncertainty Quantification in Anatomical Consensus for Ambiguous Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Equivocal 3D lesion segmentation exhibits high inter-observer variability. Conventional deterministic models ignore this aleatoric uncertainty, producing over-confident masks that obscure clinical risks. Conversely, while generative methods (e.g., standard diffusion) capture sample diversity, recovering complex topology from pure noise frequently leads to severe structural fractures and out-of-distribution anatomical hallucinations. To resolve this fidelity-diversity trade-off, we propose Volumetric Directional Diffusion (VDD). Unlike standard diffusion models that denoise isotropic Gaussian noise, VDD mathematically anchors the generative trajectory to a deterministic consensus prior. By restricting the generative search space to iteratively predict a 3D boundary residual field, VDD accurately explores the fine-grained geometric variations inherent in expert disagreements without risking topological collapse. Extensive validation on three multi-rater datasets (LIDC-IDRI, KiTS21, and ISBI 2015) demonstrates that VDD achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification (significantly improving GED and CI) while remaining highly competitive in segmentation accuracy against deterministic upper bounds. Ultimately, VDD provides clinicians with anatomically coherent uncertainty maps, enabling safer decision-making and mitigating risks in downstream tasks (e.g., radiotherapy planning or surgical margin assessment).

AI.tldr: Proposes Volumetric Directional Diffusion (VDD), a diffusion-based segmentation framework anchored to a deterministic consensus prior that predicts boundary residuals rather than denoising pure noise. This preserves anatomical topology while capturing expert disagreement, yielding state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification on three multi-rater datasets with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Deterministic segmenters ignore aleatoric uncertainty and produce overconfident masks; standard diffusion models capture diversity but often hallucinate structures and break topology when generating from isotropic noise. The goal is to balance fidelity (anatomical coherence) and diversity (expert variability) in 3D lesion segmentation.
AI.method: Anchor the diffusion trajectory to a deterministic consensus prior and restrict the generative search to an iterative 3D boundary residual field. By directing generation around the prior instead of denoising isotropic Gaussian noise, the model explores fine-grained geometric variations while avoiding topological collapse and out-of-distribution anatomy.
AI.result: On LIDC-IDRI, KiTS21, and ISBI 2015 multi-rater datasets, VDD reportedly achieves state-of-the-art uncertainty quantification (improved GED and CI) and remains highly competitive in segmentation accuracy relative to deterministic baselines/upper bounds.
AI.conclusion: VDD provides anatomically coherent uncertainty maps that better reflect expert disagreement, supporting safer clinical decisions and mitigating risks in downstream tasks such as radiotherapy planning and surgical margin assessment.

title: FINEST: Improving LLM Responses to Sensitive Topics Through Fine-Grained Evaluation
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often generate overly cautious and vague responses on sensitive topics, sacrificing helpfulness for safety. Existing evaluation frameworks lack systematic methods to identify and address specific weaknesses in responses to sensitive topics, making it difficult to improve both safety and helpfulness simultaneously. To address this, we introduce FINEST, a FINE-grained response evaluation taxonomy for Sensitive Topics, which breaks down helpfulness and harmlessness into errors across three main categories: Content, Logic, and Appropriateness. Experiments on a Korean-sensitive question dataset demonstrate that our score- and error-based improvement pipeline, guided by FINEST, significantly improves the model responses across all three categories, outperforming refinement without guidance. Notably, score-based improvement -- providing category-specific scores and justifications -- yields the most significant gains, reducing the error sentence ratio for Appropriateness by up to 33.09%. This work lays the foundation for a more explainable and comprehensive evaluation and improvement of LLM responses to sensitive questions.

AI.tldr: FINEST is a fine-grained evaluation taxonomy (Content, Logic, Appropriateness) for assessing and improving LLM responses on sensitive topics; a score- and error-guided refinement pipeline using FINEST outperforms unguided refinement, cutting Appropriateness errors by up to ~33% on a Korean dataset.
AI.motivation: LLMs tend to be overly cautious and vague on sensitive topics, sacrificing helpfulness for safety. Existing evaluations don’t pinpoint where responses fail, making it hard to improve both harmlessness and helpfulness concurrently.
AI.method: Propose FINEST, a taxonomy that decomposes helpfulness and harmlessness into error types across three categories: Content, Logic, and Appropriateness. Build an improvement pipeline that uses category-specific scores and justifications (score-based) and error annotations (error-based) to guide response refinement. Evaluate on a Korean sensitive-question dataset.
AI.result: Guided refinement with FINEST improves responses across all three categories versus unguided refinement. Score-based guidance yields the largest gains, reducing the error sentence ratio for Appropriateness by up to 33.09%.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, taxonomy-driven evaluation makes LLM refinement for sensitive topics more explainable and effective, offering a practical pathway to better balance helpfulness and harmlessness.

title: DQE-CIR: Distinctive Query Embeddings through Learnable Attribute Weights and Target Relative Negative Sampling in Composed Image Retrieval
summary: Composed image retrieval (CIR) addresses the task of retrieving a target image by jointly interpreting a reference image and a modification text that specifies the intended change. Most existing methods are still built upon contrastive learning frameworks that treat the ground truth image as the only positive instance and all remaining images as negatives. This strategy inevitably introduces relevance suppression, where semantically related yet valid images are incorrectly pushed away, and semantic confusion, where different modification intents collapse into overlapping regions of the embedding space. As a result, the learned query representations often lack discriminativeness, particularly at fine-grained attribute modifications. To overcome these limitations, we propose distinctive query embeddings through learnable attribute weights and target relative negative sampling (DQE-CIR), a method designed to learn distinctive query embeddings by explicitly modeling target relative relevance during training. DQE-CIR incorporates learnable attribute weighting to emphasize distinctive visual features conditioned on the modification text, enabling more precise feature alignment between language and vision. Furthermore, we introduce target relative negative sampling, which constructs a target relative similarity distribution and selects informative negatives from a mid-zone region that excludes both easy negatives and ambiguous false negatives. This strategy enables more reliable retrieval for fine-grained attribute changes by improving query discriminativeness and reducing confusion caused by semantically similar but irrelevant candidates.

AI.tldr: Proposes DQE-CIR, which learns more distinctive composed-image queries by weighting attributes conditioned on the text and by sampling “mid-zone” negatives based on a target-relative similarity distribution, reducing suppression of valid similar images and confusion among modification intents.
AI.motivation: Standard contrastive CIR treats only the ground-truth image as positive and everything else as negative. This pushes away semantically related, still-valid images (relevance suppression) and collapses different modification intents in the embedding space (semantic confusion), yielding weak discriminativeness for fine-grained attribute edits.
AI.method: DQE-CIR explicitly models target-relative relevance during training via two components: (1) Learnable attribute weighting that conditions on the modification text to emphasize distinctive visual features, improving language–vision alignment. (2) Target relative negative sampling that builds a similarity distribution w.r.t. the target and selects informative mid-zone negatives, excluding both easy negatives and ambiguous false negatives.
AI.result: Produces more discriminative query embeddings, better aligns visual features with textual modifications, and improves reliability for fine-grained attribute changes by avoiding pushing away semantically related but irrelevant candidates and by reducing confusion among similar modification intents.
AI.conclusion: By rethinking both feature weighting and negative selection, DQE-CIR mitigates relevance suppression and semantic confusion inherent in pure contrastive setups, leading to clearer, more distinctive query representations and more reliable fine-grained CIR. (Empirical specifics are not provided in the abstract.)

title: VietNormalizer: An Open-Source, Dependency-Free Python Library for Vietnamese Text Normalization in TTS and NLP Applications
summary: We present VietNormalizer1, an open-source, zero-dependency Python library for Vietnamese text normalization targeting Text-to-Speech (TTS) and Natural Language Processing (NLP) applications. Vietnamese text normalization is a critical yet underserved preprocessing step: real-world Vietnamese text is densely populated with non-standard words (NSWs), including numbers, dates, times, currency amounts, percentages, acronyms, and foreign-language terms, all of which must be converted to fully pronounceable Vietnamese words before TTS synthesis or downstream language processing. Existing Vietnamese normalization tools either require heavy neural dependencies while covering only a narrow subset of NSW classes, or are embedded within larger NLP toolkits without standalone installability. VietNormalizer addresses these gaps through a unified, rule-based pipeline that: (1) converts arbitrary integers, decimals, and large numbers to Vietnamese words; (2) normalizes dates and times to their spoken Vietnamese forms; (3) handles VND and USD currency amounts; (4) expands percentages; (5) resolves acronyms via a customizable CSV dictionary; (6) transliterates non-Vietnamese loanwords and foreign terms to Vietnamese phonetic approximations; and (7) performs Unicode normalization and emoji/special-character removal. All regular expression patterns are pre-compiled at initialization, enabling high-throughput batch processing with minimal memory overhead and no GPU or external API dependency. The library is installable via pip install vietnormalizer, available on PyPI and GitHub at https://github.com/nghimestudio/vietnormalizer, and released under the MIT license. We discuss the design decisions, limitations of existing approaches, and the generalizability of the rule-based normalization paradigm to other low-resource tonal and agglutinative languages.

AI.tldr: VietNormalizer is a lightweight, open-source, zero-dependency, rule-based Python library that normalizes Vietnamese text for TTS/NLP, covering numbers, dates/times, currency, percentages, acronyms, foreign terms, and text cleanup, optimized via precompiled regex and pip-installable under MIT.
AI.motivation: Real-world Vietnamese text contains many non-standard words that must be expanded to pronounceable forms before TTS or downstream NLP. Existing tools are heavy (neural), narrowly cover NSW classes, or are not standalone; there is a need for a comprehensive, efficient, easily deployable normalizer.
AI.method: A unified rule-based pipeline with precompiled regex patterns that: verbalizes integers/decimals/large numbers; normalizes dates/times; handles VND/USD; expands percentages; resolves acronyms via a customizable CSV dictionary; transliterates foreign/loanwords into Vietnamese phonetic approximations; and performs Unicode normalization plus emoji/special-character removal. No GPU or external API dependencies.
AI.result: A production-ready Python package (pip install vietnormalizer) on PyPI/GitHub under MIT license, providing high-throughput batch processing with minimal memory use and broad NSW coverage; includes discussion on design choices and generalizability to other low-resource tonal/agglutinative languages.
AI.conclusion: A zero-dependency, rule-based approach effectively fills a practical gap for Vietnamese text normalization in TTS/NLP, offering speed, coverage, and deployability, with potential transfer to similar languages; remaining challenges likely include edge-case handling, domain variability, and quality of foreign-term transliteration/dictionary coverage.

title: Long-Term Visual Localization in Dynamic Benthic Environments: A Dataset, Footprint-Based Ground Truth, and Visual Place Recognition Benchmark
summary: Long-term visual localization has the potential to reduce cost and improve mapping quality in optical benthic monitoring with autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs). Despite this potential, long-term visual localization in benthic environments remains understudied, primarily due to the lack of curated datasets for benchmarking. Moreover, limited georeferencing accuracy and image footprints necessitate precise geometric information for accurate ground-truthing. In this work, we address these gaps by presenting a curated dataset for long-term visual localization in benthic environments and a novel method to ground-truth visual localization results for near-nadir underwater imagery. Our dataset comprises georeferenced AUV imagery from five benthic reference sites, revisited over periods up to six years, and includes raw and color-corrected stereo imagery, camera calibrations, and sub-decimeter registered camera poses. To our knowledge, this is the first curated underwater dataset for long-term visual localization spanning multiple sites and photic-zone habitats. Our ground-truthing method estimates 3D seafloor image footprints and links camera views with overlapping footprints, ensuring that ground-truth links reflect shared visual content. Building on this dataset and ground truth, we benchmark eight state-of-the-art visual place recognition (VPR) methods and find that Recall@K is significantly lower on our dataset than on established terrestrial and underwater benchmarks. Finally, we compare our footprint-based ground truth to a traditional location-based ground truth and show that distance-threshold ground-truthing can overestimate VPR Recall@K at sites with rugged terrain and altitude variations. Together, the curated dataset, ground-truthing method, and VPR benchmark provide a stepping stone for advancing long-term visual localization in dynamic benthic environments.

AI.tldr: They release the first curated multi-site, multi-year underwater (benthic) dataset for long-term visual localization, introduce a 3D image-footprint ground-truthing method tailored to near-nadir AUV imagery, and show that state-of-the-art VPR methods perform markedly worse on it; traditional distance-based ground truth can overestimate recall in rugged terrain.
AI.motivation: Long-term visual localization underwater is underexplored due to the absence of curated benchmarks and the difficulty of creating accurate ground truth in benthic settings where georeferencing is imprecise and image footprints are small. Reliable evaluation requires geometry-aware correspondences rather than simple proximity.
AI.method: 1) Curate a dataset from five benthic reference sites revisited over up to six years, providing georeferenced AUV stereo imagery (raw and color-corrected), camera calibrations, and sub-decimeter registered poses. 2) Propose a footprint-based ground-truthing approach: estimate 3D seafloor image footprints for near-nadir views and link camera pairs whose footprints overlap, ensuring shared visual content. 3) Benchmark eight state-of-the-art visual place recognition (VPR) methods and compare footprint-based vs distance-threshold ground truth.
AI.result: - VPR Recall@K is significantly lower on this dataset than on established terrestrial and underwater benchmarks, indicating higher difficulty. - Distance-threshold (location-based) ground truth can overestimate VPR Recall@K at sites with rugged topography and varying altitude compared to the proposed footprint-based ground truth.
AI.conclusion: The released dataset, geometry-aware ground-truthing method, and accompanying benchmark create a solid foundation for advancing long-term visual localization in dynamic benthic environments and highlight the need for more robust VPR methods and evaluation protocols that respect true visual overlap.

title: Traces of Social Competence in Large Language Models
summary: The False Belief Test (FBT) has been the main method for assessing Theory of Mind (ToM) and related socio-cognitive competencies. For Large Language Models (LLMs), the reliability and explanatory potential of this test have remained limited due to issues like data contamination, insufficient model details, and inconsistent controls. We address these issues by testing 17 open-weight models on a balanced set of 192 FBT variants (Trott et al. 2023) using Bayesian Logistic regression to identify how model size and post-training affect socio-cognitive competence. We find that scaling model size benefits performance, but not strictly. A cross-over effect reveals that explicating propositional attitudes (X thinks) fundamentally alters response patterns. Instruction tuning partially mitigates this effect, but further reasoning-oriented finetuning amplifies it. In a case study analysing social reasoning ability throughout OLMo 2 training, we show that this cross-over effect emerges during pre-training, suggesting that models acquire stereotypical response patterns tied to mental-state vocabulary that can outweigh other scenario semantics. Finally, vector steering allows us to isolate a think vector as the causal driver of observed FBT behaviour.

AI.tldr: LLMs’ False Belief Test (FBT) performance is strongly shaped by mental‑state wording rather than robust belief reasoning: scaling helps but not monotonically; adding “X thinks” flips response patterns, partly dampened by instruction tuning yet amplified by reasoning finetuning; the effect arises during pretraining, and a controllable “think vector” causally drives these behaviors.
AI.motivation: FBT has been the de facto proxy for Theory of Mind (ToM) in LLMs, but concerns about contamination, weak controls, and limited interpretability undermine claims of socio‑cognitive competence. The paper aims to rigorously quantify how model size and post‑training shape FBT behavior and to uncover mechanistic drivers behind apparent ToM, distinguishing true situational reasoning from lexical heuristics.
AI.method: Test 17 open‑weight models on a balanced set of 192 FBT variants (Trott et al., 2023). Use Bayesian logistic regression to estimate effects of model size and post‑training (instruction vs reasoning‑oriented finetuning) and interactions with explicit propositional‑attitude phrasing (“X thinks”). Conduct a training‑trajectory case study on OLMo 2 to locate when effects emerge. Apply vector steering to identify and manipulate a representation direction (“think vector”) linked to mental‑state vocabulary and observe causal effects on FBT choices.
AI.result: - Larger models generally perform better on FBTs, but gains are not strictly monotonic across sizes and post‑training regimes. - A cross‑over effect: explicitly stating propositional attitudes (“X thinks …”) fundamentally changes response patterns, often outweighing scenario semantics. - Instruction tuning attenuates this sensitivity; reasoning‑oriented finetuning accentuates it. - In OLMo 2, the cross‑over effect appears during pretraining, indicating acquisition of stereotyped mappings tied to mental‑state terms. - Vector steering isolates a “think vector” that causally modulates FBT responses, accounting for observed behaviors.
AI.conclusion: FBT scores can misrepresent LLM ToM: models often rely on mental‑state word cues rather than reasoning about beliefs. These heuristics emerge in pretraining, can be modulated by downstream training, and are causally linked to a steerable representational direction. Future ToM evaluations should minimize lexical cueing, report robust controls, and separate scaling and post‑training effects; training that reduces over‑reliance on propositional‑attitude cues may yield more genuine socio‑cognitive reasoning.

title: TumorFlow: Physics-Guided Longitudinal MRI Synthesis of Glioblastoma Growth
summary: Glioblastoma exhibits diverse, infiltrative, and patient-specific growth patterns that are only partially visible on routine MRI, making it difficult to reliably assess true tumor extent and personalize treatment planning and follow-up. We present a biophysically-conditioned generative framework that synthesizes biologically realistic 3D brain MRI volumes from estimated, spatially continuous tumor-concentration fields. Our approach combines a generative model with tumor-infiltration maps that can be propagated through time using a biophysical growth model, enabling fine-grained control over tumor shape and growth while preserving patient anatomy. This enables us to synthesize consistent tumor growth trajectories directly in the space of real patients, providing interpretable, controllable estimation of tumor infiltration and progression beyond what is explicitly observed in imaging. We evaluate the framework on longitudinal glioblastoma cases and demonstrate that it can generate temporally coherent sequences with realistic changes in tumor appearance and surrounding tissue response. These results suggest that integrating mechanistic tumor growth priors with modern generative modeling can provide a practical tool for patient-specific progression visualization and for generating controlled synthetic data to support downstream neuro-oncology workflows. In longitudinal extrapolation, we achieve a consistent 75% Dice overlap with the biophysical model while maintaining a constant PSNR of 25 in the surrounding tissue. Our code is available at: https://github.com/valentin-biller/lgm.git

AI.tldr: They propose a biophysically-conditioned generative model that turns continuous tumor-infiltration fields into realistic, temporally coherent 3D brain MRIs, enabling controllable visualization and extrapolation of glioblastoma growth within a patient’s anatomy.
AI.motivation: Routine MRI only partially reveals glioblastoma’s true infiltrative extent, hindering reliable assessment, personalization of treatment, and longitudinal follow-up. There is a need for interpretable, controllable models that estimate and visualize hidden infiltration and plausible progression trajectories in patient space.
AI.method: Combine a modern generative image model with mechanistic tumor-infiltration maps. The maps arise from a biophysical growth model and can be propagated through time (spatially continuous tumor-concentration fields). Conditioning the generative model on these fields yields realistic MRIs aligned with patient anatomy and allows fine-grained control of tumor shape and growth over time.
AI.result: On longitudinal glioblastoma cases, the framework generates temporally coherent MRI sequences showing realistic tumor evolution and tissue response. In longitudinal extrapolation, it maintains ~75% Dice overlap with the biophysical model and a constant PSNR of ~25 in surrounding tissue. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic priors combined with generative modeling provide a practical, interpretable tool for patient-specific visualization of glioblastoma progression and for generating controlled synthetic datasets to support neuro-oncology workflows.

title: Bielik-Q2-Sharp: A Comparative Study of Extreme 2-bit Quantization Methods for a Polish 11B Language Model
comment: 17 pages, 13 tables. All models and Hessians available at https://huggingface.co/Jakubrd4
summary: We present Bielik-Q2-Sharp, the first systematic academic evaluation of extreme 2-bit quantization applied to a Polish large language model. Using Bielik-11B-v2.3-Instruct (11B parameters, Mistral architecture) as our base model, we compare six state-of-the-art post-training quantization methods -- QuIP#, SpinQuant+GPTQ, ButterflyQuant, QTIP, VPTQ, and AQLM -- all calibrated on a Polish-language corpus (CulturaX-PL) with shared Hessian matrices. Our best variant (QuIP# E8P12) achieves 71.92% across 22 Polish benchmarks versus 72.07% for the IQ2_XXS baseline -- within statistical noise, at a modest size premium (3.26 GB vs. ~2.6 GB). On eq_bench, our method scores 47.14 versus 43.53 (+3.6pp), suggesting superior preservation of higher-order reasoning. QTIP achieves the best per-bit efficiency (79.4% MC acc_norm at ~2.4 bpw, 3.27 GB), matching VPTQ's quality at 35% smaller size. We additionally document a MC-generation dissociation phenomenon where rotation-based methods preserve log-likelihood quality but fail catastrophically at autoregressive generation. The entire project was conducted by a single independent researcher on cloud GPUs (vast.ai) within a $285 budget. All models, Hessians, and evaluation logs are publicly available.

AI.tldr: First systematic academic study of extreme 2‑bit post‑training quantization for a Polish LLM (Bielik‑11B). QuIP# (E8P12) essentially matches an IQ2_XXS baseline on 22 Polish benchmarks and improves higher‑order reasoning; QTIP delivers the best per‑bit efficiency. Rotation‑based methods can look good on MC/log‑likelihood yet fail at autoregressive generation.
AI.motivation: Make Polish LLMs deployable on modest hardware via extreme (≈2‑bit) quantization while preserving task quality, and fill a gap: no systematic academic evaluation for Polish models. Also probe whether common calibration proxies (MC/log‑likelihood) faithfully predict generation quality.
AI.method: Use Bielik‑11B‑v2.3‑Instruct (Mistral) as the base. Compare six SOTA PTQ methods—QuIP#, SpinQuant+GPTQ, ButterflyQuant, QTIP, VPTQ, AQLM—calibrated on Polish CulturaX‑PL with shared Hessians. Evaluate across 22 Polish benchmarks plus eq_bench; report accuracy, per‑bit efficiency, model size. Analyze a dissociation between MC log‑likelihood and autoregressive generation. Train/evaluate on cloud GPUs with a tight budget; release models, Hessians, logs.
AI.result: QuIP# E8P12 reaches 71.92% across 22 Polish tasks vs. 72.07% for IQ2_XXS (statistically indistinguishable) at 3.26 GB vs. ~2.6 GB. On eq_bench, QuIP# scores 47.14 vs. 43.53 (+3.6 pp), indicating better higher‑order reasoning retention. QTIP attains the best per‑bit efficiency (79.4% MC acc_norm at ~2.4 bpw, 3.27 GB), matching VPTQ while ~35% smaller. Observed MC‑generation dissociation: rotation‑based schemes preserve log‑likelihood yet can catastrophically fail in autoregressive generation.
AI.conclusion: Extreme 2‑bit PTQ of a Polish 11B LLM is viable: overall accuracy can match strong 2‑bit baselines and even improve reasoning on some tasks. QTIP is the most size‑efficient; QuIP# balances accuracy and reasoning. However, rotation‑based approaches may be misleading if judged only by MC/log‑likelihood—generation must be tested explicitly. Open artifacts and low‑cost setup support reproducibility and practical adoption.

title: Revisiting the Role of Foundation Models in Cell-Level Histopathological Image Analysis under Small-Patch Constraints -- Effects of Training Data Scale and Blur Perturbations on CNNs and Vision Transformers
summary: Background and objective: Cell-level pathological image analysis requires working with extremely small image patches (40x40 pixels), far below standard ImageNet resolutions. It remains unclear whether modern deep learning architectures and foundation models can learn robust and scalable representations under this constraint. We systematically evaluated architectural suitability and data-scale effects for small-patch cell classification. Methods: We analyzed 303 colorectal cancer specimens with CD103/CD8 immunostaining, generating 185,432 annotated cell images. Eight task-specific architectures were trained from scratch at multiple data scales (FlagLimit: 256--16,384 samples per class), and three foundation models were evaluated via linear probing and fine-tuning after resizing inputs to 224x224 pixels. Robustness to blur was assessed using pre- and post-resize Gaussian perturbations. Results: Task-specific models improved consistently with increasing data scale, whereas foundation models saturated at moderate sample sizes. A Vision Transformer optimized for small patches (CustomViT) achieved the highest accuracy, outperforming all foundation models with substantially lower inference cost. Blur robustness was comparable across architectures, with no qualitative advantage observed for foundation models. Conclusion: For cell-level classification under extreme spatial constraints, task-specific architectures are more effective and efficient than foundation models once sufficient training data are available. Higher clean accuracy does not imply superior robustness, and large pre-trained models offer limited benefit in the small-patch regime.

AI.tldr: On 40×40-pixel cell images, purpose-built small-patch models trained from scratch scale better with data and beat resized foundation models in accuracy and efficiency; robustness to blur is similar across models.
AI.motivation: Cell-level pathology requires tiny inputs far below ImageNet scale, raising uncertainty about whether modern architectures or large pretrained foundation models can learn strong and robust features under such extreme spatial constraints.
AI.method: 185,432 annotated cells from 303 colorectal cancer CD103/CD8 slides. Eight task-specific architectures trained from scratch across data scales (256–16,384 samples/class). Three foundation models evaluated via linear probing and fine-tuning after upscaling inputs to 224×224. Robustness tested with Gaussian blur applied before and after resizing.
AI.result: Task-specific models improved monotonically with more data; foundation models saturated at moderate data sizes. A small-patch-optimized ViT (CustomViT) achieved the top accuracy with substantially lower inference cost than all foundation models. Blur robustness was broadly comparable; foundation models showed no qualitative advantage.
AI.conclusion: For extreme small-patch cell classification, specialized architectures are more effective and efficient once sufficient training data exist. Higher clean accuracy does not guarantee better robustness, and large pretrained models provide limited benefit in this regime.

title: When Do Language Models Endorse Limitations on Human Rights Principles?
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly mediate global information access with the potential to shape public discourse, their alignment with universal human rights principles becomes important to ensure that these rights are abided by in high stakes AI-mediated interactions. In this paper, we evaluate how LLMs navigate trade-offs involving the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), leveraging 1,152 synthetically generated scenarios across 24 rights articles and eight languages. Our analysis of eleven major LLMs reveals systematic biases where models: (1) accept limiting Economic, Social, and Cultural rights more often than Political and Civil rights, (2) demonstrate significant cross-linguistic variation with elevated endorsement rates of rights-limiting actions in Chinese and Hindi compared to English or Romanian, (3) show substantial susceptibility to prompt-based steering, and (4) exhibit noticeable differences between Likert and open-ended responses, highlighting critical challenges in LLM preference assessment.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks 11 major LLMs against 1,152 synthetic scenarios derived from the UDHR (24 rights, 8 languages) and finds systematic, language-dependent biases and sensitivity to prompt framing, with models more willing to limit economic/social/cultural rights than civil/political ones and giving different answers across response formats.
AI.motivation: LLMs increasingly mediate information and can influence rights-relevant decisions. Ensuring they respect universal human rights requires understanding how they handle trade-offs among UDHR rights, across languages and prompts.
AI.method: Construct a rights-trade-off evaluation: 1,152 synthetic scenarios spanning 24 UDHR articles and 8 languages. Query 11 major LLMs using Likert-scale and open-ended formats; test prompt-based steering; measure endorsement of rights-limiting actions by right type and language.
AI.result: Across models, (1) they more often accept limits on Economic, Social, and Cultural rights than on Political and Civil rights; (2) they show cross-linguistic variation, endorsing rights-limiting actions more in Chinese and Hindi than in English or Romanian; (3) they are highly steerable by prompt phrasing; (4) Likert vs open-ended responses diverge, complicating preference assessment.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are not consistently aligned with UDHR principles, with biases across right categories, languages, prompt phrasing, and response format. This calls for multilingual, rights-aware alignment methods, robust prompt-invariance, and evaluation protocols that reconcile format-induced discrepancies.

title: EgoPoseFormer v2: Accurate Egocentric Human Motion Estimation for AR/VR
summary: Egocentric human motion estimation is essential for AR/VR experiences, yet remains challenging due to limited body coverage from the egocentric viewpoint, frequent occlusions, and scarce labeled data. We present EgoPoseFormer v2, a method that addresses these challenges through two key contributions: (1) a transformer-based model for temporally consistent and spatially grounded body pose estimation, and (2) an auto-labeling system that enables the use of large unlabeled datasets for training. Our model is fully differentiable, introduces identity-conditioned queries, multi-view spatial refinement, causal temporal attention, and supports both keypoints and parametric body representations under a constant compute budget. The auto-labeling system scales learning to tens of millions of unlabeled frames via uncertainty-aware semi-supervised training. The system follows a teacher-student schema to generate pseudo-labels and guide training with uncertainty distillation, enabling the model to generalize to different environments. On the EgoBody3M benchmark, with a 0.8 ms latency on GPU, our model outperforms two state-of-the-art methods by 12.2% and 19.4% in accuracy, and reduces temporal jitter by 22.2% and 51.7%. Furthermore, our auto-labeling system further improves the wrist MPJPE by 13.1%.

AI.tldr: EgoPoseFormer v2 is a fast (0.8 ms) transformer-based egocentric human pose estimator that achieves large accuracy and stability gains by combining identity-conditioned queries, causal temporal attention, multi-view spatial refinement, and a massive-scale, uncertainty-aware teacher–student auto-labeling pipeline.
AI.motivation: Egocentric AR/VR motion capture is difficult because head-mounted views see only parts of the body, suffer frequent self-occlusion, and lack large labeled datasets. There is a need for temporally consistent, spatially grounded pose estimation that can be trained at scale without exhaustive manual annotation.
AI.method: A fully differentiable transformer architecture featuring identity-conditioned queries, causal temporal attention for online consistency, and multi-view spatial refinement. It supports both keypoint and parametric body representations under a fixed compute budget. Training is scaled via an uncertainty-aware semi-supervised auto-labeling system using a teacher–student framework with pseudo-labels and uncertainty distillation, enabling learning from tens of millions of unlabeled frames and better cross-environment generalization.
AI.result: On the EgoBody3M benchmark, the model runs at 0.8 ms latency on GPU, surpasses two state-of-the-art methods by 12.2% and 19.4% in accuracy, and reduces temporal jitter by 22.2% and 51.7%. The auto-labeling system further improves wrist MPJPE by 13.1%.
AI.conclusion: EgoPoseFormer v2 delivers accurate, temporally stable, and spatially grounded egocentric pose estimation in real time, while its uncertainty-aware semi-supervised training scales to vast unlabeled data and enhances generalization, making it practical for AR/VR applications.

title: Retrieval or Representation? Reassessing Benchmark Gaps in Multilingual and Visually Rich RAG
comment: ICLR 2026 Workshop I Can't Believe It's Not Better: Where Large Language Models Need to Improve
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a common way to ground language models in external documents and up-to-date information. Classical retrieval systems relied on lexical methods such as BM25, which rank documents by term overlap with corpus-level weighting. End-to-end multimodal retrievers trained on large query-document datasets claim substantial improvements over these approaches, especially for multilingual documents with complex visual layouts. We demonstrate that better document representation is the primary driver of benchmark improvements. By systematically varying transcription and preprocessing methods while holding the retrieval mechanism fixed, we demonstrate that BM25 can recover large gaps on multilingual and visual benchmarks. Our findings call for decomposed evaluation benchmarks that separately measure transcription and retrieval capabilities, enabling the field to correctly attribute progress and focus effort where it matters.

AI.tldr: Benchmark gains attributed to end-to-end multimodal retrievers mostly come from better document transcription/representation; with strong preprocessing, classical BM25 can close the gap, motivating decomposed evaluations that separate transcription from retrieval.
AI.motivation: RAG systems need accurate grounding in diverse, up-to-date documents, including multilingual and visually complex pages. Recent end-to-end multimodal retrievers report big wins over lexical methods like BM25, but it is unclear whether improvements stem from the retriever itself or from upstream document representation (OCR, layout, preprocessing).
AI.method: Hold the retrieval algorithm fixed and systematically vary transcription and preprocessing quality (e.g., OCR, layout-aware text extraction, normalization). Compare performance across multilingual and visually complex benchmarks, contrasting BM25 under different representations with end-to-end multimodal retrievers.
AI.result: When document representations are improved, BM25 regains a large portion of the purported gap on multilingual and visual-layout benchmarks, indicating that representation quality—not the retrieval mechanism per se—drives much of the observed improvements.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation should be decomposed to independently measure transcription/representation and retrieval. Progress should be attributed correctly, and effort should prioritize better document representations; simple lexical retrieval can remain competitive given strong preprocessing.

title: CLIP-Guided Multi-Task Regression for Multi-View Plant Phenotyping
summary: Modeling plant growth dynamics plays a central role in modern agricultural research. However, learning robust predictors from multi-view plant imagery remains challenging due to strong viewpoint redundancy and viewpoint-dependent appearance changes. We propose a level-aware vision language framework that jointly predicts plant age and leaf count using a single multi-task model built on CLIP embeddings. Our method aggregates rotational views into angle-invariant representations and conditions visual features on lightweight text priors encoding viewpoint level for stable prediction under incomplete or unordered inputs. On the GroMo25 benchmark, our approach reduces mean age MAE from 7.74 to 3.91 and mean leaf-count MAE from 5.52 to 3.08 compared to the GroMo baseline, corresponding to improvements of 49.5% and 44.2%, respectively. The unified formulation simplifies the pipeline by replacing the conventional dual-model setup while improving robustness to missing views. The models and code is available at: https://github.com/SimonWarmers/CLIP-MVP
