Through analyzing the evolution and influence of internal representations under various settings, we reveal that representation diversity across blocks is a crucial factor for effective learning. Based on this key insight, we propose DiverseDiT, a novel framework that explicitly promotes representation diversity. DiverseDiT incorporates long residual connections to diversify input representations across blocks and a representation diversity loss to encourage blocks to learn distinct features. Extensive experiments on ImageNet 256x256 and 512x512 demonstrate that our DiverseDiT yields consistent performance gains and convergence acceleration when applied to different backbones with various sizes, even when tested on the challenging one-step generation setting. Furthermore, we show that DiverseDiT is complementary to existing representation learning techniques, leading to further performance gains. Our work provides valuable insights into the representation learning dynamics of DiTs and offers a practical approach for enhancing their performance.

AI.tldr: They study how Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) learn internal features, find that diversity of representations across transformer blocks is key, and introduce DiverseDiT—adding long residual connections plus a diversity-promoting loss—to boost diversity, yielding faster convergence and better ImageNet FIDs (including one-step generation) across multiple backbones and sizes, and stacking with other rep-learning methods.
AI.motivation: DiTs scale well for image synthesis but their internal representation learning is poorly understood. Prior methods (e.g., REPA) rely on external pretrained encoders to align representations rather than improving DiTs’ own feature formation. The paper aims to diagnose what properties of internal features matter and exploit that to enhance training and performance natively within DiTs.
AI.method: 1) Empirical analysis of representation dynamics in DiTs across blocks/settings to assess evolution and influence of internal features; 2) Key insight: cross-block representation diversity is crucial; 3) DiverseDiT framework: (a) architectural change—long residual connections that feed earlier features to later blocks to diversify block inputs; (b) training loss—a representation diversity loss encouraging different blocks to learn distinct features; 4) Apply as a plug-in to various DiT backbones/sizes; evaluate on ImageNet at 256×256 and 512×512, including one-step generation, and combine with existing representation-learning techniques.
AI.result: Consistent performance gains and faster convergence on ImageNet 256 and 512 across different DiT backbones and scales; improvements persist in the challenging one-step generation regime; method is complementary to external-alignment techniques, providing additional gains when combined.
AI.conclusion: Representation diversity across transformer blocks is a primary driver of effective representation learning in DiTs. Encouraging diversity via long residuals and a diversity loss improves sample quality and training efficiency, offers a practical, backbone-agnostic enhancement, and can be combined with other techniques for further benefits.

title: TaxonRL: Reinforcement Learning with Intermediate Rewards for Interpretable Fine-Grained Visual Reasoning
summary: Traditional vision-language models struggle with contrastive fine-grained taxonomic reasoning, particularly when distinguishing between visually similar species within the same genus or family. We introduce TaxonRL, a reinforcement learning approach using Group Relative Policy Optimization with intermediate rewards that decomposes the reasoning process into hierarchical taxonomic predictions. Our method incentivizes models to explicitly reason about species-level, genus-level, and family-level features before making final classifications. This structured approach is designed not only to boost accuracy but also to yield a transparent, verifiable decision-making process. On the challenging Birds-to-Words dataset, TaxonRL achieves 91.7\% average accuracy, exceeding human performance (77.3\%) while generating interpretable reasoning traces. We demonstrate strong cross-domain generalization, showing substantial gains in primate and marine species verification. Our results establish that enforcing structured, hierarchical reasoning provides a powerful and transferable framework for fine-grained visual discrimination.

AI.tldr: TaxonRL uses reinforcement learning with hierarchical intermediate rewards to force explicit family/genus/species reasoning, yielding interpretable traces and state-of-the-art accuracy (91.7%) on Birds-to-Words, surpassing humans, with cross-domain gains.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained vision-language classification often fails on contrastive, within-genus/family distinctions and lacks transparent, verifiable reasoning. The work aims to improve both accuracy and interpretability via structured taxonomic reasoning.
AI.method: A reinforcement learning framework (Group Relative Policy Optimization) with intermediate rewards that decomposes predictions into hierarchical taxonomic steps (species, genus, family). The agent is incentivized to articulate level-specific features before committing to a final label, producing reasoning traces.
AI.result: On Birds-to-Words, the method achieves 91.7% average accuracy, exceeding a reported human baseline of 77.3%, and produces interpretable reasoning traces. It generalizes to other domains, showing substantial gains in primate and marine species verification tasks.
AI.conclusion: Imposing structured, hierarchical reasoning via RL provides a powerful and transferable approach for fine-grained visual discrimination, improving both performance and interpretability.

title: MOO: A Multi-view Oriented Observations Dataset for Viewpoint Analysis in Cattle Re-Identification
summary: Animal re-identification (ReID) faces critical challenges due to viewpoint variations, particularly in Aerial-Ground (AG-ReID) settings where models must match individuals across drastic elevation changes. However, existing datasets lack the precise angular annotations required to systematically analyze these geometric variations. To address this, we introduce the Multi-view Oriented Observation (MOO) dataset, a large-scale synthetic AG-ReID dataset of $1,000$ cattle individuals captured from $128$ uniformly sampled viewpoints ($128,000$ annotated images). Using this controlled dataset, we quantify the influence of elevation and identify a critical elevation threshold, above which models generalize significantly better to unseen views. Finally, we validate the transferability to real-world applications in both zero-shot and supervised settings, demonstrating performance gains across four real-world cattle datasets and confirming that synthetic geometric priors effectively bridge the domain gap. Collectively, this dataset and analysis lay the foundation for future model development in cross-view animal ReID. MOO is publicly available at https://github.com/TurtleSmoke/MOO.

AI.tldr: They release MOO, a large synthetic aerial–ground cattle ReID dataset with precise viewing-angle annotations (1,000 identities × 128 viewpoints = 128k images), use it to quantify how elevation affects cross-view generalization, discover a critical elevation threshold that improves robustness to unseen views, and show the synthetic geometric priors transfer to four real-world datasets in zero-shot and supervised settings.
AI.motivation: Animal ReID, especially across aerial vs ground views, suffers from severe viewpoint/elevation changes. Existing datasets lack accurate orientation labels, preventing systematic study of geometric factors and limiting model generalization across views.
AI.method: Construct a controlled, multi-view synthetic dataset (MOO) of cattle with 128 uniformly sampled viewpoints per individual and precise angular annotations. Perform controlled analyses isolating elevation effects to find a generalization threshold. Validate transfer by training with MOO and evaluating on four real cattle datasets in zero-shot and supervised regimes.
AI.result: Quantifies elevation’s impact on AG-ReID and identifies a key elevation threshold above which models generalize markedly better to novel views. Models pretrained or augmented with MOO’s synthetic geometric priors achieve consistent performance gains on four real-world cattle datasets in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings, indicating effective domain-gap bridging.
AI.conclusion: Precisely annotated synthetic multi-view data can encode geometric priors that materially improve cross-view animal ReID. MOO offers a foundation for studying viewpoint effects and developing more view-robust ReID models, with code/data publicly available.

title: DeNuC: Decoupling Nuclei Detection and Classification in Histopathology
summary: Pathology Foundation Models (FMs) have shown strong performance across a wide range of pathology image representation and diagnostic tasks. However, FMs do not exhibit the expected performance advantage over traditional specialized models in Nuclei Detection and Classification (NDC). In this work, we reveal that jointly optimizing nuclei detection and classification leads to severe representation degradation in FMs. Moreover, we identify that the substantial intrinsic disparity in task difficulty between nuclei detection and nuclei classification renders joint NDC optimization unnecessarily computationally burdensome for the detection stage. To address these challenges, we propose DeNuC, a simple yet effective method designed to break through existing bottlenecks by Decoupling Nuclei detection and Classification. DeNuC employs a lightweight model for accurate nuclei localization, subsequently leveraging a pathology FM to encode input images and query nucleus-specific features based on the detected coordinates for classification. Extensive experiments on three widely used benchmarks demonstrate that DeNuC effectively unlocks the representational potential of FMs for NDC and significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, DeNuC improves F1 scores by 4.2% and 3.6% (or higher) on the BRCAM2C and PUMA datasets, respectively, while using only 16% (or fewer) trainable parameters compared to other methods. Code is available at https://github.com/ZijiangY1116/DeNuC.

AI.tldr: DeNuC decouples nuclei detection from classification: a lightweight detector localizes nuclei, then a pathology foundation model encodes images and is queried at detected coordinates for nucleus-wise classification, yielding higher F1 with far fewer trainable parameters than prior joint NDC approaches.
AI.motivation: Pathology foundation models excel broadly but fail to show clear advantages on nuclei detection and classification (NDC). Jointly training detection and classification degrades FM representations, and the disparity in task difficulty makes joint optimization unnecessarily heavy for detection.
AI.method: Use a small detector to accurately localize nuclei, then use a pathology FM to encode the image and extract nucleus-specific features by querying at detected coordinates; perform classification on these FM features. This decouples training/optimization of detection and classification, preserving FM representations and reducing computation for detection.
AI.result: Across three benchmarks, DeNuC outperforms state-of-the-art NDC methods. It improves F1 by 4.2% on BRCAM2C and by 3.6% or more on PUMA, while using 16% or fewer trainable parameters than competing methods.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling detection from classification overcomes representational degradation and unnecessary compute in joint NDC training, unlocking the potential of pathology FMs and delivering new SOTA performance with greater parameter efficiency; code is publicly available.

title: A Unified Framework for Joint Detection of Lacunes and Enlarged Perivascular Spaces
summary: Cerebral small vessel disease (CSVD) markers, specifically enlarged perivascular spaces (EPVS) and lacunae, present a unique challenge in medical image analysis due to their radiological mimicry. Standard segmentation networks struggle with feature interference and extreme class imbalance when handling these divergent targets simultaneously. To address these issues, we propose a morphology-decoupled framework where Zero-Initialized Gated Cross-Task Attention exploits dense EPVS context to guide sparse lacune detection. Furthermore, biological and topological consistency are enforced via a mixed-supervision strategy integrating Mutual Exclusion and Centerline Dice losses. Finally, we introduce an Anatomically-Informed Inference Calibration mechanism to dynamically suppress false positives based on tissue semantics. Extensive 5-folds cross-validation on the VALDO 2021 dataset (N=40) demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, notably surpassing task winners in lacunae detection precision (71.1%, p=0.01) and F1-score (62.6%, p=0.03). Furthermore, evaluation on the external EPAD cohort (N=1762) confirms the model's robustness for large-scale population studies. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A morphology‑decoupled, multitask framework leverages EPVS context to detect sparse lacunae, adds biologically/topologically consistent losses, and applies anatomy‑aware post‑hoc calibration, yielding state‑of‑the‑art lacune detection on VALDO and robust generalization to EPAD.
AI.motivation: EPVS and lacunae look radiologically similar but differ markedly in prevalence and morphology. Joint segmentation suffers from feature interference and severe class imbalance, leading to poor lacune detection and false positives.
AI.method: 1) Zero‑Initialized Gated Cross‑Task Attention to transfer dense EPVS contextual cues to guide sparse lacune detection while limiting interference. 2) Mixed supervision with Mutual Exclusion and Centerline Dice losses to enforce biological and topological consistency. 3) Anatomically‑Informed Inference Calibration to suppress false positives using tissue semantics. The approach is evaluated with 5‑fold CV on VALDO (N=40) and externally on EPAD (N=1762).
AI.result: On VALDO 2021, the method achieves state‑of‑the‑art lacune detection, surpassing prior task winners in precision (71.1%, p=0.01) and F1 (62.6%, p=0.03). External testing on EPAD (N=1762) indicates robust generalization for population studies.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling morphology, guiding lacune detection with EPVS context, enforcing biological/topological constraints, and calibrating predictions with anatomy collectively reduce false positives and mitigate imbalance/interference, enabling accurate and scalable CSVD marker analysis; code to be released upon acceptance.

title: SPRINT: Semi-supervised Prototypical Representation for Few-Shot Class-Incremental Tabular Learning
summary: Real-world systems must continuously adapt to novel concepts from limited data without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. While Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) is established in computer vision, its application to tabular domains remains largely unexplored. Unlike images, tabular streams (e.g., logs, sensors) offer abundant unlabeled data, a scarcity of expert annotations and negligible storage costs, features ignored by existing vision-based methods that rely on restrictive buffers. We introduce SPRINT, the first FSCIL framework tailored for tabular distributions. SPRINT introduces a mixed episodic training strategy that leverages confidence-based pseudo-labeling to enrich novel class representations and exploits low storage costs to retain base class history. Extensive evaluation across six diverse benchmarks spanning cybersecurity, healthcare, and ecological domains, demonstrates SPRINT's cross-domain robustness. It achieves a state-of-the-art average accuracy of 77.37% (5-shot), outperforming the strongest incremental baseline by 4.45%.

AI.tldr: SPRINT is a new few-shot class-incremental learning framework designed for tabular data streams, combining mixed episodic training, confidence-based pseudo-labeling, and inexpensive retention of base-class history to avoid forgetting, achieving state-of-the-art results across six domains.
AI.motivation: Tabular real-world streams (logs, sensors, medical records) have plentiful unlabeled data, scarce expert labels, and cheap storage—conditions poorly matched by vision-centric FSCIL methods that rely on restrictive buffers. There is a need to continuously learn novel classes from few labels without forgetting past knowledge in tabular settings.
AI.method: Introduce SPRINT, an FSCIL framework tailored to tabular distributions. It uses a mixed episodic training regimen that interleaves base and novel data, applies confidence-based pseudo-labeling to expand novel-class representations from abundant unlabeled samples, and exploits low storage costs to retain rich base-class history rather than minimal buffers.
AI.result: Across six benchmarks in cybersecurity, healthcare, and ecology, SPRINT attains an average 77.37% accuracy in the 5-shot setting, exceeding the strongest incremental baseline by 4.45%, demonstrating cross-domain robustness.
AI.conclusion: SPRINT establishes the first tabular FSCIL approach that effectively leverages unlabeled data and inexpensive storage to mitigate forgetting and improve novel-class learning, delivering state-of-the-art performance and broad robustness across domains.

title: EmbodiedSplat: Online Feed-Forward Semantic 3DGS for Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Understanding
summary: Understanding a 3D scene immediately with its exploration is essential for embodied tasks, where an agent must construct and comprehend the 3D scene in an online and nearly real-time manner. In this study, we propose EmbodiedSplat, an online feed-forward 3DGS for open-vocabulary scene understanding that enables simultaneous online 3D reconstruction and 3D semantic understanding from the streaming images. Unlike existing open-vocabulary 3DGS methods which are typically restricted to either offline or per-scene optimization setting, our objectives are two-fold: 1) Reconstructs the semantic-embedded 3DGS of the entire scene from over 300 streaming images in an online manner. 2) Highly generalizable to novel scenes with feed-forward design and supports nearly real-time 3D semantic reconstruction when combined with real-time 2D models. To achieve these objectives, we propose an Online Sparse Coefficients Field with a CLIP Global Codebook where it binds the 2D CLIP embeddings to each 3D Gaussian while minimizing memory consumption and preserving the full semantic generalizability of CLIP. Furthermore, we generate 3D geometric-aware CLIP features by aggregating the partial point cloud of 3DGS through 3D U-Net to compensate the 3D geometric prior to 2D-oriented language embeddings. Extensive experiments on diverse indoor datasets, including ScanNet, ScanNet++, and Replica, demonstrate both the effectiveness and efficiency of our method. Check out our project page in https://0nandon.github.io/EmbodiedSplat/.

AI.tldr: EmbodiedSplat is an online, feed‑forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) approach that jointly reconstructs geometry and open‑vocabulary semantics from streaming images, achieving near real‑time, generalizable 3D semantic maps by binding CLIP features to 3D Gaussians with low memory overhead and adding 3D geometry-aware semantics via a 3D U‑Net.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents require immediate, online 3D scene understanding to act effectively, but existing open‑vocabulary 3DGS methods are largely offline or require per‑scene optimization, hindering real‑time deployment and generalization. There is also a need to couple rich language semantics (e.g., CLIP) to 3D representations without excessive memory cost and with stronger 3D geometric priors.
AI.method: The method maintains an Online Sparse Coefficients Field tied to a CLIP Global Codebook that associates compact semantic coefficients with each 3D Gaussian, preserving CLIP’s open‑vocabulary generality while minimizing memory. It streams >300 images to build a semantic‑embedded 3DGS in a feed‑forward manner. To inject 3D priors into 2D language features, it aggregates partial 3DGS point clouds and processes them with a 3D U‑Net to create geometry‑aware CLIP features. The pipeline supports near real‑time 3D semantic reconstruction when combined with real‑time 2D models.
AI.result: Across indoor benchmarks (ScanNet, ScanNet++, Replica), the approach demonstrates effective and efficient online 3D reconstruction with open‑vocabulary semantics, indicating strong generalization to novel scenes and near real‑time performance; it reconstructs whole‑scene, semantic‑embedded 3DGS from streaming image sequences (>300 images).
AI.conclusion: EmbodiedSplat enables simultaneous online 3D reconstruction and open‑vocabulary semantic understanding in a memory‑efficient, feed‑forward framework, improving practicality for embodied tasks. Its CLIP codebook plus 3D U‑Net yields geometry‑aware semantics that generalize to new scenes, supporting near real‑time deployment.

title: RANGER: Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts with Adaptive Retrieval Re-ranking for Pathology Report Generation
summary: Pathology report generation remains a relatively under-explored downstream task, primarily due to the gigapixel scale and complex morphological heterogeneity of Whole Slide Images (WSIs). Existing pathology report generation frameworks typically employ transformer architectures, relying on a homogeneous decoder architecture and static knowledge retrieval integration. Such architectures limit generative specialization and may introduce noisy external guidance during the report generation process. To address these limitations, we propose RANGER, a sparsely-gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework with adaptive retrieval re-ranking for pathology report generation. Specifically, we integrate a sparsely gated MoE into the decoder, along with noisy top-$k$ routing and load-balancing regularization, to enable dynamic expert specialization across various diagnostic patterns. Additionally, we introduce an adaptive retrieval re-ranking module that selectively refines retrieved memory from a knowledge base before integration, reducing noise and improving semantic alignment based on visual feature representations. We perform extensive experiments on the PathText-BRCA dataset and demonstrate consistent improvements over existing approaches across standard natural language generation metrics. Our full RANGER model achieves optimal performance on PathText dataset, reaching BLEU-1 to BLEU-4 scores of 0.4598, 0.3044, 0.2036, and 0.1435, respectively, with METEOR of 0.1883, and ROUGE-L of 0.3038, validating the effectiveness of dynamic expert routing and adaptive knowledge refinement for semantically grounded pathology report generation.

AI.tldr: RANGER is a pathology report generator that combines a sparsely-gated MoE decoder with adaptive knowledge retrieval re-ranking to produce more accurate, semantically aligned reports from whole-slide images, outperforming prior methods on PathText-BRCA.
AI.motivation: Generating pathology reports from gigapixel WSIs is difficult due to extreme scale and morphological heterogeneity. Prevailing transformer-based systems use homogeneous decoders and static knowledge retrieval, which limit specialization and can inject noisy external guidance.
AI.method: Embed a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts into the decoder with noisy top-k routing and load-balancing regularization to promote dynamic expert specialization across diagnostic patterns. Add an adaptive retrieval re-ranking module that filters and reorders knowledge-base memory based on alignment with visual features before integration.
AI.result: On the PathText-BRCA dataset, RANGER consistently improves over prior approaches, achieving BLEU-1/2/3/4 of 0.4598/0.3044/0.2036/0.1435, METEOR 0.1883, and ROUGE-L 0.3038.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic expert routing plus adaptive knowledge refinement reduces retrieval noise and enhances semantic grounding, yielding stronger pathology report generation performance.

title: A Hypertoroidal Covering for Perfect Color Equivariance
summary: When the color distribution of input images changes at inference, the performance of conventional neural network architectures drops considerably. A few researchers have begun to incorporate prior knowledge of color geometry in neural network design. These color equivariant architectures have modeled hue variation with 2D rotations, and saturation and luminance transformations as 1D translations. While this approach improves neural network robustness to color variations in a number of contexts, we find that approximating saturation and luminance (interval valued quantities) as 1D translations introduces appreciable artifacts. In this paper, we introduce a color equivariant architecture that is truly equivariant. Instead of approximating the interval with the real line, we lift values on the interval to values on the circle (a double-cover) and build equivariant representations there. Our approach resolves the approximation artifacts of previous methods, improves interpretability and generalizability, and achieves better predictive performance than conventional and equivariant baselines on tasks such as fine-grained classification and medical imaging tasks. Going beyond the context of color, we show that our proposed lifting can also extend to geometric transformations such as scale.

AI.tldr: They propose a truly color-equivariant neural architecture by lifting interval-valued channels (saturation, luminance) to a circular double-cover and building equivariant representations there, avoiding artifacts from modeling them as 1D translations, yielding improved robustness, interpretability, and accuracy; the idea also extends to scale.
AI.motivation: Conventional networks degrade under color distribution shifts. Prior color-equivariant models treated hue as 2D rotations and saturation/luminance as 1D translations, but these are interval-valued quantities, so the real-line approximation causes boundary artifacts and breaks exact equivariance.
AI.method: Lift interval-valued signals (e.g., saturation, luminance) to values on a circle (S1) via a double-cover, then construct group-equivariant representations/operations on this manifold; maintain hue with rotations. This yields true equivariance for color transformations and can be generalized to geometric transformations like scale via analogous lifting.
AI.result: The approach eliminates approximation artifacts observed in prior methods, improves interpretability and generalization, and outperforms both conventional and previous color-equivariant baselines on tasks including fine-grained visual classification and medical imaging. Specific metrics are not given in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Lifting interval quantities to circular domains enables truly equivariant architectures for color (and potentially scale), delivering better robustness and predictive performance while enhancing interpretability; it provides a principled alternative to ad hoc translation approximations.

title: ZipMap: Linear-Time Stateful 3D Reconstruction with Test-Time Training
summary: Feed-forward transformer models have driven rapid progress in 3D vision, but state-of-the-art methods such as VGGT and $π^3$ have a computational cost that scales quadratically with the number of input images, making them inefficient when applied to large image collections. Sequential-reconstruction approaches reduce this cost but sacrifice reconstruction quality. We introduce ZipMap, a stateful feed-forward model that achieves linear-time, bidirectional 3D reconstruction while matching or surpassing the accuracy of quadratic-time methods. ZipMap employs test-time training layers to zip an entire image collection into a compact hidden scene state in a single forward pass, enabling reconstruction of over 700 frames in under 10 seconds on a single H100 GPU, more than $20\times$ faster than state-of-the-art methods such as VGGT. Moreover, we demonstrate the benefits of having a stateful representation in real-time scene-state querying and its extension to sequential streaming reconstruction.

AI.tldr: ZipMap is a stateful, feed-forward 3D reconstruction model that compresses an entire image collection into a compact scene state in one pass, achieving linear-time, bidirectional reconstruction. It matches or exceeds the accuracy of quadratic-cost transformers (e.g., VGGT, π^3) while being >20× faster, handling 700+ frames in <10 s on a single H100, and supports real-time querying and streaming.
AI.motivation: Current feed-forward transformer methods for multi-view 3D reconstruction scale quadratically with the number of images, making them impractical for large collections. Sequential methods reduce cost but hurt reconstruction quality. There is a need for an approach that scales linearly without sacrificing accuracy and that supports real-time and streaming scenarios.
AI.method: Introduce a stateful feed-forward architecture with test-time training (TTT) layers that ‘zip’ all input images into a compact hidden scene state in a single forward pass. This enables linear-time processing and bidirectional 3D reconstruction. The state can be queried in real time and incrementally updated for streaming sequences.
AI.result: Reconstructs over 700 frames in under 10 seconds on a single H100 GPU, more than 20× faster than state-of-the-art quadratic-time methods like VGGT, while matching or surpassing their reconstruction accuracy. Demonstrates real-time scene-state querying and extension to sequential streaming reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: A practical, scalable alternative to quadratic-time transformers for large-scale 3D reconstruction. The stateful scene representation provides both efficiency and flexibility, enabling accurate, fast reconstruction, real-time queries, and streaming operation.

title: ViterbiPlanNet: Injecting Procedural Knowledge via Differentiable Viterbi for Planning in Instructional Videos
summary: Procedural planning aims to predict a sequence of actions that transforms an initial visual state into a desired goal, a fundamental ability for intelligent agents operating in complex environments. Existing approaches typically rely on large-scale models that learn procedural structures implicitly, resulting in limited sample-efficiency and high computational cost. In this work we introduce ViterbiPlanNet, a principled framework that explicitly integrates procedural knowledge into the learning process through a Differentiable Viterbi Layer (DVL). The DVL embeds a Procedural Knowledge Graph (PKG) directly with the Viterbi decoding algorithm, replacing non-differentiable operations with smooth relaxations that enable end-to-end optimization. This design allows the model to learn through graph-based decoding. Experiments on CrossTask, COIN, and NIV demonstrate that ViterbiPlanNet achieves state-of-the-art performance with an order of magnitude fewer parameters than diffusion- and LLM-based planners. Extensive ablations show that performance gains arise from our differentiable structure-aware training rather than post-hoc refinement, resulting in improved sample efficiency and robustness to shorter unseen horizons. We also address testing inconsistencies establishing a unified testing protocol with consistent splits and evaluation metrics. With this new protocol, we run experiments multiple times and report results using bootstrapping to assess statistical significance.

AI.tldr: ViterbiPlanNet integrates a Procedural Knowledge Graph into a differentiable Viterbi decoding layer, enabling end-to-end, structure-aware training for visual procedural planning and achieving state-of-the-art performance with far fewer parameters and improved sample efficiency and robustness.
AI.motivation: Procedural planning methods often rely on large models that learn structure implicitly, leading to poor sample efficiency, high compute cost, and fragile generalization. There is also inconsistency in evaluation protocols across datasets. The authors aim to explicitly encode procedural knowledge and provide a unified, statistically sound evaluation.
AI.method: They embed a Procedural Knowledge Graph (PKG) within a Differentiable Viterbi Layer (DVL) by relaxing non-differentiable operations (e.g., max/argmax) to smooth, trainable counterparts, enabling backpropagation through Viterbi decoding. This makes graph-constrained plan decoding part of the model’s computation graph, allowing end-to-end optimization that aligns representations with procedural constraints.
AI.result: On CrossTask, COIN, and NIV, ViterbiPlanNet attains state-of-the-art accuracy using roughly an order of magnitude fewer parameters than diffusion and LLM-based planners. Ablations indicate gains stem from the differentiable, structure-aware training rather than post-hoc decoding/refinement. The model is more sample-efficient and more robust on shorter, unseen horizons. They also standardize testing with consistent splits/metrics and use bootstrap resampling to report statistical significance.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, differentiable incorporation of procedural knowledge via Viterbi decoding yields a more efficient and robust planner than large implicit-structure models, while strengthening evaluation rigor with a unified, statistically grounded testing protocol.

title: SSR: A Generic Framework for Text-Aided Map Compression for Localization
summary: Mapping is crucial in robotics for localization and downstream decision-making. As robots are deployed in ever-broader settings, the maps they rely on continue to increase in size. However, storing these maps indefinitely (cold storage), transferring them across networks, or sending localization queries to cloud-hosted maps imposes prohibitive memory and bandwidth costs. We propose a text-enhanced compression framework that reduces both memory and bandwidth footprints while retaining high-fidelity localization. The key idea is to treat text as an alternative modality: one that can be losslessly compressed with large language models. We propose leveraging lightweight text descriptions combined with very small image feature vectors, which capture "complementary information" as a compact representation for the mapping task. Building on this, our novel technique, Similarity Space Replication (SSR), learns an adaptive image embedding in one shot that captures only the information "complementary" to the text descriptions. We validate our compression framework on multiple downstream localization tasks, including Visual Place Recognition as well as object-centric Monte Carlo localization in both indoor and outdoor settings. SSR achieves 2 times better compression than competing baselines on state-of-the-art datasets, including TokyoVal, Pittsburgh30k, Replica, and KITTI.

AI.tldr: They propose a text-enhanced map compression pipeline for robot localization that pairs lightweight textual scene descriptions—highly compressible by LLM-based codecs—with tiny image embeddings learned to only store information missing from text (via a new Similarity Space Replication, SSR). This achieves about 2× better compression than SOTA while maintaining high-fidelity localization across indoor/outdoor datasets (TokyoVal, Pittsburgh30k, Replica, KITTI).
AI.motivation: Robot maps are growing large, making cold storage, network transfer, and cloud-based localization queries costly in memory and bandwidth. There is a need for compression that preserves localization quality without prohibitive resource usage.
AI.method: Treat text as an alternative modality that can be (near-)losslessly compressed by language models. Represent scenes using: (1) lightweight text descriptions and (2) very small image feature vectors that encode only information complementary to the text. Introduce Similarity Space Replication (SSR) to learn, in one shot, adaptive image embeddings that replicate the similarity structure relevant for localization while excluding information already captured by text. Evaluate on Visual Place Recognition and object-centric Monte Carlo localization in varied environments.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks (TokyoVal, Pittsburgh30k, Replica, KITTI), the approach maintains high-fidelity localization and yields approximately 2× better compression versus competitive baselines.
AI.conclusion: Combining text with minimal complementary image features—via SSR—substantially reduces map memory and bandwidth demands without sacrificing localization, suggesting a practical path to scalable, cloud-friendly robotic mapping and localization systems.

title: A multi-center analysis of deep learning methods for video polyp detection and segmentation
summary: Colonic polyps are well-recognized precursors to colorectal cancer (CRC), typically detected during colonoscopy. However, the variability in appearance, location, and size of these polyps complicates their detection and removal, leading to challenges in effective surveillance, intervention, and subsequently CRC prevention. The processes of colonoscopy surveillance and polyp removal are highly reliant on the expertise of gastroenterologists and occur within the complexities of the colonic structure. As a result, there is a high rate of missed detections and incomplete removal of colonic polyps, which can adversely impact patient outcomes. Recently, automated methods that use machine learning have been developed to enhance polyps detection and segmentation, thus helping clinical processes and reducing missed rates. These advancements highlight the potential for improving diagnostic accuracy in real-time applications, which ultimately facilitates more effective patient management. Furthermore, integrating sequence data and temporal information could significantly enhance the precision of these methods by capturing the dynamic nature of polyp growth and the changes that occur over time. To rigorously investigate these challenges, data scientists and experts gastroenterologists collaborated to compile a comprehensive dataset that spans multiple centers and diverse populations. This initiative aims to underscore the critical importance of incorporating sequence data and temporal information in the development of robust automated detection and segmentation methods. This study evaluates the applicability of deep learning techniques developed in real-time clinical colonoscopy tasks using sequence data, highlighting the critical role of temporal relationships between frames in improving diagnostic precision.

AI.tldr: Deep learning methods that exploit temporal/sequence information in colonoscopy videos—validated on a new multi‑center dataset—can improve real‑time polyp detection and segmentation, reducing misses and aiding clinical decision‑making.
AI.motivation: Polyp appearance, location, and size vary widely, and colonoscopy outcomes depend heavily on operator skill within a complex anatomy. These factors lead to missed detections and incomplete resections, undermining CRC prevention. There is a need for robust, real‑time automation that can capture temporal dynamics rather than treating frames independently.
AI.method: A collaboration between data scientists and gastroenterologists assembled a comprehensive, multi‑center, diverse dataset of colonoscopy sequences. The study evaluates deep learning approaches for real‑time polyp detection and segmentation that explicitly model temporal relationships across frames (sequence/temporal modeling) instead of single‑frame analysis.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation indicates that leveraging frame‑to‑frame temporal information enhances diagnostic precision over frame‑independent methods, supporting applicability of deep learning to real‑time clinical colonoscopy. (No specific quantitative metrics reported in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: Incorporating sequence data and temporal modeling is critical for robust, real‑time automated polyp detection/segmentation. Multi‑center datasets and clinician–data scientist collaboration enable generalizable systems that can lower miss rates and improve patient management in CRC prevention.

title: Gaussian Wardrobe: Compositional 3D Gaussian Avatars for Free-Form Virtual Try-On
summary: We introduce Gaussian Wardrobe, a novel framework to digitalize compositional 3D neural avatars from multi-view videos. Existing methods for 3D neural avatars typically treat the human body and clothing as an inseparable entity. However, this paradigm fails to capture the dynamics of complex free-form garments and limits the reuse of clothing across different individuals. To overcome these problems, we develop a novel, compositional 3D Gaussian representation to build avatars from multiple layers of free-form garments. The core of our method is decomposing neural avatars into bodies and layers of shape-agnostic neural garments. To achieve this, our framework learns to disentangle each garment layer from multi-view videos and canonicalizes it into a shape-independent space. In experiments, our method models photorealistic avatars with high-fidelity dynamics, achieving new state-of-the-art performance on novel pose synthesis benchmarks. In addition, we demonstrate that the learned compositional garments contribute to a versatile digital wardrobe, enabling a practical virtual try-on application where clothing can be freely transferred to new subjects. Project page: https://ait.ethz.ch/gaussianwardrobe

AI.tldr: Gaussian Wardrobe introduces a compositional 3D Gaussian avatar that disentangles the human body from multiple free-form garment layers from multi-view videos, canonicalizes garments to a shape-agnostic space, achieves SOTA novel-pose rendering, and enables cross-subject clothing transfer for virtual try-on.
AI.motivation: Most 3D neural avatar methods bind body and clothing together, making it hard to model complex garment dynamics and preventing reusing clothes on different people.
AI.method: Use a compositional 3D Gaussian representation to decompose an avatar into a body and multiple layers of shape-agnostic neural garments. Learn to disentangle each garment from multi-view videos and canonicalize it into a subject-independent space for re-composition on new bodies.
AI.result: Produces photorealistic avatars with high-fidelity clothing dynamics, sets new state-of-the-art on novel pose synthesis benchmarks, and supports transferring learned garments to new subjects in a practical virtual try-on setup.
AI.conclusion: Layered, compositional Gaussian avatars enable a reusable digital wardrobe: garments are modeled independently of body shape, improving realism, control, and reusability across subjects for applications like virtual try-on.

title: Motion Manipulation via Unsupervised Keypoint Positioning in Face Animation
summary: Face animation deals with controlling and generating facial features with a wide range of applications. The methods based on unsupervised keypoint positioning can produce realistic and detailed virtual portraits. However, they cannot achieve controllable face generation since the existing keypoint decomposition pipelines fail to fully decouple identity semantics and intertwined motion information (e.g., rotation, translation, and expression). To address these issues, we present a new method, Motion Manipulation via unsupervised keypoint positioning in Face Animation (MMFA). We first introduce self-supervised representation learning to encode and decode expressions in the latent feature space and decouple them from other motion information. Secondly, we propose a new way to compute keypoints aiming to achieve arbitrary motion control. Moreover, we design a variational autoencoder to map expression features to a continuous Gaussian distribution, allowing us for the first time to interpolate facial expressions in an unsupervised framework. We have conducted extensive experiments on publicly available datasets to validate the effectiveness of MMFA, which show that MMFA offers pronounced advantages over prior arts in creating realistic animation and manipulating face motion.

AI.tldr: MMFA is an unsupervised keypoint-based face animation method that disentangles expression from other motion via self-supervision and a VAE, introduces controllable keypoint computation for arbitrary motion, and enables expression interpolation, achieving more realistic and controllable animations than prior work.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised keypoint methods create realistic portraits but lack controllability because identity, expression, rotation, and translation are entangled. Decoupling these factors is needed to explicitly control motion and interpolate expressions.
AI.method: (1) Self-supervised representation learning to encode/decode facial expressions in a latent space separate from pose/identity; (2) a new keypoint computation pipeline designed for arbitrary motion control; (3) a variational autoencoder that maps expression features to a continuous Gaussian distribution to enable smooth, unsupervised expression interpolation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on public datasets indicate MMFA surpasses prior approaches in producing realistic animations and allowing flexible manipulation of face motion, including arbitrary motion control and smooth expression interpolation.
AI.conclusion: By disentangling expression from other motion signals and redesigning keypoint computation, augmented with a VAE over expression features, MMFA delivers controllable, realistic face animation and, for the first time in an unsupervised setting, enables expression interpolation; experiments validate its advantages over prior art.

title: Dual Diffusion Models for Multi-modal Guided 3D Avatar Generation
summary: Generating high-fidelity 3D avatars from text or image prompts is highly sought after in virtual reality and human-computer interaction. However, existing text-driven methods often rely on iterative Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) or CLIP optimization, which struggle with fine-grained semantic control and suffer from excessively slow inference. Meanwhile, image-driven approaches are severely bottlenecked by the scarcity and high acquisition cost of high-quality 3D facial scans, limiting model generalization. To address these challenges, we first construct a novel, large-scale dataset comprising over 100,000 pairs across four modalities: fine-grained textual descriptions, in-the-wild face images, high-quality light-normalized texture UV maps, and 3D geometric shapes. Leveraging this comprehensive dataset, we propose PromptAvatar, a framework featuring dual diffusion models. Specifically, it integrates a Texture Diffusion Model (TDM) that supports flexible multi-condition guidance from text and/or image prompts, alongside a Geometry Diffusion Model (GDM) guided by text prompts. By learning the direct mapping from multi-modal prompts to 3D representations, PromptAvatar eliminates the need for time-consuming iterative optimization, successfully generating high-fidelity, shading-free 3D avatars in under 10 seconds. Extensive quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in generation quality, fine-grained detail alignment, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: PromptAvatar builds a 100k-pair multimodal dataset and trains two diffusion models (for texture and geometry) to map text and/or image prompts directly to shading-free 3D avatars in under 10 seconds, outperforming prior methods in fidelity, fine-grained control, and speed without iterative SDS/CLIP optimization.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity, controllable 3D avatar generation is valuable for VR and HCI, but current text-driven approaches rely on slow iterative optimization (SDS/CLIP) with weak fine-grained control, while image-driven methods suffer from limited, costly 3D scan data that hampers generalization.
AI.method: Construct a large-scale dataset (>100k pairs) spanning four modalities: detailed text descriptions, in-the-wild face images, light-normalized UV textures, and 3D geometry. Propose PromptAvatar with dual diffusion models: a Texture Diffusion Model (TDM) enabling flexible multi-conditional guidance from text and/or images, and a Geometry Diffusion Model (GDM) guided by text. Train them to directly map prompts to 3D representations, removing iterative optimization.
AI.result: Generates high-fidelity, shading-free 3D avatars in <10 seconds. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experiments show significant improvements over state-of-the-art in generation quality, fine-grained detail alignment, and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A data-rich, dual-diffusion pipeline enables fast, accurate, and controllable prompt-to-3D avatar synthesis, overcoming the speed, control, and data scarcity limitations of prior text- and image-driven methods.

title: Scalable Evaluation of the Realism of Synthetic Environmental Augmentations in Images
summary:
Evaluation of AI systems often requires synthetic test cases, particularly for rare or safety-critical conditions that are difficult to observe in operational data. Generative AI offers a promising approach for producing such data through controllable image editing, but its usefulness depends on whether the resulting images are sufficiently realistic to support meaningful evaluation.
  We present a scalable framework for assessing the realism of synthetic image-editing methods and apply it to the task of adding environmental conditions-fog, rain, snow, and nighttime-to car-mounted camera images. Using 40 clear-day images, we compare rule-based augmentation libraries with generative AI image-editing models. Realism is evaluated using two complementary automated metrics: a vision-language model (VLM) jury for perceptual realism assessment, and embedding-based distributional analysis to measure similarity to genuine adverse-condition imagery.
  Generative AI methods substantially outperform rule-based approaches, with the best generative method achieving approximately 3.6 times the acceptance rate of the best rule-based method. Performance varies across conditions: fog proves easiest to simulate, while nighttime transformations remain challenging. Notably, the VLM jury assigns imperfect acceptance even to real adverse-condition imagery, establishing practical ceilings against which synthetic methods can be judged. By this standard, leading generative methods match or exceed real-image performance for most conditions.
  These results suggest that modern generative image-editing models can enable scalable generation of realistic adverse-condition imagery for evaluation pipelines. Our framework therefore provides a practical approach for scalable realism evaluation, though validation against human studies remains an important direction for future work.

AI.tldr: They propose a scalable framework to judge the realism of synthetic adverse-condition edits (fog, rain, snow, night) to driving images using a VLM-based perceptual jury and embedding-based distributional similarity. Across 40 base images, generative editors far outperform rule-based augmenters; fog is easiest, night is hardest. A VLM-imposed realism ceiling shows even real adverse images aren’t always ‘accepted’; top generative methods match or exceed real-image scores for most conditions. Human validation remains needed.
AI.motivation: Rare and safety-critical conditions are underrepresented in operational data, so synthetic imagery is attractive for evaluating AI systems—if it is realistic enough. The work aims to rigorously and scalably assess whether modern generative image editing can produce sufficiently realistic adverse-condition data.
AI.method: Start with 40 clear-day, car-mounted camera images. Apply adverse-condition transformations via (a) rule-based augmentation libraries and (b) generative AI image-editing models. Evaluate realism with two automatic metrics: (1) a vision–language model (VLM) ‘jury’ for perceptual acceptance; (2) embedding-based distributional analysis measuring similarity to real adverse-condition datasets. Establish practical performance ceilings by scoring genuine adverse-condition images with the same VLM jury.
AI.result: Generative methods achieve ~3.6× the VLM acceptance rate of the best rule-based approach. Fog is easiest to simulate; night remains most challenging. The VLM jury gives imperfect acceptance to real adverse images, defining a ceiling. By that yardstick, leading generative methods meet or surpass real-image performance for most conditions.
AI.conclusion: Modern generative editing can scalably produce realistic adverse-condition imagery suitable for evaluation pipelines. The proposed framework is practical for automated realism assessment, but confirmation with human studies is an important next step.

title: ArtHOI: Articulated Human-Object Interaction Synthesis by 4D Reconstruction from Video Priors
summary: Synthesizing physically plausible articulated human-object interactions (HOI) without 3D/4D supervision remains a fundamental challenge. While recent zero-shot approaches leverage video diffusion models to synthesize human-object interactions, they are largely confined to rigid-object manipulation and lack explicit 4D geometric reasoning. To bridge this gap, we formulate articulated HOI synthesis as a 4D reconstruction problem from monocular video priors: given only a video generated by a diffusion model, we reconstruct a full 4D articulated scene without any 3D supervision. This reconstruction-based approach treats the generated 2D video as supervision for an inverse rendering problem, recovering geometrically consistent and physically plausible 4D scenes that naturally respect contact, articulation, and temporal coherence. We introduce ArtHOI, the first zero-shot framework for articulated human-object interaction synthesis via 4D reconstruction from video priors. Our key designs are: 1) Flow-based part segmentation: leveraging optical flow as a geometric cue to disentangle dynamic from static regions in monocular video; 2) Decoupled reconstruction pipeline: joint optimization of human motion and object articulation is unstable under monocular ambiguity, so we first recover object articulation, then synthesize human motion conditioned on the reconstructed object states. ArtHOI bridges video-based generation and geometry-aware reconstruction, producing interactions that are both semantically aligned and physically grounded. Across diverse articulated scenes (e.g., opening fridges, cabinets, microwaves), ArtHOI significantly outperforms prior methods in contact accuracy, penetration reduction, and articulation fidelity, extending zero-shot interaction synthesis beyond rigid manipulation through reconstruction-informed synthesis.

AI.tldr: ArtHOI reframes zero-shot human–object interaction synthesis as 4D articulated scene reconstruction from diffusion-generated monocular videos, enabling physically plausible, geometry-consistent interactions with articulated objects without 3D/4D supervision.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot video diffusion can depict HOI but lacks explicit 4D geometry and is largely limited to rigid-object manipulation, leading to implausible contact, penetrations, and poor articulation modeling. There is a need to synthesize interactions that are physically grounded and temporally coherent for articulated objects using only weak (video) priors.
AI.method: Treat generated 2D video as supervision for an inverse-rendering problem that reconstructs a full 4D articulated scene (human + object). Two key designs: (1) flow-based part segmentation to disentangle dynamic vs. static regions as a geometric cue; (2) a decoupled reconstruction pipeline that first recovers object articulation, then synthesizes human motion conditioned on the reconstructed object states, improving stability under monocular ambiguity.
AI.result: Across articulated scenarios (e.g., opening fridges, cabinets, microwaves), ArtHOI achieves better contact accuracy, reduces penetrations, and improves articulation fidelity relative to prior zero-shot/rigid-focused methods, while maintaining semantic alignment and temporal coherence.
AI.conclusion: By bridging video generation and geometry-aware 4D reconstruction, ArtHOI delivers semantically aligned, physically grounded HOI for articulated objects in a zero-shot setting, extending interaction synthesis beyond rigid manipulation and demonstrating the power of reconstruction-informed synthesis.

title: Balancing Fidelity, Utility, and Privacy in Synthetic Cardiac MRI Generation: A Comparative Study
summary: Deep learning in cardiac MRI (CMR) is fundamentally constrained by both data scarcity and privacy regulations. This study systematically benchmarks three generative architectures: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM), Latent Diffusion Models (LDM), and Flow Matching (FM) for synthetic CMR generation. Utilizing a two-stage pipeline where anatomical masks condition image synthesis, we evaluate generated data across three critical axes: fidelity, utility, and privacy. Our results show that diffusion-based models, particularly DDPM, provide the most effective balance between downstream segmentation utility, image fidelity, and privacy preservation under limited-data conditions, while FM demonstrates promising privacy characteristics with slightly lower task-level performance. These findings quantify the trade-offs between cross-domain generalization and patient confidentiality, establishing a framework for safe and effective synthetic data augmentation in medical imaging.

AI.tldr: Benchmark of DDPM, LDM, and Flow Matching for mask-conditioned synthetic cardiac MRI shows DDPM best balances image fidelity, downstream segmentation utility, and privacy under limited data; FM offers stronger privacy with modest utility drop. Provides a framework to quantify utility–privacy trade-offs for safe augmentation.
AI.motivation: CMR deep learning suffers from small datasets and strict privacy rules. Synthetic data could mitigate scarcity, but practitioners lack clear guidance on which generative models yield realistic, useful, and privacy-preserving images in this domain.
AI.method: Two-stage conditional synthesis: anatomical masks guide image generation. Systematic benchmarking of three generators—DDPM, LDM, and Flow Matching—under limited-data regimes. Evaluation spans three axes: fidelity (realism), utility (downstream segmentation and cross-domain generalization), and privacy (memorization/re-identification risk).
AI.result: Diffusion-based approaches, especially DDPM, achieve the best overall balance of fidelity and downstream segmentation performance while preserving privacy. Flow Matching demonstrates comparatively stronger privacy characteristics but with slightly reduced task-level performance. The study quantifies the trade-offs among fidelity, utility, and privacy.
AI.conclusion: For CMR augmentation in low-data settings, DDPM is the most effective general choice; FM is attractive when privacy is prioritized over peak task performance. The tri-axis evaluation framework enables principled selection and safer deployment of synthetic medical imaging data.

title: Hold-One-Shot-Out (HOSO) for Validation-Free Few-Shot CLIP Adapters
summary: In many CLIP adaptation methods, a blending ratio hyperparameter controls the trade-off between general pretrained CLIP knowledge and the limited, dataset-specific supervision from the few-shot cases. Most few-shot CLIP adaptation techniques report results by ablation of the blending ratio on the test set or require additional validation sets to select the blending ratio per dataset, and thus are not strictly few-shot. We present a simple, validation-free method for learning the blending ratio in CLIP adaptation. Hold-One-Shot-Out (HOSO) presents a novel approach for CLIP-Adapter-style methods to compete in the newly established validation-free setting. CLIP-Adapter with HOSO (HOSO-Adapter) learns the blending ratio using a one-shot, hold-out set, while the adapter trains on the remaining few-shot support examples. Under the validation-free few-shot protocol, HOSO-Adapter outperforms the CLIP-Adapter baseline by more than 4 percentage points on average across 11 standard few-shot datasets. Interestingly, in the 8- and 16-shot settings, HOSO-Adapter outperforms CLIP-Adapter even with the optimal blending ratio selected on the test set. Ablation studies validate the use of a one-shot hold-out mechanism, decoupled training, and improvements over the naively learnt blending ratio baseline. Code is released here: https://github.com/chris-vorster/HOSO-Adapter

AI.tldr: HOSO-Adapter learns the CLIP blending ratio without a validation set by holding out one shot per class to tune the ratio while training the adapter on the remaining shots, yielding >4-point average gains over CLIP-Adapter across 11 datasets and even surpassing test-set–tuned CLIP-Adapter in 8- and 16-shot settings.
AI.motivation: Few-shot CLIP adaptation requires choosing a blending ratio between frozen CLIP knowledge and task-specific supervision. Existing methods often sweep this ratio on the test set or rely on extra validation data, violating strict few-shot assumptions and hindering fair, practical deployment.
AI.method: Hold-One-Shot-Out (HOSO) for CLIP-Adapter-style methods: from the few-shot support set, hold out one labeled example per class to learn the blending ratio; train the adapter on the remaining shots. Training of the adapter and the ratio is decoupled via this one-shot hold-out mechanism. The approach is validation-free and improves upon naively learning the ratio directly on the training shots.
AI.result: Under a validation-free few-shot protocol on 11 standard datasets, HOSO-Adapter improves average accuracy by over 4 percentage points versus CLIP-Adapter. In 8- and 16-shot regimes, it even outperforms CLIP-Adapter with an oracle (test-set–tuned) blending ratio. Ablations support the benefits of the one-shot hold-out, the decoupled training scheme, and superiority over a naive learned-ratio baseline.
AI.conclusion: A simple, practical, validation-free strategy for setting the CLIP blending ratio delivers consistent gains and can outperform even oracle-tuned baselines at moderate shot counts. This strengthens the case for strict few-shot evaluation and offers an easily adoptable improvement; code is available for reproduction.

title: Enhancing Authorship Attribution with Synthetic Paintings
comment: Accepted for publication at the 24th IEEE International Conference on Machine Learning and Applications (ICMLA 2025)
summary: Attributing authorship to paintings is a historically complex task, and one of its main challenges is the limited availability of real artworks for training computational models. This study investigates whether synthetic images, generated through DreamBooth fine-tuning of Stable Diffusion, can improve the performance of classification models in this context. We propose a hybrid approach that combines real and synthetic data to enhance model accuracy and generalization across similar artistic styles. Experimental results show that adding synthetic images leads to higher ROC-AUC and accuracy compared to using only real paintings. By integrating generative and discriminative methods, this work contributes to the development of computer vision techniques for artwork authentication in data-scarce scenarios.

AI.tldr: Synthetic, DreamBooth‑tuned Stable Diffusion images used to augment scarce real paintings improve authorship classification (higher ROC‑AUC and accuracy) versus real‑only training.
AI.motivation: Authorship attribution in art suffers from scarce labeled paintings per artist and overlapping styles, limiting supervised models’ performance and generalization.
AI.method: Generate artist‑specific synthetic paintings via DreamBooth fine‑tuning of Stable Diffusion and train discriminative classifiers on a hybrid dataset mixing real and synthetic images; evaluate on authorship classification across similar styles with ROC‑AUC and accuracy.
AI.result: Hybrid training with synthetic images outperforms real‑only baselines in ROC‑AUC and accuracy, indicating better generalization across stylistically similar works.
AI.conclusion: Combining generative augmentation (DreamBooth SD) with discriminative classifiers is a viable way to boost artwork-authorship models in data‑scarce settings, aiding authentication workflows.

title: Underrepresented in Foundation Model Pretraining Data? A One-Shot Probe
summary: Large-scale Vision-Language Foundation Models (VLFMs), such as CLIP, now underpin a wide range of computer vision research and applications. VLFMs are often adapted to various domain-specific tasks. However, VLFM performance on novel, specialised, or underrepresented domains remains inconsistent. Evaluating VLFMs typically requires labelled test sets, which are often unavailable for niche domains of interest, particularly those from the Global South. We address this gap by proposing a highly data-efficient method to predict a VLFM's zero-shot accuracy on a target domain using only a single labelled image per class. Our approach uses a Large Language Model to generate plausible counterfactual descriptions of a given image. By measuring the VLFM's ability to distinguish the correct description from these hard negatives, we engineer features that capture the VLFM's discriminative power in its shared embedding space. A linear regressor trained on these similarity scores estimates the VLFM's zero-shot test accuracy across various visual domains with a Pearson-r correlation of 0.96. We demonstrate our method's performance across five diverse datasets, including standard benchmark datasets and underrepresented datasets from Africa. Our work provides a low-cost, reliable tool for probing VLFMs, enabling researchers and practitioners to make informed decisions about data annotation efforts before committing significant resources. The model training code, generated captions and counterfactuals are released here: https://github.com/chris-vorster/PreLabellingProbe.

AI.tldr: A data-efficient probe predicts a vision-language foundation model’s zero-shot accuracy in a target domain using only one labeled image per class by contrasting image-text similarities against LLM-generated counterfactual captions, achieving high correlation (r=0.96) with true accuracy across five datasets, including underrepresented African domains.
AI.motivation: VLFMs (e.g., CLIP) perform inconsistently on novel or underrepresented domains, and labeled test sets are often unavailable—especially in Global South contexts—making it hard to know whether to invest in annotation. A cheap, reliable way to forecast zero-shot performance would inform resource allocation.
AI.method: For each class, use one labeled image and have an LLM generate plausible counterfactual (hard negative) descriptions. Compute the VLFM’s similarity between the image and the true vs. counterfactual texts in the shared embedding space, derive discriminative features, and train a simple linear regressor on these features to predict domain-level zero-shot accuracy.
AI.result: The probe estimates zero-shot accuracy with Pearson r ≈ 0.96 across five diverse datasets (standard benchmarks and African datasets). Artifacts (code, captions, counterfactuals) are released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: LLM-generated counterfactual probing is a low-cost, reliable tool to anticipate VLFM transferability and guide annotation decisions in niche and underrepresented domains; it enables pre-labeling assessment without extensive labeled test sets.

title: FocusGraph: Graph-Structured Frame Selection for Embodied Long Video Question Answering
summary:
The ability to understand long videos is vital for embodied intelligent agents, because their effectiveness depends on how well they can accumulate, organize, and leverage long-horizon perceptual memories. Recently, multimodal LLMs have been gaining popularity for solving the long video understanding task due to their general ability to understand natural language and to leverage world knowledge. However, as the number of frames provided to an MLLM increases, the quality of its responses tends to degrade, and inference time grows. Therefore, when using MLLMs for long video understanding, a crucial step is selecting key frames from the video to answer user queries.
  In this work, we develop FocusGraph, a framework for keyframe selection for question answering over long egocentric videos. It leverages a lightweight trainable Scene-Caption LLM Selector that selects query-relevant clips based on their graph-based captions, and a training-free method for selecting keyframes from these clips. Unlike existing methods, the proposed Scene-Caption LLM Selector does not rely on the original sequence of low-resolution frames; instead, it operates on a compact textual representation of the scene. We then design a training-free Patch-wise Sparse-Flow Retention (PSFR) method to select keyframes from the resulting sequence of clips, which are fed into an MLLM to produce the final answer. Together, these components enable FocusGraph to achieve state-of-the-art results on challenging egocentric long-video question answering benchmarks, including FindingDory and HourVideo, while significantly reducing inference time relative to baseline approaches.

AI.tldr: FocusGraph selects query-relevant clips via a Scene-Caption LLM Selector operating on graph-based textual scene summaries, then applies a training-free Patch-wise Sparse-Flow Retention method to pick keyframes, enabling MLLMs to answer long egocentric video QA with state-of-the-art accuracy and faster inference.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon video understanding is essential for embodied agents, but MLLM performance degrades and latency grows as more frames are processed. Efficiently selecting only query-relevant visual evidence is needed to maintain accuracy and reduce computational cost.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) a lightweight, trainable Scene-Caption LLM Selector ranks and selects clips using compact, graph-based captions instead of raw low-res frame sequences; (2) a training-free Patch-wise Sparse-Flow Retention (PSFR) procedure chooses keyframes within those clips; the retained frames are fed to an MLLM to produce answers.
AI.result: FocusGraph attains state-of-the-art results on long egocentric video QA benchmarks (FindingDory and HourVideo) while markedly cutting inference time versus baselines.
AI.conclusion: Operating on textual scene graphs for clip selection plus motion-aware, training-free keyframe pruning effectively focuses MLLMs on salient evidence, improving both accuracy and efficiency for long video question answering.

title: Helios: Real Real-Time Long Video Generation Model
summary: We introduce Helios, the first 14B video generation model that runs at 19.5 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU and supports minute-scale generation while matching the quality of a strong baseline. We make breakthroughs along three key dimensions: (1) robustness to long-video drifting without commonly used anti-drifting heuristics such as self-forcing, error-banks, or keyframe sampling; (2) real-time generation without standard acceleration techniques such as KV-cache, sparse/linear attention, or quantization; and (3) training without parallelism or sharding frameworks, enabling image-diffusion-scale batch sizes while fitting up to four 14B models within 80 GB of GPU memory. Specifically, Helios is a 14B autoregressive diffusion model with a unified input representation that natively supports T2V, I2V, and V2V tasks. To mitigate drifting in long-video generation, we characterize typical failure modes and propose simple yet effective training strategies that explicitly simulate drifting during training, while eliminating repetitive motion at its source. For efficiency, we heavily compress the historical and noisy context and reduce the number of sampling steps, yielding computational costs comparable to -- or lower than -- those of 1.3B video generative models. Moreover, we introduce infrastructure-level optimizations that accelerate both inference and training while reducing memory consumption. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Helios consistently outperforms prior methods on both short- and long-video generation. We plan to release the code, base model, and distilled model to support further development by the community.

AI.tldr: Helios is a 14B autoregressive diffusion video generator that achieves 19.5 FPS on a single H100, supports minute-scale outputs, and claims state-of-the-art quality—without relying on common anti-drift heuristics or standard acceleration tricks—via unified modeling, context compression, and infrastructure optimizations.
AI.motivation: Long video generation often drifts over time, requires complex heuristics, and struggles to run in real time without specialized acceleration or large-scale distributed training. The field also lacks a single model that natively handles T2V, I2V, and V2V efficiently. The authors aim to deliver robust long-horizon video generation that is fast, simple to train, and widely usable.
AI.method: A 14B autoregressive diffusion model with a unified input representation to natively support text-to-video, image-to-video, and video-to-video. They analyze drift failure modes and train by explicitly simulating drift and removing repetitive motion sources. For efficiency they heavily compress historical/noisy context and cut sampling steps, and add infrastructure-level memory/throughput optimizations. Notably, they avoid KV-cache, sparse/linear attention, quantization, and do not rely on parallelism/sharding, yet claim to fit up to four 14B models in 80 GB.
AI.result: On a single NVIDIA H100, Helios reportedly runs at 19.5 FPS and generates minute-scale videos while matching or exceeding a strong baseline in quality. Computational cost is comparable to or below that of 1.3B models. Experiments show consistent outperformance of prior methods on both short- and long-video benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Helios advances robust, real-time, long-horizon video generation with a simpler training/inference stack and a unified task interface. If validated, it sets a new efficiency/quality bar and may broaden access; the authors plan to release code, the base model, and a distilled model.

title: SimpliHuMoN: Simplifying Human Motion Prediction
summary: Human motion prediction combines the tasks of trajectory forecasting and human pose prediction. For each of the two tasks, specialized models have been developed. Combining these models for holistic human motion prediction is non-trivial, and recent methods have struggled to compete on established benchmarks for individual tasks. To address this, we propose a simple yet effective transformer-based model for human motion prediction. The model employs a stack of self-attention modules to effectively capture both spatial dependencies within a pose and temporal relationships across a motion sequence. This simple, streamlined, end-to-end model is sufficiently versatile to handle pose-only, trajectory-only, and combined prediction tasks without task-specific modifications. We demonstrate that this approach achieves state-of-the-art results across all tasks through extensive experiments on a wide range of benchmark datasets, including Human3.6M, AMASS, ETH-UCY, and 3DPW.

AI.tldr: A single, simple transformer with stacked self‑attention jointly models spatial pose structure and temporal motion, unifying pose, trajectory, and combined human motion prediction and reaching state of the art on Human3.6M, AMASS, ETH‑UCY, and 3DPW.
AI.motivation: Holistic human motion prediction requires handling both body pose dynamics and global trajectory. Prior work optimizes for one subtask at a time, and integrated approaches have not matched specialist models on standard benchmarks. A unified, end‑to‑end solution that performs competitively across all subtasks would simplify deployment and training while improving performance.
AI.method: An end‑to‑end transformer architecture composed of stacked self‑attention blocks models (1) spatial dependencies among joints within each pose and (2) temporal relationships across frames. The design is task‑agnostic, requiring no head or loss modifications to switch among pose‑only, trajectory‑only, or combined predictions.
AI.result: Across diverse datasets—Human3.6M and AMASS (3D pose/motion), ETH‑UCY (trajectory/social navigation), and 3DPW (in‑the‑wild 3D)—the approach achieves state‑of‑the‑art results on pose‑only, trajectory‑only, and combined prediction benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A streamlined transformer with self‑attention suffices to unify and excel at multiple human motion prediction tasks. The results suggest that shared attention over spatial and temporal tokens can replace task‑specific architectures, offering a strong, general baseline for holistic motion forecasting.
